Notes: Model of EVH 5153 Stealth’s Blue channel from firmware 23.04 for Axe-Fx III, without some of the later (more authentic) modeling improvements. Has sound of its own

amps.name: FAS WRECK
body: Model: Custom, OG WRECKER 1 model
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman GB
Power tubes: EL34
Notes: “Original WRECKER 1 from Axe-Fx Ultra”
“FAS Wreck better than any Trainwreck/Wreck-style amp IMO. Good bits without bad stuff”
“Real amp is a nasty thing, very spitty as expected, given lack of grid stopper resistor between last triode stage & PI”
“Got Trainwreck Express out, compared to model & it’s spot- on. Nasty, spitty, Neil Young-esque blocking distortion & all”

“Express (at least mine) has 3-position switch: Off, 100pF, 500pF. Amp block only supports Off & On, figured people wouldn’t know fine details, separate model seemed easiest solution. Can vary Bright Cap, but you’d have to know real amp has 2 caps, etc”
Tips: “For all hoopla surrounding Trainwrecks, actually pretty bad designs. Tons of blocking distortion in PI. Don’t want all the blocking distortion turn down PI Bias Excursion”
“Turned down PI Bias Excursion yields ultimate Trainwreck, better sounding IMO, set Preamp Bias so it’s less off-center”

“Amp exhibits lot of blocking distortion (in phase inverter) due to design. Reduce via PI Bias/Master Bias Excursion parameters. Alt: FAS Express model, puts resistors between last triode stage/PI, reduces bias excursion”

amps.name: FOX ODS
body: Model: Fuchs Overdrive Supreme 50, OD chnl
Orig. Cab: Fuchs 2x12 (Eminence)
DynaCab: 4x12 Rumble EV12S
Controls: Gain, Input, Brite sw, Deep sw, Master, Accent (Presence)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: D-style amp, so it’s similar to Dumble
“Supposedly Dumble ODS clone but ref amp has way more gain than our Dumble”

“Topology of Fuchs is pure Dumble, difference is there’s less padding between stages 2 & 3 (IIRC), it’s a high-gain Dumble. Personally don’t think Dumble topology lends itself to that much gain, but I didn’t design amp”
Dumble-type amp with loads of gain
Artists playing Fuchs amps: Al Di Meola, Joe Bonamassa & Dweezil Zappa

“Excels at everything it does, crystal clear/detailed clean sounds to harmonically complex & articulate OD, holds sonic integrity at any volume. Larry Carlton or Robben Ford live, that’s the tone! Nails Dumble sound to a T & then some!” - 20th Century Guitar
OD channel has separate OD in/out controls. OD in sets saturation level. OD out sets final balance of clean/dirty channels. MV, master reverb & accent adds presence/edge to output stage

Entire signal path’s fully tube (except reverb). ODS has regulated high voltage DC supply for low noise, consistent tone despite line voltage variations & regulated DC preamp tube heaters for additional noise reduction. Techniques used in finest audiophile & tube studio equipment, unique to guitar amps.” – Fuchs
Has same channels & controls as Dumble ODS
Fractal models based on OD channel. Of all D-style amp models in Fractal devices, these models provide most gain

Modeled with Preamp Bypass (PAB) on (boosts mids/adds gain) g2 gain controls for OD channel (Input Drive & Overdrive). On all amps like this, including Engl, Fryette, Carol-Ann & MESA, 1st control sets sensitivity & 2nd sets amount of distortion. Models with Input Drive & Overdrive controls feel elastic/spongy
Master Volume amp, distortion created by preamp tubes, not power amp. MV works in power amp section, important to tone/ feel. Decide setting that work. Default’s good start point
Fuchs uses Eminence speakers, not available in stock cabs
Tips: Has lots of gain, keep Input Drive low
Accent (reduces negative feedback at higher freqs)
Brite switch adds sparkle
Jazz-setting of Rock/Jazz switch applies LP filtering
Like base Dumble sound but want more gain w/o Fuchs fuzziness? Try Carol-Ann models
“Model has so much gain, I turn down Input Drive ~3.0” - yek

amps.name: FOX ODS DEEP
body: Model: Fuchs OD Supreme 50, deep switch on
Notes: Deep switch shifts overall tonality of amp, slightly increasing low freqs, often useful for single-coil guitars, which benefit from low-freq boost
Modeled with Preamp Bypass (PAB) on (boosts mids/adds gain)

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE 2010
body: Model: 2010 Friedman BE-100 (probably Marsha edition)
Orig. Cab: Friedman 4x12 (V30 &/or G12M)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman V30
Controls: Vol I & II, Master, Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (JJ)
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “BE/HBE has fixed depth circuit, gives lot of bass boost, model defaults Depth to match this”
Fixed Depth, defaults to value corresponds to fixed circuit
“BE has very light power supply filtering, like old Plexis”
“All Friedman models now based on latest amp. Our ref amp was made in 2018 & has feedback off 4-ohm tap”

“BE100 design has changed over the years. Dave’s constantly fiddling with things. Original “Marsha” (which became BE100 after cease-and-desist) had Gain control after 2nd stage. Later versions have gain control after 3rd stage, so even if you turn gain down, may still get some distortion. Also numerous changes to component values, feedback tap, etc. Our models are authentic recreations of 2 particular versions we have. Whether versions match your particular version is unknown.”
C45 switch is treble boost on input
“Added Negative Feedback control to UI for Friedman BE/ HBE models since it’s now a control on actual amp” g3 Voicings: V1,V2,V3 corresponds to 3 Voice switch positions

“Friedman model story: BE/HBE have gain pot before last gain stage, poor design choice IMO, always getting distortion, even turning gain down. Bad gain-staging IOW. Before Cygnus, I was more about “make it sound good” vs accuracy, hence, non-authentic pot tapers, etc. I moved gain pot forward a stage, as it sounded better to me. I left treble peaker before last stage & moved gain pot forward, more like classic Marshall. But as products became popular, cries of “doesn’t sound exactly like my amp” were more frequent, Cygnus became about authenticity. Some remnants of Ares left (pot tapers) that’ve been corrected over time as discovered, now models behave like amps, tweaks making amps “sound better” removed, for better or worse”-Cliff

“BE tone stack almost identical to Marshall. Reason BE’s smoother is there’s several caps sprinkled about that roll off highs. Snubber cap’s 2x as big as a Marshall & rolls off highs in power amp more. Feedback network has fixed Depth cap, boosts lows. Also strange that feedback is taken off speaker jack rather than tap on transformer. So if using a 16ohm cab, it sounds different vs 8ohm load (even more bassy)”

(firmware 32) “Fixed wrong B+ voltage in Friedman BE/HBE models (wrong dropping resistor value). Models remastered accordingly. Default Negative Feedback changed to being commensurate with being taken from 4-ohm tap. Note: 1st gen BE-100 had feedback off speaker jack. Was then changed to 4-ohm tap. Latest version has 3-way switch to pick from 4/8/16ohm taps, audition/adjust as needed”

When I was ready to hear what sorta gain this monster could muster, Sat (saturation boost), helped me do so in spades. I immediately understood why Steve Stevens & Jerry Cantrell are using BE in their rigs. Pure, raw aggression lunging from amp was staggering. Low notes were super tight & punchy, highs carried a really nice sting. As far as midrange voicing, hard- pressed to best Brown Eye’s OD.

With BE, Friedman packed decades of circuit design & mod know-how into a head that represents finest Marshall-inspired tones he can muster. Players who gravitate toward amps with strong upper-midrange spike & immediate attack should really take a look at BE. If you love pure aggression of late ’60s plexi, yet demand modern features like channel switching, extremely hard to beat” -PremierGuitar
Voice switch changes tone slightly. Switched right, amp sounds slightly brighter & bigger bass. Left, amp’s slightly darker with more mids
Tips: Friedmans sound great at low-to-high MV
Non V1/V2/V3 models based on original Marsha amp, rather dark & very bassy. Reduce Depth/ Negative Feedback to handle this or EQ

“Not a lot of tweaking required, models sound pretty awesome at default settings. I prefer V2 models, as I like mids. Like Dirty Shirley model, range of gain control (Input Drive) enables covering everything from mild crunch tone to very high gain. Can use a Scene Controller to vary gain across scenes. Don’t forget SAT switch. BE won’t let you clean up gain entirely, need to use Dirty Shirley model for that. Dirty Shirley is more vintage, BE sounds more modern.” - yek
Replicate C45: Filter block before amp, Tilt EQ, Freq:700Hz, Gain:4.5dB
SAT switch designed for BE/C45 mode, add gain/comp/saturation. Don’t overlook, it’s a treat! Also works well: engage Boost

“Recommend starting amp on BE channel with Bass 10, Mids ~6, Treble 5, Presence 5. Set Gain ~8, bring master up to taste. After plugging in, you’ll notice instantly, tight bottom end & rich harmonically detailed chords/single notes, the Friedman sound. Amp cleans up remarkably well with guitar’s volume control even with amp on higher gain settings. For even more gain switch to HBE mode” – Manual

“Design’s weird as negative feedback is taken off speaker jack. So if using 16ohm speaker, get more feedback (& much more bass) vs with 8-ohm speaker. Model based on using matching cab. Too much bass for me, I turn down Depth & reduce Negative Feedback a bit”
BE/HBE pair well with lotta speakers, including G12M, G12H, V30. Can combine speakers. Wanting Mark Day’s custom cab sound with Friedman BE, use stock cabs Fractal 4x12 (V30s+greenbacks)
“Newer BEs have fdbk off 4ohm tap (Negative Feedback ~3.87)”

“Want more clarity in low mids from BE/HBE? Reduce Negative Feedback. Friedman’s designs are bit odd, he takes feedback off speaker jack vs off transformer tap. Mating cab for BE/HBE is 16ohms, so lots of feedback. But connecting amp to 8ohm load, there’s ~30% less feedback, amp sounds tighter/clearer in low mids”

With all versatility/power offered, Presence control really sets it apart. Doesn’t just boost highs/add shimmer, it adds girth, dimension, gain & perceptible volume. Taming BE’s high-gain settings only required lowering Presence, softens high-end response & eased off screaming gain a bit, as if you have a retractable muzzle, with higher settings pulling back mask to let razor-sharp mids & highs bite through

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE C45
body: Model: later BE-100, C45 switch engaged
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location:
Notes: See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info
C45 is Friedman’s Custom 45 response mod. Flipping switch smoothed out tone bit more, adds bit of chime & openness to highs

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE V1
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: left
Notes: Voice switch left: slightly darker, more mids
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE V1 FAT
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: left, FAT switch on
Notes: 3 Voicings are 3 positions of Voice switch, changes tone slightly. Switched left, amp’s slightly darker, more mids
“Fat switch thickens up low end, helps fill out rhythm parts. I like it, as it didn’t increase gain. Fat switch on livens up Tele tone without added grit to spanky sound, helps Tele project better”
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE V2
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: middle (brightest)
Notes: 3 Voicings are 3 positions of Voice switch
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN BE V3
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: right
Notes: Voice switch right: amp sounds slightly brighter, bigger bass
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE 2010
body: Model: ‘10 Friedman BE-100	, HBE mode, May be Marsha edition
Notes: HBE mode has even more gain
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE C45
body: Model: later BE-100, C45 switch on, HBE mode
Notes: HBE mode has even more gain
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info
C45 is Friedman’s Custom 45 response mod. Flipping switch smoothed out tone bit more, adds bit of chime & openness to highs

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE V1
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: left, HBE mode
Notes: HBE mode has even more gain

(Reply to “weird” sounding HBE modes) “That’s the way HBE channels sound. Problem with design is drive control located between 3rd/4th gain stages, too far back IMO, causes lot of distortion & bias excursion before drive control & no way to control it. In most amps, drive control’s located after 1st stage (input buffer). Some amps have 2nd drive control, often called OD, further downstream, allows balancing pre/ post distortion. Way to deal with it: Decrease Input Trim & increase Input Drive. Can also try reducing Master Bias Excursion, but won’t get rid of pre-drive control distortion. Lot of people like this sound though, has certain stringy quality many people find desirable”
Voice switch left: slightly darker, more mids
See 153 Friedman BE 2010 for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE V1 FAT
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: left, FAT on, HBE
Notes: HBE mode has even more gain g3 Voicings are 3 positions of Voice switch, changes tone slightly. Switched left, amp’s slightly darker, more mids

“Fat switch thickens up low end, helps fill out rhythm parts. I like this as it didn’t increase gain. Engaged Fat switch livens up Tele tone without added grit to spanky sound, helps Tele project better”
See 153 & 161 Friedman BE/HBE for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE V2
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: middle (brightest), HBE mode
Notes: HBE mode has even more gain g3 Voicings are 3 positions of Voice switch
See 153 & 161 Friedman BE/HBE for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN HBE V3
body: Model: later BE-100, voice: right, HBE mode
Notes: Voice switch right: slightly brighter, bigger bass
See 153 & 161 Friedman BE/HBE for more info

amps.name: FRIEDMAN SMALL BOX
body: Model: Friedman Smallbox, Channel 2 (high gain)
Orig. Cab: Friedman 3x12 (V30 &/or G12M)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman GB
Controls: Gain, Master, Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence, Bright switch (channel 1 “Plexi” only)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (JJ)
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Similar to BE-100 but “grittier”
Friedman amps are medium-to-high gain amps delivering really fat tones
Often labeled as BE-100 in smaller enclosure, but not full story, has its own identity
Channel 2 has slightly different voicing on Brown Eye sound, more vintage/pronounced mids, has modern high gain tone Friedman’s known for
“Dave Friedman’s Smallbox, 50w head was designed for guitarists wanting classic tones reminiscent of great vintage plexis & Friedman’s modern high gain offerings
Smallbox was designed to take pedals, loves boosts, OD’s, phasers, flangers, tremolos & wahs

Tips: “Recommend starting with Bass 8, mids 6-7, treble 5. Gain ~8, master to taste. Notice tight low end & rich harmonic detailed chords/single notes, Friedman sound. Amp cleans up with guitar volume control, even higher amp gain”

“Schematic shows 2.2nF coupling cap off 1st stage but actual amp has 4.7nF, 2.2 nF would mean less bass. I can only assume amp was built to latest specs. If amp model is “G3’d” it’s deadly accurate. I go through amps & check values of each component, measure voltages, compare freq response to model, etc. If model’s too bassy, then you’d find real amp too bassy” - Cliff
Very bassy in sub lows. Remedy: decrease Negative Feedback/Depth, or increase Low Cut

Master Volume amp, distortion created by preamp tubes, not power amp. MV works in power amp section still, important to tone/ feel. Friedman amps sound great at low-to-high Master Volume. Default setting is a good start
Smallbox has G12M Creamback speaker. For Mark Day’s cab with Friedman BE, use stock cabs Fractal 4x12 (V30s+greenbacks)
“For more clarity in low mids, reduce Negative Feedback. Friedman design’s a bit odd, takes fdbk off speaker jack rather than off transformer tap. Low mids will sound tighter/clearer”
“Using 16 ohm speaker has more feedback & bass vs 8 ohm speaker. Too much bass for me, so I turn down Depth/reduce Negative Feedback”

amps.name: FRYETTE D60 LESS
body: Model: Fryette Deliverance 60 Series II, less mode (3 gain stages)
Orig. Cab: Fryette 4x12 P50E (Fane F70G)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman V30
Controls: Gain 1 & 2, Master, Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence, Depth
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A
Power tubes: KT88
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Popular with metal players

amps.name: FRYETTE D60 MORE
body: Model: Fryette Deliverance 60 II, more mode (4 gain stages)
Notes: More gain with this mode, see 166 Fryette for more info

amps.name: GIBTONE SCOUT
body: Model: Gibson GA17RVT Scout
Orig. Cab: Gibson 1x10
DynaCab: 1x10 BF Princetone
Controls: Volume (Drive), Tremolo
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6AQ5 (EL84)
Tonestack location: PRE
Tips: Scout’s only control is Volume. For authenticity, stick to Input Drive & leave everything else at default
For evolved version of Scout, try Nuclear-Tone model, based on Swart Atomic Space Tone. Inspired by Scout, great bias tremolo too
Original speaker is Gibson 10” (no factory cab), pick 1x10 stock cabs

amps.name: HERBIE CH2+
body: Model: Diezel Herbert, Channel 2+ (based on Marshall JC800)
Orig. Cab: Diezel 4x12 (V30, G12-K100)
DynaCab: 4x12 Recto Straight
Controls: Bass, Mid(1k), Treble, MV, Presence(>3k), Deep(120Hz)
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: KT66 or KT77
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “Humans perceive high freq thermal noise as hiss. More gain, more hiss. Diezel’s have lotta filtering, large grid stoppers, plate capacitors, etc, which reduces bandwidth & lowers hiss

“Main differences vs VH4 is voicing, circuit design & general layout. Overall, Herbert has more familiar, slightly looser feel, without losing much of Diezel signature definition/tightness, a player’s amp, forgiving, ready for whatever one’s soul/mood desires” - Diezel
(+) Mode: similar voice to (-), but much more gain. Kicks everything up a few notches & offers crunch with good bite & ample pick sensitivity, for punchy/heavy rhythm & searing solo tones”
“Channel 2 offers whole spectrum of crunch sounds (or even lead) you can think of, covers soft/heavy OD & distortion sounds
Tips: “Diezels compress a lot due to cathode follower design. Reduce by lowering Compression in Cathode Follower section”
Use IRs of V30 or G12-K100 speakers in 4x12 cab
Decrease bass by lowering Depth
Get typical Diezel trademark crunch (gain ~40%), tight, punchy, fast attack, a dream for harder stuff. Rotate gain to 60% & play leads”

“Tone controls work in unusually wide range, little adjustment goes a long way, less is often more. Suggest start exploring channels with controls set to 12 o’clock & MV just slightly cracked open”- Manual
Mid Cut switch not modeled. Replicate: Peak EQ at 400Hz, Q 1.4. Decrease Gain until desired scoop. Play with Q to set scoop width”

amps.name: HERBIE CH2-
body: Model: Diezel Herbert, Channel 2- (based on Marshall Plexi)
Notes: (-) Mode: dynamic range from vintage clean to broad crunch. Ample pick sensitivity. Blues/classic rock sounds easily obtained
See 169 Herbie for more details
Tips: Gain ~35% you get typical cranked Plexi sounds. Gain a bit higher, ~60% (all other pots noon) voilà: 800s territory

amps.name: HERBIE CH3
body: Model: Diezel Herbert, Channel 3 (based on Diezel VH4)
Notes: “Channel 3 offers high gain structure, but with very good pick sensitivity & easy control. Distortion structure is transparent, yet percussive & brutal”

“Gain ~30%, for metal rhythm channel, use mid cut for Recto territory. Mid cut disengaged, gain at 50% & mids bit cranked for screaming or creamy lead, depends on style/axe/fingers. Crank mids bit above noon for some bite. Use for both rhythm & lead, find ideal setting & engage 2nd master to get personal volume boost”

“Channel’s voiced for highly articulate single note lines, or very heavy/massive rhythm guitar. Due to slight midrange accent & very high gain structure, possesses good punch & will, with ease & authority, rule any stage/studio. Less is often more applies here”
See 169 Herbie for more details

amps.name: HERBIE MK3
body: Model: Diezel Herbert MKIII, Channel 2+
Notes: “I like MkIII more vs old version. Original’s too dark, fuzzy, compressed & muddy IMO. Sounds great playing alone, but playing along with recording, gets lost. Tighter. Sits in mix better”
See 169 Herbie for more details

amps.name: HIPOWER BRILLIANT
body: Model: 1974 Hiwatt DR-103, Brilliant channel
Orig. Cab: Hiwatt 4x12 (Fane)
DynaCab: 4x12 Lerxst
Controls: Volume, Master, Bass, Mid, Treble, Presence
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: Unique tonestack, bright & chimey tone “Based on Harry Joyce, Hylight-era model, original tubes, closet find, sounds glorious”

Has raw tone, slightly glassy & hollow in a good way. Crank it & enjoy pure British power amp distortion. Wanna hear a Hiwatt in its full glory? Listen to The Who’s Live At Leeds, quoted by some as best live rock recording of all time (Pete Townsend used CP103, based on DR103). Then there’s David Gilmour, Pink Floyd’s master of tone, who’s played Hiwatt all his life.

“The amp that started it all. DR103 has proved itself as cornerstone of such a huge variety of acts that’s provided them all with platform on which to build their own, unique sounds. Amp kicks out such pure, powerful tone it really compliments pedals put through it vs competes with them, like some other amps, yet has rich, classic ‘British’ tone, which stands out distinctively, particularly when amp’s cranked. Dual channel preamp with high/low sensitivity inputs & normal/bright options on each. Volume controls for each channel & master volume control. 3 band EQ; bass, middle & treble. 4xEL34s at power stage, either 4xECC83s or 3xECC83s & 1xECC81 (tighter sound) at preamp stage.” – Hiwatt

“Hiwatts were marketed as high-end amp & indeed their quality in construction, from use of premier Partridge transformers, to exquisite attention, to detail being made in electronic wiring & assembly, quickly further enhanced Hiwatt’s rep. Even die-hard/loyal Marshall enthusiasts have to admit the construction quality of early Hiwatts were vastly superior to Marshalls. Person responsible for this was Hiwatt’s Chief Tech Harry Joyce, who oversaw construction of these amps, built & signed many of them himself. Harry Joyce was a military-certified wiring tech & as a result, all Hiwatt produced during Harry Joyce era were produced at military-spec levels. Military specs required products made be wired in such a way components would be easily accessible & wiring itself would remain “clean”, to allow quick/efficient repairs of military equipment in the field. Looking inside a Hiwatt chassis, this attention to “Mil- Spec’s” evident, attention to detail was a primary reason Hiwatt production was limited to 40 amps a month.

Most famous Hiwatt was Custom 100 DR103. 100w output compared to Marshall Super Lead is quite different. Hiwatt DR103 is notably louder, can run much cleaner vs 100w Marshalls if needed & have tremendous headroom. Playing a Hiwatt at loud volume is an experience. When I played through one the 1st time, all of a sudden, my Marshalls felt thin in comparison. Hiwatt are loud, punchy, rich, with even-ordered harmonics & ballsy as hell! I can think of no better example of the epitome of clean/lush tube tone. OD is certainly available when Hiwatt’s pushed, but must be acquired by use of higher-gain “brilliant” channel & high- output pickup helps further.  “Normal” channel’s voiced to stay clean & powerful at all levels. Another benefit to DR103’s tone & design was its inclusion of tone controls that actually seemed to respond with much wider range vs other amps of the time. Bass response could be bassy, mids added strong punch & wide range of treble/presence controls delivered all upper freqs & sibilants desired”
Hiwatt creates a great clean tone. Glassy but different from a VOX, just listen to Pink Floyd
Presence doesn’t do a lot, but it opens up amp a little
(Reply to comment saying Hiwatts seem sterile) “Real ones are sterile. They’re more like Hi-Fi amps than guitar amps”
Tips: To roar, crank Master/channel Vol & engage Boost
“I like the Brilliant model, crank the MV, Bass turned down, use Drive to dial desired volume & gain level. To force into a lot of power amp distortion, I engage Boost” - yek
Hiwatt amps & Fane speakers go hand in hand, try 4x12 Hipower(RW) stock cab. Red Wirez stock cabs captured with neutral microphone, consider mic model in Cab block

amps.name: HIPOWER JUMPED
body: Model: Hiwatt DR-103, jumpered channels
Note: See 173 Hipower for more info

amps.name: HIPOWER NORMAL
body: Model: Hiwatt DR-103, Normal Channel
Note: See 173 Hipower for info

amps.name: HOT KITTY
body: Model: Bad Cat Hot Cat 30, Dirty channel
Orig. Cab: BadCat 1x12 (proprietary Celestion)
DynaCab: 1x12 Hot Kitty
Controls: Gain, Edge, Level, Bass, Mid, Treble, Brilliance (Hi-Cut), Master
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST

amps.name: JAZZ 120
body: Model: Roland Jazz Chorus 120
Orig. Cab: Roland 2x12 Silver AlNiCo
DynaCab: 2x12 Double Verb
Controls: Volume, Bass, Mid, Treble, Bright switch
Power tubes: Transistor (solid-state)
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: Famous for its clean tones in jazz, as well as metal tunes
“Power amp has high damping factor, impedance curve is flattened”
JC-120 players: Andy Summers, Alex Lifeson, Metallica & more
Originally used mostly in jazz scene, revival in metal/nu-metal era. Bands like Metallica & Limp Bizkit used for clean parts of songs

It’s associated with ultra-clean tones & its built-in chorus/vibrato, a true stereo chorus, called Dimensional Space Chorus by Roland. IT seems to refer to Roland’s Dimension chorus, but JC-120’s built-in chorus circuit is identical to famous vintage CE-1 pedal. As a matter of fact, JC’s chorus predates CE-1

“1st intro back in ‘75, JC-120 is one of few guitar amps that can truly be called a legend. Roland’s long-standing flagship is universally recognized by pros as benchmark in clean guitar sound, rich tone & famous built-in stereo chorus effect heard on countless popular songs over its history. Now celebrating 40 years of continuous production & counting, JC-120 remains the undisputed “king of clean” & enduring choice of serious guitarists everywhere” -Roland

“In the 80s I owned 1, though I didn’t (couldn’t) play jazz. Were a lot of these amps around, especially in jazz/jazz-rock scene. I remember seeing Billy Cobham’s guitar player use one. Although ultra-clean amp itself, handles drive pedals well. I get great OD tones with mine & H&K Tube Factor pedal or Tube Screamer” - yek
The amp does have a Distortion control, but sounds so terrible it should be forbidden by law to use it
Tips: Turn up Master. “I always crank the Master.” – yek
Replicate JC-120 Chorus: CHORUS block set to CE-2, placed AFTER Amp block (to keep it stereo)
For stereo output, JC-120 has 2x12” Roland speakers (silver Alnico). Used to have as stock cabs, but were replaced. Try other Alnico stock cabs, like 2x12 Class-A 30W Silver

amps.name: JMPRE-1 CLEAN 1
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, Clean 1
DynaCab: 4x12 1960TV
Controls: Volume, Gain, Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence, Bass shift, Depth, PA Presence (power amp)
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Rack tube preamp, based on 90s JCM 900 Marshall amp
Bands like Def Leppard, ZZ Top & Deftones known for using JMP-1
Reworked result of JMPre-1 models (Brit Pre model was 1st)
Power amp section based on 90s Marshall 100w power amp
No Master Volume, because it’s a preamp

“2 channels have preset tone stacks, then there’s a digitally controlled 4-band graphic EQ, which is B/M/T & Presence. This technique gets around problem of trying to digitally control a tone stack. Triaxis uses LDRs in place of potentiometers. Soldano X-99 uses motorized pots. Both techniques are expensive, so JMP-1 uses fixed tone stack & then digitally-controlled graphic EQ. AxeFx model of JMP-1 is “better” in sense tone controls control tone stack, rather than graphic EQ, has separate graphic EQ to further tailor sound. What I didn’t do & why people probably feel model differs, is “normalized” tone controls, so B/M/T at noon, tone stacks match. On AxeFx, may need to set Bass 3, Mid 7, etc, to get tone to match. I just used standard Marshall tone stack, whereas JMP-1 uses standard Marshall tone stack, but pots replaced with fixed resistors, but values don’t necessarily correspond to pots at noon.”

Real amp has fixed, passive tone stack & active 4-band EQ. Therefore, Bass/Mid/Treble, using Authentic Tone Controls are mapped to Graphic EQ. Clean channels only: passive tone stack is adjustable. All controls at noon, tone stack equivalent to real amp’s fixed tone stack. Can access using Ideal Tone Controls/via Axe-Edit

“Man, that Brit Pre is just a magic amp model. I know some celebrity guitarists who played JMPs for years & prefer it. With Def Lep, A/B’d AxeFx presets with Phil Collen’s actual JMP rig, we chose Brit Pre”
“JMP-1 tone controls have more range vs actual preamp, has about ±8 dB for each tone control, model has standard ±12 dB”
Tips: Visual tone control settings don’t line up to actual amp due to different tone stack

“Models of JMP-1 are well balanced. At default, they already sound fine, maybe turn up gain. I prefer non-BS models, as I don’t like too much bass in my tone, but it’s a matter of personal preference” -yek

amps.name: JMPRE-1 CLEAN 1 BASS SHIFT
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, Clean 1, bass shift on

Notes: (Bass shift) “Subtle low freq character control. It thickens bottom end & tightens playing feel. On clean models, adds body. On OD1/2, provides extra low end throb, essential for heavy rock sound”
See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

amps.name: JMPRE-1 CLEAN 2
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, Clean 2
Notes: See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

amps.name: JMPRE-1 CLEAN 2 BASS SHIFT
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, Clean 1, bass shift on

Notes: (Bass shift) “Subtle low freq character control. Selected, it thickens bottom end of sound & tightens playing feel. On clean models, it adds body. On OD1/2, it provides extra low end throb, essential for heavy rock sound”
See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

amps.name: JMPRE-1 OD1
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, OD1 mode
Notes: “warm vintage drive reminiscent of Marshall Super Lead amps through to modern day MVs”

“Features purity of distortion from golden age of plexi glass Super Leads. As gain increases, passes through degrees of distortion to a rich, higher gain mode, with right nuances for modern blues rock player. Vintage crunch, modern metal & fat blues lead sounds are all readily available” – Marshall
See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

Tips: (Def Leppard) “Amp model you’re looking for is JMPre-1 OD1. Some cabs you may like to try with that it: Factory 2 #97 2x12 RECTO V60 57 OH, Factory 2 #995 4x12 UBER V30 57 OFF-AXIS, Factory 2 #530 4x12 BRIT 70s GB 121 I”

amps.name: JMPRE-1 OD1 BASS SHIFT
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, OD1 mode, bass shift on

Notes: (Bass shift) “Subtle low freq character control. Selected, it thickens bottom end of sound & tightens playing feel. On clean models, it adds body. On OD1/2, it provides extra low end throb, essential for heavy rock sound” - Marshall
See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

amps.name: JMPRE-1 OD2
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, OD2 mode
Notes: “Smooth or aggressive, depends on EQ/gain settings”
“Tightly focused high gain. Modern, aggressive, smooth, fluid, gain levels & singing tone of channel for infinite sustain”
See 178 JMPRE-1 for more info

amps.name: JMPRE-1 OD2 BASS SHIFT
body: Model: Marshall JMP-1 Preamp, OD2 mode, bass shift on
Notes: See 178 JMPRE for info & JMP-1 Settings below:

amps.name: JR BLUES
body: Model:Fender Blues Junior 15w
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x12
(Eminence, Jensen C12N, P12R)
DynaCab: 1x12 Jr Blues
Controls: Volume, Treble, Bass, Middle, Master, Fat button
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL84
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: “Actually a decent lil amps, sounds great cranked”
Different kind of Fender, not as clean as most others. Some love’ em, some hate ‘em

“Blues Jr is a 15w, warm-toned, longtime favorite, perfect grab-n-go tube amp for stage & studio. Known for fat mid tones characteristic of EL84 output tubes, warm 12AX7 preamp tube OD, real spring reverb, simple layout, foot switch FAT boost & external speaker capability” - Fender

“Blues Jr is a pro tube guitar amp introduced in 1995 by Fender, aimed at achieving warm, tube-driven tone common in many styles of American blues & blues rock, dating back to the ‘50s, while remaining both portable & affordable. Most similar to Fender Blues Deluxe, which adds a drive channel, effects loop & uses 6L6GC output tubes for 40w of rated output. Fender Blues Jr was introduced after Fender Pro Junior, has entirely different circuitry, other than EL84 output tubes rated at 15w”
One of the few Fender amps with Master control. Has fixed presence circuit & no Bright switch
“MV in Blues Jr. is linear taper. This is ridiculous, makes it very difficult to find the sweet spot. Model uses audio taper instead, which makes finding sweet spot easier”

“Blues Jr. is a very colored preamp. Best you can do is turn Bass/Treble to 0 & Mid all the way up, that’ll make it somewhat flat, but still have bright cap on drive & bandpass between preamp & power amp”
“Reference amp has Fender speaker in it. No indication who makes it, but probably Jensen”

”Fender Blues Jr is a pro tube amp with that classic, late night blues tone found nowhere else but in a Fender. Compact size makes it ideal for rehearsal, backstage warm-up & studio work. Controls on amp are all that’s needed to get every sound from vintage clean to expressive growl. Fender vintage Special Design 12”
“The Eggie is very similar to a Blues Jr, basically same preamp with some minor tweaks.”
Tips: Use with no Damping (negative feedback) to sound like an Egnater Rebel 20
Combo has single Eminence Legend, Jensen C12N or P12R speaker (as stock cab: 1x12 Blues Junior)

amps.name: JR BLUES FAT
body: Model: Fender Blues Junior, Fat switch on
Notes: FAT switch boosts gain/mid freqs in preamp stage
Notes: See 186 Jr Blues for info
Tips: Use FAT model & turn up MV for typical Blues Jr sound, has additional preamp boost, adds warm, Fender American Tube distortion

amps.name: JS410 CRUNCH ORANGE
body: Model: Joe Satriani’s Marshall JVM410HJS, Crunch Orange (JCM 2203)
Orig. Cab: Marshall 4x12 (V30 & G12H)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman V30
Controls: Volume, Gain, Bass, Middle, Treble, Master, Presence, Resonance, Mid-Shift (off:~650Hz, on:~500Hz)
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “JVM has huge plate resistor on cathode follower, JS doesn’t”

“Model based on Joe’s personal amp, he sent it to me. When I entered values from the schematic, it didn’t match. Tapers of controls were different & were some other differences. Can only assume his personal amp is different than production models, often the case as production models are designed to be inexpensive to build (ex: using control tapers used in other models to reduce inventory, etc). Another possibility is there were changes made during production. Not uncommon for amps to have changes made to circuitry over production run. For example, Blues Jr. has had numerous revisions, 1st ones sound nothing like later models. Sometimes done due to perceived customer reaction/issues. Simply adjusting B/M/T should get you what you want. Control tapers make big difference, ex: Bass pot that’s a linear taper would need to be set to 1.0 to match a Log10A taper at 5.0”
(Difference between production amp & Satriani’s own amp?) “Yes, they measure quite differently”

(firmware 24) “Added Global MV control to JS410 amp models. MV has been renamed Channel MV. These 2 MV controls can be used to balance tone into power amp (as in real amp). Channel MV decreases high freqs as it’s turned down, whereas Global MV is transparent.”
“Models based on Joe’s personal amp, isn’t same as stock version of amp. Personal amp is modded & considerably darker vs stock.”

“Joe Satriani JVM head looks very similar to stock JVM410H, but look & listen closer, you’ll realize how different this amp is. Reverb pots replaced by 4 independent, studio-quality noise gates, 1 for each channel & as specified by Joe, footswitchable Mid-Shift button added (around 650hz off/around 500hz on). Tonally very different from stock JVMs. Clean channel based on 30th anniv. 6100 when in green mode, with orange & red modes being hotter variations of that. Crunch channel includes some popular AFD circuitry & OD channels are voice matched, for smoother transition between modes. Channels & modes re-voiced to Joe’s exact specs after experimenting with prototypes on tour/during recording sessions. Amp also features individual channel EQ, 2 master volumes & switch memory that can recall reverb, FX loop & master settings. Also emulated output can be used for recording or, as Joe does, used to monitor on stage” - Marshall

Clean channel Green mode based on 30th Ann. Marshall 6100 (Satriani’s fav clean tone), some of Marshall AFD circuitry in Crunch channel. OD1 & OD2 channels are identical (voice matched for smoother transition between modes) & re-voiced

“I love the Crunch channel because, to me, it’s as if a very important period of Marshall History’s sitting there. It represents 3 of my all-time favorite amps I use to record all the time. I’m not going to go too crazy trying to over-describe these because anyone who knows & loves Marshall will know the classic amps & sounds they’re based on
ORANGE: Based on JCM800 2203, a standard in rock music
Tips: “Joe said he puts all tone controls at around 10:00”
Marshall’s accompanying 1960A cab has 4 G12-75 speakers

amps.name: JS410 CRUNCH RED
body: Model: Marshall JVM410HJS, Crunch Red (modded JCM 2203)

Notes: RED Mode: Telling, modded JCM800 sound that defined the ‘80s & still a go-to sound for any rock band. This channel’s so useful. For me, to be able to make 2 albums with this channel, a solo record & “Chickenfoot III” & to tour using all 3 modes during shows, a true testament to how good the Crunch channel is/how many different tones it can give you”
See 188 JS410 for more info

amps.name: JS410 LEAD GREEN
body: Model: Marshall JVM410H, Lead Green (OD1 on original JVM)
Notes: Basically 191/192, w/o added Mid-Shift/few other mods
See 188 JS410 for more info

amps.name: JS410 LEAD ORANGE
body: Model: Marshall JVM410HJS, Lead Orange (more gain)

Notes: (OD channels) “It’s a crazy idea I know, but 2 channels are identical. Generally, logic & intuition would tell you rational thing to do if you got 4 channels is make them all different. That said, I’ve realized, particularly from playing live, once you get tone dialed in, you wish you could also get it a little bit louder, with slightly different EQ. I’ve found this the case more often than not & no one wants to walk around with 2 heads if they don’t have to, they’d much rather have 1 head with sounds in it. Hence, reason for OD1 & OD2 being the same. So working with Santiago, we made the 2 channels essentially the same as OD1 on original JVM410H, but then, at a click of a programmable button, the added Mid-Shift, you can make either, or both of them, be identical to original JVM OD2 channel too, in terms of EQ curve. So on JVM410HJS, you can dial in that big, full, organic “Get Your Buzz On” CF guitar tone. Or, you can activate that switch & there’s a bit of a scoop, which I found while still being a really fat tone, makes more room for Sammy’s vocal in overall shape of the sounds that make up Chickenfoot. But then, by the same token, I find that on my solo work, that space needs to be filled by my guitar, so I need extra midrange to do so. I’ve now got the ability to have both those sounds & as gain structures are identical & the way 2 channels behave are also the same, can successfully use the channels back to back
ORANGE Mode: Even more Gain
See 188 JS410 for more info

amps.name: JS410 LEAD RED
body: Model: Marshall JVM410HJS, Lead Red (even more gain)

Notes: RED Mode: None more Gain Nigel Tufnel! In conclusion: I don’t wanna say “I’m as happy as a peach”, as that’s a horrible analogy! Let’s just say I’m spoiled for choice because there’s just so many options for how wild you want your guitar to sound within the JVM410HJS’ 12 Modes. I love it, I really do.”
See 188 JS410 for more info

amps.name: LEGEND 100
body: Model: Carvin Legacy VL100, Steve Vai’s VL100, lead channel
Orig Cab: Carvin 2x12/4x12 (V30)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman V30
Controls: Presence, Treble (11k), Mid(650Hz), Bass(80Hz), Drive, Vol
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “Thing that makes a Legacy unique is tone stack, it uses a “James” tone stack which is more like hi-fi tone controls.”

“Adds the “Legato 100” model based on Steve Vai’s Legacy 100. This model is unique in that it uses a Baxandall (sometimes called a “James”) tone stack. More often used on tube hi-fi gear the controls are less interactive vs typical guitar amp tone stack & has different tonal quality”

“The Legato 100 is based on a one-of-a-kind version owned by a certain Californian guitar player. It’s a very dark amp. Partly due to tapers on tone pots. With everything at noon, tone stack looks like a highshelf with a good amount of high freq cut. Tone stack is a Baxandall, more common in hi-fi gear than guitar amps. It lends a unique character to tone.”

“What is happening is the power amp is oscillating at that very low frequency cabinet resonance. This occurs because the Legacy has so much negative feedback (12dB, highest of any amp I’ve modeled). Combined with the smallish output transformer there is a loss of phase margin at low frequencies which causes the power amp to become unstable. The Rumble cab is unique in that it is ported & has two different types of speakers in it. This causes a double resonance & one of the resonances is at a very low frequency. For typical power amps with reasonable amounts of feedback this isn’t an issue.”
“The tonestack in a Legacy is a James. A Baxandall is active.”

Tips: “SV’s personal settings are: Drive: 7.5, Bass: 6, Mid: 4, Treble: 8, Presence: 8. I had to increase the Mid control on the model to about 5 to match the amp which indicates the mid pot on the amp was greater than spec. This is not unusual. Typical consumer-grade pots have pretty poor tolerance in both end-to-end resistance & resistance at the midpoint.”
Cali Leggy: “Drive 6.5, Bass 7.0, Middle/Treble 3.5, everything else default. High gain: Boost switch on, turn down Drive”- yek
Range of tone controls is unlike other amps. Ex, difference between outer positions of Middle control is enormous.

“For mild tube saturation, set DRIVE control between 1-2. For some of the best saturation, set control around 3-6. For full blown OD, set control between 8-10. Drive settings above 8 are subject to over saturation, depending on output of guitar pickups used. High-output pickups can over saturate causing sluggish distortion. Play your guitar with its volume at 10 & decrease amount of drive until crisp highs come back.” - Carvin
Legacy has matching 2x12/4x12 cab with V30s. Try 2x12 SV Legend cab

amps.name: LEGEND 100 II
body: Model: Carvin VL300 Legacy 3, channel 3
Controls: Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence, Drive, Volume, Master
Notes: See 193 Legend 100 for more info

amps.name: MATCHBOX CHIEFTAIN 1
body: Model: Matchless Chieftain, Single channel
Orig. Cab: Matchless 2x12 (G12H)
DynaCab: 2x12 Chiefman
Controls: Vol, Master, Bass, Mid, Treble, Brilliance (HiCut)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Secret of Chieftain is split cathode bias. Most Class-A amps use shared cathode bias, but Chieftain uses separate cathode bias networks, less bias shift (less fizz)”

“Treble control in Chieftain is actually a Preamp Presence control, similar to Presence control in a Dual Rectifier in Modern mode. It’s separate from tone stack & located later in gain stages. Tone stack has no treble control”
Matchless are often associated with country music/Nashville but are used by artists like Kings of Leon & Dave Grissom
Volume control sets gain of 1st preamp stage, acts as a sensitivity & drive control

Brilliance control adjusts overall high-freq response of power amp, used to smooth out high-freq edginess/bring it forward, for extra flexibility & tone shaping by widening/narrowing amp’s broadband response. It’s mapped to Hi-Cut in amp block. Default 0, may like it higher to tame brightness
Master control adjusts overall gain of output amp. For max clean headroom set Master to maximum position. For dirtier tones, lower Master & turn up Volume
Tips: Bass control has very wide range, excessive amounts can cause floppiness & lack of definition at high volumes.
Use Hi-Cut to tame brightness
Matchless amps commonly use a combo of G12H/G12M speakers. Try 2x12 Boutique stock cabs
“This amp with Master cranked, Hi-Cut ~12 o’clock, low Bass & Input Drive anywhere from 2-6 is a delight to play. I love power amp compression created by high MV” - yek

Chnl 2 EF86 has high-gain pentode coupled to tone switch

Chnl 2 utilizes pentode vacuum tube as 1st preamp stage, 6-position rotary switch follows, controls tone envelope. Preamp tube’s capable of more gain vs chnl 1 & capable of OD harder. Tone control’s passive, may be channel of choice for heavier sound, especially when OD & used with MV control. Multi-position rotary tone switch varies dimension/ width of tone envelope. Min position (counter-clockwise) has thinner sound, becomes thicker clockwise, 6 total tonal textures” - Manual

amps.name: MR Z HWY 66
body: Model: Dr. Z Route 66
Orig. Cab: Dr. Z 2x12 (V30 & G12H)
DynaCab: 2x12 Chiefman
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble
Preamp tubes: EF86
Power tubes: KT66
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Has thick blues & medium rock tones

“Route 66 is all original Dr. Z design based on incredible KT66 tube originally built by Genelex, tube generates “Milkshake Thick” tones heard on John Mayall’s “Bluesbreaker” album featuring Eric Clapton (“Beano Album” as it’s known). Not a duplicate of Marshall JTM45, but completely original spin, with new tonal results. Has EF-86 front-end, 9-pin pentode, offers incredible gain & input dynamics, normally in high-end stereos, mostly for accurate transfer of input signal, balance & headroom. Route 66 has deceptively simple tone stack, Volume, Bass, Treble & feeds non-negative feedback Phase Inverter, for true harmonic content & full output tube dynamics. GZ-34 Tube Rectifier to complete the round enveloped tone. Piano-like clarity, with endless sustain, even at low volumes. 32w truly sing when driven hard in focused, thick distortion, with tightest bass response you’ve ever heard” - Dr Z

“EF86 front end coupled to big bottled KT66 output tubes lets loose thick low end & clear singing top. Plenty of classic rock n roll crunch & sweet blues sustain. Makes any pedal sound like it was built into amp”
Tips: Put amp into OD, turn up all 3 controls (Vol, Treble, Bass)
On amp, Treble/Bass operate normal til 12:00 & dials in gain past noon
Amp has no Master, keep at default for authenticity
Try: Bass 7, max Treble, Vol~7, Ultra-Res 2x12 Boutique cab -yek

amps.name: MR Z MZ-38
body: Model: Dr. Z Maz 38 SR
Orig. Cab: Dr. Z 1x12 (G12H)
DynaCab: 1x12 AC20
Controls: Volume, Master, Bass, Mid, Treble, Cut
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL84
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “Dr. Z is quintessential country amp”

“IMO poor design, they exhibit a papery buzz. Problem with most cathode biased Class A amps is 1. aren’t really Class A (more like hot Class AB) & 2. Go into Class B operation when OD. Cathode capacitor charges up & bias point shifts dramatically, causes lotta crossover distortion (papery buzz), worse on lower notes as lower notes have more energy & charge cap more. Probably reason Leo Fender switched to fixed bias amps with negative feedback. Fixed bias is just that, bias point’s fixed so amp doesn’t shift into Class B operation when overdriven (not as much anyways, depends on bias circuit, grid stoppers, etc). Negative feedback linearizes amp & reduces crossover distortion (at expense of gain). That’s why buzzing went away when you increased negative fdbk. Crossover distortion’s a unique sound. For cleaner sounds tends to be objectionable. For OD sounds some find it desirable. EVH ostensibly liked his amps biased cold for some crossover distortion. There’s even some amps that have circuits to intentionally generate crossover distortion, some distortion pedals also do this. Lowering Cathode Resistance reduces crossover distortion, keeps amp in Class A operation longer but tubes run hotter & don’t last as long, it’s not a problem with virtual amps though. I forget actual values, but I’m pretty certain if you look at Cathode Resistance value for those amps, it’s pretty high, means amps are biased somewhat cold to begin with & shift to very cold when overdriven. Then there’s shared vs. split cathode. If you look at schematics online, Maz-38 is shared cathode. I could never get model to sound exactly like our ref amp. Finally I traced the entire circuit & our particular amp is a split cathode. Soon as I changed model to split cathode, it was spot on. This is not exposed to user though (there’s a hidden shared/split switch). Our particular amp says Humbucker in chassis. Not sure if difference in cathode circuit’s due to Dr. Z likes split cathode for humbucking guitars?” - FAS g38 satisfies many different styles & playing approaches. Dial in mid ‘60s blackface sounds & UK flavored chime. A truly versatile amp, whether Blues, Country, Roots, or Rock” - Dr. Z
Tips: Ratio Drive/Master is important to keep tone tight
Pick a 1x12” G12H, V30 or Alnico Blue stock cab

amps.name: MR Z MZ-8
body: Model: Dr. Z Maz 8
Orig. Cab: Dr. Z 1x12 G12H
DynaCab: 1x12 AC20
Controls: Volume, Master, Bass, Mid, Treble, Cut
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL84
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: The “quintessential country amp”
Famous Dr. Z amp players: Brad Paisley, Joe Walsh, etc.
“Based on Maz 18 platform, Maz 8 is a full featured 8w amp that will gives incredible range of shimmering cleans to full on rock action at reasonable volume levels.

Maz 8 gives you true class A experience. EL84 output section can run in Pentode or Triode mode. In Pentode is signature sound of Maz 18. Chimey top end, sweet low end bloom, at much lower volume, plenty of dynamic range for clean country picking to grinding riff rock bliss. EQ bypass’ a great gain boost, for effortless leads & sustain for days. In triode mode, a more vintage feel/sound, volume level drops back, tone fattens up, like you plugged into a 50s lost gem. EQ bypass in triode will have you reaching for your slide” - Dr. Z
Tips: Ratio Drive/Master is important to keep tone tight
Amp has no negative feedback (Damping 0)

“Like Maz 8 even more than 38. Not limited to country. These amps are capable of putting out vintage medium rock tones too & excel at clean tones at verge of breakup. Loose & bold power amp tones. I use 2x12 Boutique stock cabs” - yek
Pick a 1x12” G12H, V30 or Alnico Blue stock cab

amps.name: NUCLEAR-TONE
body: Model: Swart Atomic Space Tone
Orig. Cab: Swart 1x12 (Mojotone BV-25m)
DynaCab: 1x12 Nuclear Tone
Controls: Volume (Drive), Tone (Treble), Tremolo effect
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6V6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Unique character comes from PI. It’s a cathodyne phase inverter so it doesn’t drive grids as hard”

“Known for tremolo, why we used bias trem in #63 preset, works by modulating power tube grid bias. Side effect is less pronounced playing hard (auto ducks). Since it’s modulating bias, has Univibe-like effect, as phase changes a bit too.”
Has wonderful bias-regulated tube tremolo & tube reverb

“Amp has 20w of twin 6V6 power in high Class-A biased AB & brings a soulful, rich, near 3D tone. Has to be one of the most expressive amp made to our knowledge & we’ve tried a few. Tube reverb & tremolo, virtually nonexistent in today’s marketplace, is just part of the picture. Includes 12” Custom British Vintage Series BV-25m that screams TONE with body & soul. Amp has excellent breakup. Amp’s in its own league.

Idea of amp hit me when I was working on a vintage Gibson Scout guitar amp. Had Reverb, Tremolo, & 15w of pure rocking vintage tone power but only 3 preamp tubes & 2 EL84 output tubes. Thought to my self, I’ve never seen an amp that could do so much with so little & sound surprisingly good, but I knew it could be better. I knew something could be built revolving around my favorite little tone generator, the 6V6. Only thing I’ve found to equal the soul & tone of ST-6V6se, but MORE power
Damn if it’s not a winner - this beast sounds amazing.
Brings more tube soul than I thought possible, even compared to some of my favorite vintage amps. All tube circuitry, even Tremolo & Reverb, leading into a 12” Mojotone BV-25m speaker
I’m so excited by this amp, aurally & visually. When playing out with this beast, everyone that knows comes up to talk about the sound. Like OG ST-6V6se, nothing out there like it.

AC cleaned/rectified by reliable JJ 5AR4, a robust tube with excellent sonics due to total lack of solid-state circuits in rectification stage (SS rect leads to more hard/harsh tone & slams tubes on powerup for excess tube wear). JJ500V Surface mount capacitor is mains filtering device” – Swart
Tips: Amp’s Tone control mapped to Treble
Amp’s Bias Tremolo has also been modeled

“IMHO Nuclear-Tone model’s a real gem. Combo of Tweed & VOX character. Find yourself playing endless solo sessions & drifting away. Don’t forget to turn on Tremolo in Amp block (set Depth 100%). I set Input Drive around 4 for a clean tone with bod. Treble is ~4 (remember: this is Tone control)” - yek
Has Mojotone BV-25m speaker, similar to G12M greenback. Cab model of combo in stock cabs: 1x12 Nuclear Tone

amps.name: ODS-100 CLEAN
body: Model: Dumble ODS-100 Ford, clean channel
Orig. Cab: Dumble 2x12 or 4x12 (G12-65, EVM/EV-L)
DynaCab: 4x12 Rumble EV12L
Controls: Volume, Bass, Middle, Treble, OD Level, OD Ratio, Master, Presence, Bright switch, Mid boost switch (Deep), footswitch PAB
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: Dumble is THE most expensive amp available
Famous Dumble players: Robben Ford, Larry Carlton, John Mayer, Santana, Keith Urban, SRV, Eric Johnson, Bonamassa
Howard Alexander Dumble retired in 90s. Under 300 amps total

There’s always talk on forums about Robben Ford’s Dumble tone. It’s an elusive tone, worshiped by lot of jazz, blues & fusion players. Thick, mid-heavy, smooth, stable, very touch- sensitive. That trademark tone isn’t for everybody: hit a power chord & it may be terrible to your ears. Note flip is a certain tonal character attributed to Dumble & some of its clones. So what’s this mysterious note flip? “Sounds kinda like it’s starting to feedback, but it’s not. It flips note up so a different harmonic order is heard, or something like that”.
There’s more to Dumble than Ford’s tone. Each was voiced specifically for its buyer. Replicating an original Dumble is hard. Amp circuit often has “goop” on it, hiding electronics.
“Has 2 drive controls, 1 at input, other is in lead boost section”

“Hate to admit it, but there’s something special about Dumbles. I don’t know what it is, but there’s something in the voicing. Various clones I have are cool & have their own thing going but they don’t sound like Dumble. Hard amp to play though. Unforgiving but tremendous clarity”

“Only amps with bright cap on MV are Dumbles & their various derivatives. Was/is done to compensate for lack of buffer for fx loop. Loop send is taken right off MV, means output impedance is very high. Any significant length of cable then attenuates highs due to cable capacitance. Bright cap boosts highs to compensate. Same idea as bright cap on guitar’s volume knob. Crude. FWIW, this is a terrible loop design, but “it’s a Dumble” so it’s part of mystique. Dumble then released “Dumbleator”, nothing more than buffer/recovery in an external box. Dumble fanatics, of course, claim to have true Dumble experience, you MUST use Dumbleator, just another load of BS”

Firmware 21: “Added “OD Volume” to Dumble-type amp models (ODS-100, Two Stone, etc), sometimes labeled Ratio/Lead Master. As MV on these amps often has bright cap, OD Volume allows setting MV higher to counteract bright cap, then lowers power amp drive via Volume”
“Dumble tonestack is same as Plexi, except it’s plate driven, not cathode follower driven. Increased source impedance increases insertion loss, which increases more at higher freqs”
“ODS Clean default tone stack is Skyliner, location set “MID”, matches amp. Notch freq ~422Hz, 400Hz is appropriate, keep reference frequency value default for accurate tonestack”

Controls on original amp: preamp Volume (Input Drive), Treble/Mid/ Bass (shared preamp tone controls), OD Level/OD Ratio, Master/ Presence (power amp). Clean channel has bright cap on Master Volume, causes tone to get brighter as MV is reduced & vice-versa.
Tips: Since V1 is running clean, can get more gain by simply increasing Input Trim. It’ll have basically same effect as increasing trimmer”

Amp has 3 switches which work on both channels: 1.	 Bright: adds sparkle, less noticeable when volume is turned up 2.	Mid: boosts mids for more gain, like FAT switch in amp block 3.	Rock/Jazz: Jazz setting adds some low-pass filtering
On real amp, 1st 2 switches often off, Rock/Jazz often set Rock
Mid switch (Deep) shorts out 390pF cap in tonestack (in series with 2nF cap), leaving 2nF cap. If PAB on, lowers lowcut freq”
Gain controlled through Volume/OD Level (Input Drive/Overdrive). On amps like this, 1st control sets sensitivity, 2nd sets amount of distortion
“Input Drive increases gain amount turned clockwise. As gain increases, tone shifts from treble/upper mid emphasis (up front sparkling tone), to lower mid/bass emphasis (thick meaty tone)
Overdrive increases gain turned clockwise (no tonal balance alteration)

Different combos of Input Drive & Overdrive has dramatic effect on response of amp & personality of instrument. Easy to get familiar with action of these controls & amazed with ability to make any guitar sound mellow, fat, soulful or aggressive”
Models with Input Drive/OD controls feel elastic & spongy, a joy to play

Master Volume amp, amp’s distortion created by preamp tubes, not in power amp. MV, which works in power amp section, still very important to tone & feel. Have to decide setting in block work best for you. Default’s good starting point
“Maybe best clean model IMO. Really crank MV(like real amp)”
Zendrive pedal designed to mimic Dumble tone. Robben Ford himself uses pedal through Fender amps (Model: Zen Master)
Cliff’s Dumble cab w/ EV speakers IR captured (4x12 Rumble cabs)
Alt, try stock cab 1x12 Bludo, dual-ported 1x12 closed-back cab IR, modern replacement for EVL-SRO: Blackhawk Alnico WGS
For Robben Ford tone, need G12-65 speaker. No near-field IR of G12-65 among stock cabs. 2x12 Double Amp stock cab also works well, an IR of Two-Rock CR12, sounds similar to G12-65

amps.name: ODS-100 FORD
body: Model: #102 Dumble, Schematics of Robben Ford #102 Dumble OD channel, PAB off
Orig. Cab: Dumble 2x12 or 4x12 (G12-65, EVM)
DynaCab: 4x12 Rumble EV12L
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: ODS-100 Ford amp models based on schematics, supposedly represent Robben Ford’s Tan Dumble, serial #102

“ODS-100 Ford model not based on HRM model with HRM tonestack removed. Whether or not model matches a Dumble is unknown, haven’t been able to find non-HRM Dumble yet. Model based on schematics given to us, supposedly accurate representation of Ford’s Dumble. Voltage divider at output of V1B set to 0.078 based on values indicated on schematic for set-point of trimmer. As no Dumbles are identical, stands to reason a person’s interpretation of ideal Dumble voicing won’t agree with another’s. Since V1 is runs clean, can get more gain increasing Input Trim, similar effect as increasing trimmer”

“Signal divider, correctly called voltage divider, calculated by taking resistance to ground divided by sum of resistances. Here, 26.7/317=0.084, close to values given (0.078). Our model uses local feedback on V1B (~2-3dB less gain), accounts for voltage divider’s affect on AC load-line. Dumble stages usually has AC load-line different from DC load-line due to load resistance’s significance in relation to plate resistor. As V1A & V1B run clean (linear), Input Trim accomplishes same thing as raising trimmer into drive stages, as linear system is commutative (see any intro Signals & Systems text for linear system properties). Commutative property is where AxB=BxA, so doesn’t matter if gain is before or after linear stages”
See 203 ODS-100 for more info
Robben Ford Dumble + G12-65 Sound & Presets (FAS forum)
Robben Ford Amp Settings (Guitar Chalk)
Robben Ford’s Dumble Settings (sorry for crap image quality):
Vol~3.5, Bright off, Deep off, Rock mode, Treble~3.2, Mid~4.4, Bass~6.4, OD level~3.3, OD ratio~5, Master~3.1, Presence~3
Ford’s Dumble Settings on Instagram post (crap quality again):
Vol~4, Bright off, Deep off, Rock mode, Treble~6, Mid~5, Bass~4.5, OD level~4, OD ratio~4, Master~3.3, Presence~3.7

amps.name: PLEXI 100W 1970
body: Model: 1970 Marshall Plexi g100W EL34 model, jumpered channels
Orig. Cab: Marshall 4x12 (G12M, G12H)
DynaCab: 4x12 1960TV
Controls: Vol Nrml/Treble, Bass, Middle, Treble, Presence
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: EL34
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: 0.68uF cap on 2nd triode
“The world’s greatest rock amp is a Marshall “Plexi”

(Reply to top end squish when digging in) ”What you hear is output transformer high freq resonance. Old Plexis didn’t do it, they had good transformers. New ones are crappy & resonate at cutoff freq, since they’re underdamped, it causes raspy, fizzy texture to distortion. Transformer resonance adjustable but parameter isn’t exposed to FAS users. When you turn it up you get that same squishy rasp you’re hearing. For Plexis I have damping set flat with no resonance, as that’s how our vintage Plexis measure. Our 50W is little underdamped but people complain about raspy highs so I erred on safe side” - Cliff
(Reply to BMT knobs doing little to tone) “All distortion comes from power amp, so tone controls don’t do much”
“Plexi 100W 1970 model is a non-MV Plexi. Circuit’s slightly different vs late 60s model”
“The 1970 model has less negative feedback”

So why’s Plexi become so popular amp? “1st n maybe most important is sense of dynamics/rich harmonics. No large- production amp created before/after early Plexi series has captured feel of a player through varied degrees of dynamics & coloration from (mostly) EL34 tube circuits. Call it a lucky accident with Marshall, but they were able to create amplifiers that really responded well to guitars played through them. Wide palette of distortion color that’s rich, full, powerful & timeless in tone is what these amps deliver. Takes some time to really get used to playing a basic amp like a plexi. No multi channels, reverb, effects & no master volume. Turn up the amp & play. Play hard & amp rewards you with fullness of tone & smooth distortion. Back off playing, amp will respond & move into lighter shades of OD. Roll down guitar volume some for warm clean sound. There’s nothing like feeling a set of power/ preamp tubes ODing together musically” -
Plexis are raw, unrefined, honest & touch sensitive. Hit a power chord & feel explosion of sound in your face/body
Numerous guitar heroes played some kind of Plexi: Pete Townsend, Jimi Hendrix, Jimmy Page, Angus Young, Yngwie Malmsteen, Jeff Beck & of course Eddie Van Halen
Some people complain that a Plexi sounds fizzy. “It’s the way a Plexi’s supposed to sound, due to cathode follower. That raspiness helps it cut through a mix. I own 3, all fizzy”

Many Plexi players use patch cables to jumper inputs: 2nd input of channel 1 goes to 1st input of channel 2 for benefit of both channels at once & 2 Drive controls. Set at same position, or keep Normal Drive lower vs Treble Drive

amps.name: PLEXI 100W HIGH
body: Model: ‘69 Marshall Super Lead Plexi 100w, High Treble channel
Notes: 0.68uF cap on 2nd triode
See 209  Plexi 100w 1970 for more Plexi info

“All pots are log pots in our amp. I was puzzled by pot tapers but they look original. I thought about it & wondered if it’s why Bogner amps use log pots. His stuff’s definitely Plexi-inspired, or maybe all Marshall could find at surplus electronics store that week was log pots?”
“100w power supply sags more, ours sags near dbl our 50w”

“Marshalls in those days were wildly inconsistent. Conflicting reports of tone stacks. Reference has log pots. George Metropolous says most he’s seen had log pots. Some claim linear pots were more prevalent”

“Our 100W Plexi is sorta between ‘68-69. 820ohm cathode resistor on V1B. Coupling cap is 2.2n. Feedback’s 47K off speaker jack(!!!). Model assumes a full stack with 2 16ohm cabs, the equivalent of 8ohm tap”

“Our amp has 560p treble cap & 10A treble pot taper, both appear to be original. Tech who checked it out after I bought it said it was one of the best sounding 100W Plexis he’d ever heard. The tech just happens to be Alan Phillips of Carol-Ann Amps. So I modeled it to agree with the reference amp”
“Default tone stack is Plexi 100W. Equivalent tone stack with linear mid & treble pots is Plexi. Dumble tone stack is same as Plexi, except it is plate driven instead of cathode follower driven”
Tips: Lead (Treble) chnl has boosted bright tone, borders on painful. Popular in Plexi to clip bright cap. Amp block: Turn off Bright/adjust Bright Cap (Adv page), loss of amp gain
“Our Plexi 100 has log taper tone pots. For a more conventional 100W Plexi sound, use the 1959SLP models”

amps.name: PRINCETONE 5F2
body: Model:Fender Tweed Princeton Late ‘50s 5F2-A model
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x10 (C10n)
DynaCab: 1x8 Princetone
Controls: Volume, Tone
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6V6
Tonestack location: PRE

Notes: 1st widely-produced Princeton, used a 6SL7 or 6SC7 dual-triode tube, 2 stages of RC-coupled voltage in preamp section, power amp had a cathode-biased 6V6 beam power tetrode, configured for Class A operation. Had a volume control & simple low-pass tone to control treble response. Princeton circuits up through 5C2 differed from Fender Champ, with 2 (vs 1) preamp stage (6SC7 dual-triode vs 6SJ7 pentode) & added tone control, not in Champs, 12AX7 Princeton models 5D2 to 5F2-A were basically Champ circuits 5D1 to 5F1, with tone control & slightly larger output transformer

“Princeton Amp’s often misinterpreted as a Princeton Reverb without reverb. Just looking at front panel & knob functions, may seem so, but closer study of circuit design reveals Princeton Reverb has an extra gain stage (half of 12AX7 V3 tube) just after dry/wet reverb signals are mixed, means there’s 1 extra tube stage that can cause preamp gain & contribute to tone with sustaining harmonics, compression & sag. Hence, Princeton- Amp is cleaner than Princeton-Reverb when volume is pushed beyond 3-4. Volume knob is less sensitive on Princeton Amp & you can play them on volume 7-8, still sounding clean
Tips: Tone control mapped to Treble

amps.name: PRINCETONE AA964
body: Model: Fender Princeton 68- 70 Silverface AA964, no reverb
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x10
DynaCab: 1x10 SF Princetone
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6V6
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: “Based on Fender AA964 Princeton, modeled amp is early CBS Silverface still using pre-CBS design/components”

Compared to many bigger Fender amps, Princeton Reverb (PR) went through CBS/silverface periods with very little changes. Rectifier tube was one tube & circuit changes where 5U4GB was replaced by GZ34. In build quality & component selection (brands of caps, resistors, pots), silverfaces not as robust as blackfaces. Glued/stapled baffles on silverface amps is one big difference of blackface & silverfaces, or cabs. Still, many consider silverface Princeton Reverb tone as good as blackface. Being almost a blackface amp with a “wrong” faceplate, large # of silverfaces became popular player’s amps”
Mesa’s MkI is a heavily hot-rodded Princeton, modded preamp, Bassman transformer, higher gain output
Has Volume, Bass & Treble controls, tremolo & 2 inputs
Tips: No Master, keep at default for authenticity
There’s several 10” Princeton speakers as stock cabs
Has 2 inputs, Fractal models of Fender amps are based on High input on amp. For Low input equivalent, set Input Trim to 0.5

amps.name: PRINCETONE REVERB
body: Model: Fender Princeton Reverb g1966 Blackface AA964 model
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x10
DynaCab: 1x10 SF Princetone
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6V6
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: “Has extra gain stage due to reverb recovery(more gain)”

In 1961, a new Princeton of fundamentally different design was introduced, instead of being essentially an upgraded Champ, was more like a Jr Deluxe. This “brownface” version used a 7025 dual triode in preamp, 12AX7 dual triode, half operates a tremolo oscillator, other serves as split-load phase inverter & 2 6V6GT tubes, fixed-biased in Class AB push-pull configuration in power section. In ‘63, tone control replaced with individual bass & treble knobs & the Princeton Reverb was born

“Princeton Amp is often misinterpreted as Princeton Reverb without reverb. Just looking at front panel & knob functions, may seem so. Closer study of circuit design reveals the Princeton Reverb has an extra gain stage (half of 12AX7 V3 tube) just after dry & wet reverb signals are mixed, means there’s an extra tube stage, can cause preamp gain & contribute to tone with sustaining harmonics, compression & sag. Hence, Princeton- Amp is cleaner vs Princeton Reverb if volume pushed past 3-4

When it hit stores that year, 5150 turned a lot of influential ears, especially hard rock & metal guitarists looking for a new sound to complement extreme new music coming from the underground. Guitarist/metal producer Andy Sneap was working in Nottingham UK guitar store when 5150 hit the street

“Peavey 5150 was huge step forward for high-gain amps. Previously we tried to mod amps or do tricks with pedals before the preamp. Was 1st amp you could plug straight into for heavier, thrashier tone”

“In amp’s master section, Resonance alters damping factor around speaker’s resonant peak, so amp controls transient response of loudspeaker. Higher amp’s damping factor, more control amp exerts over speaker. Resonance reduces damping factor at lower freqs, causing dramatic changes to low-end response & allowing speaker to recreate low freqs with clarity & punch. Patented Peavey Resonance circuit was key to bringing metal’s new lows to masses”

“Defining attribute largely responsible for 5150 sound is fixed bias. Commonly described analog a car engine & its respective idle, 5150 bias was set to lower value (lower engine “idle”), resulted in Power Tubes running at lower energy, known as cold-biased. While electrical theory behind this can easily be examined/theoreticized, 5150 & configuration resulted in more controllable gain setting (ie. having more forgiving sonic range vs similar “hot-biased” configs. This engineering choice set 5150 up to intrinsically sound best with minimal change. Since tube-amps are still uncontested in music amps, far as tonal quality’s concerned, (see hard-clipping) optimal setting for tubes are when they’re pushed to natural distortion (ie. Increases in volume or Bells(dB). Thus, allows heavy gain without sacrificing tonal definition, amp could be pushed due to colder biasing, needing more current, vs hotter setting from beginning (volume/potentiometer knob value of 1)”
Tips: Peavey’s 6505 cab has V30 speakers
Original 4x12 cab had Sheffield speakers (4x12 PVH cabs)

“I have a “holy grail” Block Letter 5150. Sounds way better vs most 5150s for some reason, which is good for you guys. I think it’s partly due to fact it has bias mod, it’s biased bit warmer vs stock version”

amps.name: PVH 6160 BLOCK
body: Model: Peavey “Block Letter” EVH 5150, Lead channel
Orig. Cab:
DynaCab:
Controls: Pre Gain, Post Gain (Master), Low, Mid, High, Resonance/ Depth, Presence
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (JJ)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Eddie Van Halen’s first signature amp, a collab with Peavey. After endorsement ended, amp continued as Peavey’s 6505, same amp as original EVH 5150
Model based on High Gain input. 2 channels: Rhythm/Lead (based on Lead channel). Controls: Pre Gain (Input Drive), Post Gain (Master), Low, Mid, High, Resonance (Depth), Presence

Why “5150”? It’s the police code for a crazy person on the loose. After Electro-Voice complained about block letters on side of face plate, was replaced with Eddie’s signature. Around same time, Sylvania tubes were replaced with other tubes, supposedly of lesser quality & why block letter 5150 amps are held in high esteem vs later signature ones, no other differences for models

“5150 has gained popularity with modern hard rock, hardcore punk & metal bands/guitarists due to large amount of distortion. Jerry Cantrell (Alice in Chains) uses this amp. While touring with Van Halen, Cantrell asked EVH, “if I could buy one off him at end of tour & when I got home there were 3 full stacks & 2 guitars waiting for me.” In 2004, Peavey & EVH parted ways, Eddie taking 5150 brand name with him, resulted in renaming the amp as Peavey 6505, with slightly updated styling, but original circuitry. 5150 II, which contains an extra preamp tube for more headroom & gain on Rhythm channel, is old equivalent to new 6505+. In 2010, Peavey released a new amp for 6505 line, 6534+. It’s much like 6505+, but 6534 has EL34 power tubes instead of 6L6 power tubes on standard 6505 amps” g1992 was a crucial year for guitar-based rock music. Thrash metal peaked, hair metal evaporated, grunge went Platinum & guitar amp that’d soon eviscerate them all was about to inspire a new gen of players/producers to remake metal in its image.
Peavey’s engineers put finishing touches on the 5150, a new breed of high-gain guitar amp outfitted with 9 tubes, hyper-responsive tone stack & soon patented Resonance control, lynchpin to its tone
No Master, keep at default for authenticity
Has 2 inputs, Fractal models of Fender amps are based on High input. For Low input equivalent, set Input Trim to 0.5
There’s several 10” Princeton speakers as stock cabs

Notes: “A great amp, sorta Marshall/SLO vibe, but more open”
“Revv actually has MV that makes sense. Instead of driving power amp into oblivion around 3, power amp OD doesn’t start happening until you turn knob up quite a bit”

Revv provide a mix of traditional tones & ultra high gain, modern features & great build quality. This tube amp takes tone from most brutal high gain aggression to touch-sensitive Nashville clean & crunch. Each of 4 channels has distinctive tone & is perfectly usable.

Green channel reminds of straight Marshall-esque rock tone, where Drive switch alters gain structure. g3 Green models probably correspond to 3 Drive switch settings (Off/Blue/Red), altering gain & reduces intensity of certain freqs
Using Revv amps: Jeff Loomis, but also roots-oriented players
Bright, Cut & Fat switches extend highs, mids & lows (Bright engaged by default). AFAIK, Cut/Fat controls in Fractal models don’t exactly mimic functionality of switches on real amp
Players report that both real amp & Fractal models don’t require a lot of tweaking to acquire a great tone
Tips: Revv uses Celestion Vintage 30 speakers in its cabs. Default speaker impedance curve in models is that of MESA 4x12 Rectifier cab. Start with 4x12 Recto DynaCab

“Ruby Rocket’s based on a Paul Ruby Rocket, which is based on a Trainwreck Rocket, but with some notable differences, which in turn is based on a VOX”

“Bright switch changes a coupling cap & the treble cap in the tone stack, cuts bass, shifts midrange & lowers gain slightly. That’s why there’s 2 separate models, otherwise you’d just engage Bright switch under Treble knob”
Trainwreck Rocket & Ruby Rocket sound like a VOX AC30 Top Boost, as is with Morgan & Matchless amps, many think the Ruby’s a better version of that British amp

body: Model: Bogner Shiva 20th Anniversary, Clean channel
Orig. Cab: Bogner 1x12 or 2x12 (Classic 80, V30)
DynaCab: 2x12 Lead 80
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble, Bright switch, Presence, Master, Excursion (Depth)
Preamp tubes: ECC83
Power tubes: KT88
Tonestack location: MID
Notes: Warm clean tone & woody, thick Bogner gain sound

KT88 Shiva has less gain vs EL34 model. “Historically, KT88 far more popular with hifi stereo manufacturers vs guitar amp builders, given high-power/low-distortion character. Due to this, often replace 6550 tubes for a less distorted amp tone”

“Has 2 highly evolved channels, capable of clean tones that shimmer & tube saturation so rich & powerful it should be illegal. Clean channel has an entire new boost circuit. Unlike tonestack bypass boost on other Shiva, this boost has a separate gain stage, brings channel to Plexi territory if boost engaged. Tone controls stay in circuit & affect sound”

Power amp dampening control added to the back panel. Labeled Excursion, it’s great to dial in a full sound at low volumes or adjust right balanced bass to a cab. Be careful, as it can add massive bottom to your sound, which might overpower other freqs & cover up an overall balanced sound. Best kept low at higher volumes” - Bogner
Tips: For beefy clean & OD tones, turn up Input Drive
Turn up Treble a lot to make it less dark
Bright switch is less effective higher Volume/Drive is set
“Stays clean until high Volume settings. Beefy clean tone, especially turning up MV (always do). Dark at default settings, but right Treble/Bright/Presence combo = shimmering cleans
To achieve crunchy OD on clean channel, set Volume 7+, Master 7. Sounds really good overdriven. Don’t hesitate to turn up Treble & Presence to add bite & clarity to sound” - yek

“People think Bogners are dark, but really aren’t. Reason they seem that way is the pot tapers. Many assume knobs should be set around noon. If you do this on a Bogner, it’s like turning treble way down on a Marshall. Close your eyes & adjust tone controls by ear. Don’t be afraid to turn them way up/down.”

“Human nature is to put knobs near noon, reluctant to deviate much from noon. Amp designers exploit this & use different tapers to change sound of their amps WITH KNOBS AT NOON. Prime example is Bogner amps. Everyone says “Bogner amps are dark”. They’re not, but uses a Log10A taper for treble pot. It’s a standard Marshall tone stack. Usually a linear taper pot is used for treble. Treble 5.0 on Bogner is like treble at 1.0 on a Marshall. Treble 8-9, sounds a lot like Plexi”
Shiva’s cabs are 1x12, 2x10 or 2x12 with Classic 80s or V30s. There’s IRs of Austinbuddy’s 1x12 Shiva cabinet: 1x12 Shiver
“Bogners are notoriously slow due to low B+ voltage. Main reason I can’t get along with them. Can try increasing Variac”
“Most amps use linear taper for treble pot, Bogner uses log taper. Knob at noon on Bogner is like 8:00 on most others”

amps.name: SHIVER LEAD
body: Model: Bogner Shiva 20th Anniversary, Lead channel
