Notes: Gain channel features higher gain & slightly more refined type of gain. 2 push button switches added to further shape the tone. Shift button shifts the EQ. On, you’ll have a more Fender-like tone, less mids softer feel. One could say it’ll please the Dumble favoring players. Mode button on will give a more aggressive, in-your-face feel
See 251 Shiver for more info

Tips: Lead channel has Bogner’s trademark thick. Saturation only happens in upper area of Gain control, makes it a great amp for classic rock tones. Metal ain’t its thing. At default settings, channel is also dark, but works well in a band context. Turn up Treble & Presence to add bite & clarity to sound” – yek

amps.name: SOLO 100 CLEAN
body: Model: Soldano SLO-100, Normal channel, Clean mode

DynaCab: 4x12 Solo 100
Controls: Gain, Bass, Middle, Treble, Master, Presence, Bright switch (Normal channel only), Depth (with Depth mod)

Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “Cold-biased stages are where there’s a very large cathode resistor so operating point is close to supply rail. Ex, SLO 100 has 39K cathode resistor on cold-biased stage”

“Loudest amp I’ve ever used. Feels like there’s a small nuclear explosion going off inside when ever you hit a power chord. It’s a wicked amp, but not something I’d recommend for anything but large gigs. If you like SLO-100 model, may want to try Recto models too. It’s not widely known but Rectifier preamp is derivative of SLO- 100. Some minor changes, but basic topology’s identical”

“I have a really good SLO-100, original black faceplate version. That amp was one primary amp used in G2 modeling development. It was by studying that & really nice JCM-800 that I finally figured out the secret to cathode follower”
“Mine doesn’t have depth mod but I used values from schematic I have & I believe schematic’s correct”
“SLOs are brittle at low volumes. When cranked up, it smooths out. Problems is at cranked volumes, they can kill small animals”
“Key to SLO100 is run MV high so mids thicken up. Otherwise it’s a shrill mess. In certain contexts with right IR, can be a cool sound”
“Knobs on 5 on AxeFx correspond to 5 on real SLO, as they go to 11. Noon on real SLO isn’t actually knobs at half-way. Range of knobs is like 8:00-6:00 as opposed to 7:00-5:00, biased clockwise”

“Another caveat when comparing amps: many times knobs aren’t “centered”. If you put Treble knob at noon, it’s not actually at 50%. Can see this by turning knob all the way down/up. It may not be symmetrical. This happens when pots don’t have a flat spot &/ or pot is rotated within mounting hole, or in case of SLO100 it’s intentional. On SLO100, all the way down’s around 8:00 & all the way up is 6:00, so 50% is around 1:00, not noon”
“Many people find SLOs too bright, “Warren Haynes” mod is a popular mod to reduce brightness”
“Some amps designed to get character from power amp distortion. If you don’t push power amp, you just hear the preamp, voiced to be trebly. Power amp then compresses highs & sound gets fatter”
“If you want that Recto crunch but not so flubby/fizzy try SOLO models. They’re much brighter, so I turn treble down quite a bit”
“SLO100 isn’t a smooth amp, it’s nasty/aggressive. Gotta crank MV”
“A Recto isn’t a Mark series amp, totally different preamp topology”. “Rectifier preamp is almost identical to an SLO100”
“Pretty awesome amps. To sound great, crank ‘em. Ear-splitting loud”
SLO used by Gary Moore, Eric Clapton & Mark Knopfler

“100w Super Lead Overdrive sets not only the standard, but the benchmark, for high-gain tube amp OD & distortion. SLO’s tone, has made it the heart & soul of many of Rock, Metal & Electric Blues’ most creative & celebrated players. Because of this, you’ve been hearing the SLO 100 on your favorite records since 1987. From Clapton to Van Halen, Warren DeMartini to Lou Reed, SLO is the player’s choice

About Eric Clapton & Soldano: “The California custom amp builder Michael Soldano was commissioned to build 2 of his SLO-100 amps for Clapton in 1988. When interviewed in November 1988 for the Japanese Young Guitar magazine while on tour in Japan, Eric Clapton spoke about his newly acquired Soldano amps. Clapton commented he’d been using Fender Dual Showman amps when he heard Mark Knopfler at rehearsals & was impressed by his sound. He realized it was Knopfler’s amp rather than guitar that was responsible for the sound character. Clapton tried Knopfler’s amp & liking its sound he described as warm & round, immediately placed an order with Soldano. Clapton explained that although he was allowed to go on top of the waiting list, he waited 2 months before he received his amps because they’re handmade & not mass-produced. Clapton went on to say Soldano was the best amp for him, it’d be a classic & record his next album with one

Tips: There’s some popular mods. Depth mod increases depth for low- tuned guitars, emulate by increasing Depth in Amp block. The “Warren Hayes mod” prevents the amp from getting too bright & harsh at low gain levels. Emulate by disabling Bright switch in model.

“Secret of SLO is the arch enemy of most you guys, Volume. It has to be run hard with less preamp gain, it transforms into a gorgeous amp like that. It was designed as a large stage/stadium amp. Cue Warren Haynes. Running one at the local pub is going to give very thin & buzzy results. I would imagine the best tones from the Axe model will probably be achieved by increasing Master & backing off preamp, just like the real deal.” - Carol-Ann
Soldano cabs use 12” Eminence V12 spkrs. Try stock cabs: 4x12 Solo V12 (RW). Red Wirez cabs captured with neutral microphone, may wanna add mic model in Cab block

Notes: “Splawns sound weird bc tone stack is plate driven. Loses cathode follower compression & high source resistance of plate alters tone stack freq response. Plate driven tone stack in Splawn reduces highs considerably. Can see this running Tone Stack Calculator for Marshall tonestack & increase source resistance”
“OD2 switches in cathode bypass cap, increases gain of stage”
“Quick Rod is a bit odd in that Drive pot is linear taper. Most amps use audio taper pot, means that at noon, amp is nearly at full gain. Not sure why they did it that way but it is what it is”
“Signature Splawn tone, lotta bite, strong mids & 3 gear versatility”

Overdrive channel has 3 gears. 1st gear designed to sound like Plexi, 2nd gear like hot-rodded JCM 800 & 3rd gear like super hot-rodded JCM 800. All 3 gears are modeled (OD1 models). OD2 models are the same, with additional gain boost

DynaCab: 1x12 Hot Kitty
Controls: Bass, Middle, Treble, Gain (Drive), Drive (Master)

Notes: “Badgers are very different amps. If used to a hot-rodded Marshall tone, won’t be your thing, they’re edge-of-breakup amps. No negative fdbk, they’re loose, can be farty with certain pickups. Players amps, for guys relying on technique & riding volume knob”
“Badgers were Mimic’d with power scaling at full. Knobs are bit confusing Badger. Knob closest to input jack labeled Gain, Drive knob in AxeFx. Knob labeled Drive is Master Volume”

“Lowering Cathode Resistance reduces crossover distortion, it keeps amp in Class A operation longer, so amps biased kinda cold to begin with & get very cold when OD. A reason I like AC20 is it runs tubes hotter & exhibits less crossover distortion. Worst amp for this is Badger 18. Large cathode resistors & amp shifts into Class B early, has spitty, buzzy distortion, but is liked by many, goes to show some people like that, like fuzz pedals” - Cliff
“Badger doesn’t have a Bright switch” g18w amps are mostly used for crunchy tones at low volume levels
Capable of achieving clear cleans to saturated leads
Used by Pete Thorn, Guthrie Govan & others

“18w, all-tube amp has wide range of classic guitar sounds. Driven by cathode biased EL84 power section & features 5Y3GT tube rectifier, amp delivers a variety of warm, sparkling cleans & rich, dynamic OD tones”
Tips: “Master Volume is labeled Drive. Drive at 10 means your power amp is at full blast. This can make the bass muddy. Turn Drive down if muddy”
For max headroom turn up Master & keep Drive low, for OD tone lower Master & turn up Drive. Lower bass & turn up mids
For full-bodied clean tone, crank Master & use little gain
These amps/models, especially 18w, have lot of bass due to lack of negative feedback. Dial down Bass or use Cut switch in Amp block. Hi Cut control in model is non-functional

“Bass, Middle & Treble tone controls for each channel are post- gain & passive cut-style. Recommend starting around 5 & dialing to taste. As Gain/Drive increased, recommend dialing back Bass to retain clarity & definition in low-end”
Badgers have 1-2 WGS Veteran 30 spkrs, like a Celestion V30

Versatile amps, for clear cleans to saturated leads. 30w has more headroom & versatility vs Badger 18
See 270 for more Badger info/tips
Tips: Turn up Master & mids

amps.name: SUPER VERB NORMAL
body: Model: 1964 Fender Super Reverb
AB763, Normal Channel
Orig. Cab: Fender 4x10
DynaCab: 4x10 Bassguy RI
Controls: Vol, Bass, Treble, Bright
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Model based on amp with V1 removed (a common mod). This increases level into power amp by about factor of 2”

“Blackface Pro Reverb AA165, was almost identical to AB763 Super Reverb except for speakers & tone stacks. On Super Verb model, set Tone Stack type to Blackface you basically have Pro Reverb. Set Mid fairly high to emulate fixed 6.8K mid resistor, say ~7-8”

(Blackface) “Not a huge change with Silverface versions. They’re little cleaner & brighter but topology’s the same. Some minor circuit changes here & there, same transformers. Biggest change, IMO, was changing PI resistors to 47K, gives little less gain in PI”
“Really just 50W Twin Reverb with different speakers”
(Bright cap) “It’s only 120pF, so it’s subtle”
Big sound, lotta bass & treble, sought-after to achieve SRV sound

“AB763 Super Reverb’s a very popular Fender due to huge tone, robustness & pure tone. Blackfaces have more growl & distorted, cranked tone. Big sounding amp with potential for being very loud & bassy, given the right (wrong) speakers. Nature of 4×10” speaker config is a directional tone with screaming treble right forwards & backwards & unfortunately little spread. Lot of deep bass produced by the big cab, while mids are fairly low, a scooped tone. Many players are stupid & increase volume to compensate for lack of spread & mids, introduces even more bass & sharp treble. Playing a Super Reverb isn’t supposed to be easy, despite difficulty of this amp, there’s sometimes gigs where amp won’t hurt anyone in 3 meter radius. Turn the volume beyond 4, where amp comes alive. We remember these occasions as our happiest musical moments. Notes bounce off our strings & we’re musically & physically connected with the amp just using pick attack & guitar volume to roll back & switch from rhythm & solo. Maybe a transparent boost pedal to take us to David Gilmour land, with sustain, harmonics & creamy tone. Can physically feel dynamic response from 1 of the best vintage Fender amps, only if you tame the beast
The original modeled amp has been modded for more drive

“Super Reverb commonly used by blues guitarists for its loud, warm tube distortion through its 4 10” speakers. Also known for a scooped mids tonality, so there’s less midrange & emphasis of sparkly treble & full, round bass freqs”

Fender sold these amps with several speakers. 1st AA763 models in ‘64-65 had Jensen C10r. Then there were CTS ceramics & alnicos, as well as Oxford 10L6 in blackface AB763 amps. In silverface amps, Fender continued with Oxford & CTS, also used Utah, Rola & Eminence. This is a reason why 2 Super Reverbs can sound very different. In our opinion, Super Reverbs with Jensen & CTS are valuable, as both collectors & players’ amps today. These speakers perform very well & one doesn’t need to upgrade to modern, efficient speakers, like many other vintage speakers. CTS alnicos are also very popular speakers, even if they’re little efficient. During the years, Alnico magnets get weaker too. CTS Alnico require you to increase volume a lot & you’ll see players that dial in their amps at 6-7 on volume knob. With ceramic speakers, this is enormously loud. With vintage Alnico speakers, you’ll get more breakup from amp due to their low efficiency. On other hand, CTS ceramics are known for their power, efficiency & full low end. They also have lots of sparkle & treble & very good speakers in our opinion”

Tips: Try magic six settings, Volume 6, Bass 2, Middle 3, Treble 6 (6, 2x3, 6). NOTE: range of controls different on AxeFx, can’t copy exact settings to Axe’s. To translate settings, use table in intro of guide
“Modeled Super with V1 pulled as most do, so amp has more grunt. Turn down MV to make it cleaner”
“Model is Diaz Modded. IOW, I pulled V1, gives amp lot more gain so not surprised if getting breakup at ~1.5. Can un-mod model by turning down MV Trim to ~0.5”
Fractal models of Fender amps based on High input on amp. For Low input equivalent, set Input Trim to 0.5
Has 4x10” speakers, like a Bassman or Concert. Try stock cabs of 4x10 Super cab (4x10 Super)

Mid-heavy edgy & dirty amp, used by Jimmy Page on early Zep albums
Jimi Hendrix, Jimmy Page, Albert Lee & countless other stars used Supros to lay down foundations of blues & rock, but the roots of go back to the very birth of the electric guitar

Supro’s origins date to the pre-electric guitar days & formation of the National Resophonic guitar company around 1926, with roots in the resonator guitars that became a blues tone standard prior to amplification.

amps.name: SV BASS 1
body: Model: Ampeg SVT Bass Amp
Early model, prior to Ares 2.05

DynaCab: 8x10 SV Bass
Controls: Vol, Treble, Midrange, Bass, MV, Ultra-Hi switch (Bright)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7 (Syl)
Power tubes: 6550
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: James tonestack

Maybe the world’s most popular bass amp, SVT still is Ampeg’s flagship product. SVT stands for “Super Valve Tube” or “Super Vacuum Tube”, even Ampeg doesn’t know for sure. The size of a SVT rig (head & 8x10 cabinet) referred to it as “the fridge”

Don’t know which specific SVT amp was modeled by Fractal. A vintage one, or maybe current SVT-CL or SVT-VR version. Either way, SVT is a 300w rig, powered by 6 6550 tubes, with inputs for passive (0dB) & active (-15dB) instruments. Tone controls are flat at noon. Some models have Master control, Mid-Freq selector & Ultra Lo/Hi switches to emphasize low or high freqs
Tips: Ampeg SVT-CL settings suggestions (Guitar Chalk)
IRs of various Ampeg bass cabs as stock cab(s): 1x15 SV, 4x10 SV, 8x10 SV. Dr Bonkers also has IRs of Ampeg 8x10

AxeFX doesn’t model Master Volume tapers of real amp

“With Lonestar, there’s Gain, channel MVs & Overall output, analogous to Input Drive & MV. If you put MV at noon (Lonestar) & Input Drive at Noon, even at 100w there’s some edge & breakup, even with Output knob low (on amp). Also, can Bypass Output model & have MVs act independently, for true 2-channel independent MV by disengaging FX loop in back. This, IMO is accurate to how TX star clean models modeled in Axe”- RoshRoslin

Tips: “For me, on a clean amp model, if you get MV out of the sweet spot, it looses it’s mojo. Clean amps are about dialing in Input & getting power amp distortion from MV. Unlike a real amp, models can get really low input settings for desired gain, then adjust Amp block Level to desired volume. If more comfortable with mid-range input settings, lower input trim. This gives more clean range of input setting vs. being in 1.0 area of input” - barhrecords

“I crank Master & lower drive with this amp sim. Tutorial we did with AxeFxTutorials discussed clean sims & this amp. Try to get Gain close to breaking point & play lighter for mojo. Can always lower Drive & compensate with Volume knob” - ML Soundlab

Notes: Fredrik Thordendal is the guitar player of metal band Meshuggah. Not only known for djent-style metal, he’s also an accomplished fusion-type lead player.
Originally Fractal’s Thordendal models were custom models, based on Thordendal’s own specs. Then this happened:

“Old Recto models weren’t terribly accurate. When G3 came out, models were accurate but Fredrik contacted me & said something like “ahh, I loved the old Recto model”. So I took the old Recto models & made them “Thordendal” models”

amps.name: TREMOLO LUX
body: Model: Fender Tremolux g60’s Blackface, AA763
Orig. Cab: Fender 2x10
DynaCab: 2x12 Super 6G4
Controls: Vol, Bright, Treble, Bass
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7 Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: Smaller than Bandmaster, larger than Deluxe
Tremolux is smaller version of Bandmaster
“Blackface Fender Tremolux was produced from 1963-66 & had 1 circuit model, blackface Tremolux AA763. In terms of power & clean headroom it’s the smallest of all Fender piggy back amps

Blackface Tremolux & Bandmaster (AB763) are almost identical amps, but Tremolux is sized for smaller events & gigs. The Tremolux had tube rectifier, while blackface Bandmaster had diode. Both amps had tremolo but no reverb. Tremolux speaker cab was 2×10” & not 2×12”. Tremolux had smaller transformers (power & output) than Bassman & Bandmaster. All this adds up to an amp with less volume, less clean headroom & more sag/compression, a more forgiving tone. It feels very much like a blackface Bandmaster, normal channel of Vibrolux Reverb & Pro Reverb or normal channel of earliest blackface Bassman (AA864). Sound is nice & clean & tone remains relatively clean when one turns volume knob. One doesn’t reach sweet spot as early as with Reverb- amps with additional gain stage in preamp section. Tremolux uses only 1 12ax7 tube in preamp section in both channels before signal enters phase inverter. Tremolux is appreciated for its simple & basic circuit. A less complicated circuit means fewer things that can go wrong, less component drift issues, easier to service & has less potential for noise problems. Tremolux is rarer vs other blackface piggy back amps, since it was produced over a shorter period of time. It was discontinued in 1966. 2×10” closed cabinet that came with Tremolux is practically sized & performs well on most stages. Oxfords or CTS speakers were delivered with these cabs in blackface years. A 2×10” cab is easier to carry around vs 2×12” cab & with the right (wrong) speakers, can get almost as loud as the 12s just with less spread, lower bass & mids. Jumping channels in AA763 Tremolux can give a wider spectrum of tones than by using just 1 channel. You may for example use normal channel to deliver bass tones & vibrato channel for sparkling clean sound with bright switch on & 0 bass. This gives you a full & meaty tone you won’t get using just 1 channel. Tremolux came without a mid pot”
Was Fender’s 1st amp with built-in tremolo (vibrato). Has been described as larger version of Fender’s Tweed Deluxe
Fender released a re-issue around 2011: EC (Eric Clapton) Tremolux, with a G12-65 speaker
Head rated at 35w, has 2 6L6 power tubes. Model based on Vibrato channel, reverb not modeled

“Even though we already have an excellent collection of models of legendary Fender amps, Tremolux deserves your attention. It’s a great model IMHO, stays rather clean (with single coils) for the most part of the Volume control” – yek
Tips: Sounds great at stock settings
Tremolux provides 2 inputs per channel. Fractal models of Fender amps always based on High input on amp. To get equivalent of Low input, set Input Trim in amp block to 0.5
No Middle tone control, keep at 5 for authenticity
No Master Volume, keep Master control dimed
Tremolux was piggy-back amp: head & horizontal 2x10” cab

amps.name: TRIPLE CREST 3
body: Model: MESA Triple Crown TC-100, Channel 3 (Hi)

Notes: “Channel 3 delivers full-blown, yet controlled, mayhem with even more saturation & harmonic layering vs Channel 2. Makes it great for Heaviest Crunch Rhythm below 12:30 on GAIN control & singing liquid sustain reminiscent, but perhaps even more of the high gain voice MARK Series amps showcased above 12:30. Highest range on Channel 3’s GAIN control offers surely the most saturation & sustain available from any MESA amp, past or present” - Manual

“Just tried Triple Crest 3 for 1st time tonight & I found that, with minimal tweaking, model gave me a shocking range of tones that I love, from cleanest I’d want to most high gain, seemed like, unlike most other amps, I didn’t have to touch knobs to compensate freqs when adjusting gain, it was surreally easy to dial in for whatever I wanted” REPLY: “Just tried this amp on your recommendation & I’m a bit stunned. You’re right, huge range of sounds, no tweaking, a new favorite”

“Oh, 1 other thing about Triple Crown: since it’s so incredibly easy to dial in, it’s the best candidate for a gapless preset of 8 tones. Last night, I dialed in the highest gain tone I’d want to use as Scene 8, then assigned the Gain knob to a Scene Controller. Went to the Scene Controller screen & adjusted rest of the scenes descending the knob to taste, until it hit a nice not-quite-clean tone. All gapless & marvelous. You can of course go way more complicated with Scene Controllers, but I just did that as a test & it took maybe 3 minutes” - Forum thread
See 284 Triple Crest 2 for more info on this amp

amps.name: TUBE PRE

DynaCab: 1x12 G12T-100

Power tubes: 5881
Notes: “Vintage tone stack is a passive EQ model, has flat response if controls at noon. For active EQ, set type to Active”
Not a guitar amp, it’s a generic model of a 12AX7 preamp tube, neutral & low-gain, with power amp model added
Tips: Disable the power amp to use it as a preamp only
Can this model to warm up a source, such as vocals, acoustic guitar or synth, without much distortion
You can also use as a flat power amp-only model by keeping all tone controls default. Ex: using separate preamp with AxeFx
“If only needing power amp modeling, ex: when using external preamp through AxeFx, use this model. Uses Vintage tonestack which is flat when tone controls are at noon”

amps.name: TWO STONE J35
body: Model: Two-Rock Jet 35
Lead mode, PAB off
Orig. Cab: Two Stone 2x12 (G12-65)
DynaCab: 4x12 Rumble EV12L
Controls: Treble (pulled: Bright), Mid (pulled: boost), Bass (pulled: Deep), Gain (pulled: Bypass), Master (pulled: Lead mode), Lead Gain, Lead Master & Reverb
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6 (6v6 used in 22w version)
Tonestack location: PRE

Notes: “Very smooth but there’s a unique chirp or something I’ve never heard in an amp before. When you hit a note there’s a blast of high end that rapidly decays so it has almost vocal quality. Freq shaping’s very different vs most other amps”
“Two Rock is the most unique amp I’ve ever modeled. Almost like a violin but with this really cool chirp on pick attack”
“Even though J35 model’s supposed to be a D-style amp model, I’ve used it successfully for rock leads. It cuts through the mix nicely & it’s easy to generate musical feedback” – yek
Based on amp with Bypass engaged which cuts EQ section out of signal path, allowing more input signal to hit gain stage
Two-Rock is most famous for being amps used by John Mayer. Jet 35 is a Dumble-style 35w head.

“A peerless small venue or studio amp, designed to deliver killer Two-Rock tone at a lower volume vs any other series in the line. Has pull bright, mid & deep boost, plus bypass & lead switches available by a pull pot on front of amp“ - Two-Rock
Tips: Keep all tone controls default for authenticity
Two-Rocks cabs often have G12-65 speakers. Try 2x12 Double Amp stock cab, the IR of Two-Rock CR12, similar to G12-65

amps.name: TWO STONE J35 PAB
body: Model: Two-Rock Jet 35, Lead mode PAB on (tonestack byp)

amps.name: USA BASS 400 1
body: Model: MESA Bass 400 amp
Orig. Cab: Mesa bass cab
DynaCab: 1x15 Heart Key
Controls: Bass, Middle, Treble, Pull Bright, Bass Shift, 7-band GEQ, Master, Volume
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6550
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: Model’s Fat switch has same function as Amp’s Treble Shift
Original 400 often considered warmer & more vintage vs 400+

“Channel One: Input 1 & Volume l, offers high headroom preamp, you might find preferable for active type basses. Gain’s slightly lower than Channel 2 & tonal personality is somewhat different. Pull Bright switch built into Volume 1 can be great for funk bass, it accen­tuates harmonics above Treble control region & clear, distinct pitch (without boominess or mud)

Channel Two: Input 2 & Volume 2 recreate classic sounds of D180, predecessor to BASS 400. Gain’s slightly higher & may be better suited for traditional basses vs Channel 1. Both Channels tonal personalities are usable with very interesting differences
Tone Controls: BASS 400 uses traditional type tone controls, tailored for bass & ranges can be extended via Pull Shift switches

Treble control, when set high, becomes most powerful of B/M/T. Settings of 7+, it’ll minimize effect of Bass & Middle, but they’ll become the stronger controls when Treble’s below 5. Most players find “sweet spot” between 4-7, where tonal balance’s just right
Pulling out Treble Shift alters its freq center-point downward, towards upper mid-range & since there’s more musical energy at these freqs, amp will seem to get a little louder too
Using either Bright switch injects extra dose of high treble
Bass Control is wide ranging & power can be boosted via Pull Shift

BASS 400, especially when used with FAS speaker cabs, can have more true fundamental tone than just about anything else. Bottom end response is truly profound, yet avoids boominess/mud. Middle control is most subtle of B/M/T, purpose is filling in gap between treble & bass freqs. Spice according to your liking

Master: comes after Effects Loop & after optional Graphic EQ. Best use is as a sensitivity control for Effects Return &/or power amp section. Full undistorted power develops at settings above 5. There’s rarely any reason to run Master above 6 or 7, as higher settings merely increase noise without improving power or tone

Tips: “Has been my go to bass amp since I got the AFX. I couple it with 4x10 Aluminum cab (Factory #67), sometimes adding 1x15 or 8x10. I can tweak this setup to sound good in any mix. As for tips, good bass tone is “all about that midrange.” Concentrate less on sub 150hz range & pay attention to 150-1khz range. I’ve found 800hz to give my bass a barky sound that comes forward really well. When I used to play out live, that approach made me cut through 2 Marshall 100w half stacks & a LOUD drummer, with less than 250w amp & a 4x10 cab” REPLY: “I unfortunately have to disagree. 140Hz to 500Hz usually needs to be heavily subdued. 500Hz to 900Hz is the famous cardboard zone. If your bass is sounding flat, cut that zone. 80Hz to 140Hz is where bass lives in the mix. That’s the area that is very important to get right.” - Forum thread

Introduced guitarists to liquid, smooth lead tones & scooped high gain
Known for smooth high gain tones, loved by metal heads & fusion guitarists. John Petrucci of Dream Theater is probably most famous endorser of Mark series. Add Santana, Metallica, Keith Richards, etc

“Back when I was testing IIC+’s every day in the burn-in room, I always thought the non-graphic amps had a certain attack & purity to the sound that amps with Graphic EQ on them just didn’t have. There was urgency & bold punch to the sound, they seemed tighter & more cohesive. Granted, we made far more amps with on-board EQ than without, probably 70% had EQ, but not many people had an opportunity to compare day-in/day-out as I did. EQ model had the shaping advantage, no doubt about it & certainly all sounds IIC+ are famous for were created with EQ being an integral part of that sound, but when it came to the straight sound, no EQ, non-graph model always got to me with its speed & authority. So it was that I came to be the obsessive/compulsive owner of no less than 8 of them. I hand picked these as being the best sounding amps for me, out of 1000s of IIC+ we made. Out of these 8 amps, I immediately found my favorite, which I dubbed “His Highness the C-ness”, which I used as a reference model to have our Chief Tech & Archival Guru Michael Bendinelli, copy exactly on the other 7. Everything was measured, scrutinized (pot values, resistors, caps, transformers swapped, etc.), duplicated, & in the end, all 8 sounded alike. In our endless comparisons of many original IIC+’s, both EQ & non-EQ samples, alongside this golden reference, we discovered that I wasn’t just Tone-dreaming. There’s actually a difference between EQ model & those non-EQ models. It all came down to a coupling capacitor at the end of the EQ circuit that feeds the driver. In the EQ model, it was a great big cap that let a lot of sub-low pass, slowing down the sound & making it fatter. In my amp, a non-EQ version, this cap was smaller & didn’t let as much sub-low through, which speeds up the sound & makes everything tighter & more urgent. There it was, a simple part, but it made all the difference in the time domain. Yet there were so many more of these slower, fatter sounding EQ versions out in the world many more people were used to hearing as their reference. It wouldn’t do well to set MARK V permanently to this faster, tighter way. Too many players would have a tough time adjusting & when they compared the 2 amps side by side, the V would sound faster & tighter yes, but also stripped of sub-low & therefore maybe not as fat in comparison to their trusty IIC+ Graphic model or MARK IV, which also had the bigger coupling cap” – Douglas Wes
IIC+ amps don’t have as many features as later Mark IV & V models. Still, they aren’t easy to dial in

“I have a Mark IIC+, Mark IV, Mark V & Triaxis, they’re all completely different. Mesa always says stuff like “sounds the same as IIC+”, but circuits are different & knob tapers are completely different”
“Mark series graphic EQ sucks. I know some people want authenticity but it’s simply a bad design”
Tips: The IIC++ is easier to dial in (vs IIC+). Turn down mids & bass, compensate by using GEQ if necessary

“Mark series have lots of mids because tonestack is Pre. Can move to post, but will change feel. Pre position is what gives that singing lead tone & sustain. As with real amp, rule of thumb is set bass pretty low. Can add it back with EQ after. The kids all do the “V” EQ but I use very little. I happen to like lots of mids, it makes tone fit in mix better”
“Key to good Boogie sound, IMO, is Fat Switch. This is treble Pull Shift on IIC+ & Pull Fat on Mark IV. Mesa knew this & Lead 2 modes on Triaxis had treble shift engaged by default”

“Tone stack on a Boogie is pre-distortion. Increasing gain, want less bass into distortion stages, idea is to turn bass down. In later Boogie versions (like TriAxis) they changed taper & noon was equivalent to 1 on earlier models, as people are reticent to move controls far from noon”

Turn bright switch on. Every bit of info I’ve found says that people typically used Pull Bright on Volume knob. This is equivalent to Bright switch under Treble control on Block. I always turn it on & I’ve set it on by default for Q2.03.

“USE THE EQ. Tone stack is pre-distortion, atypical for a high- gain amp. Tone stacks almost always post-distortion. Since tone stack is pre-distortion, need to do post distortion tone shaping using EQ. Tone controls set feel & distortion texture, EQ shapes final tone. I like to do a gentle V-curve”
“With presence control at noon, it’s roughly neutral. Turn it down & you’re actually boosting bass & reducing treble”

“Pull Bright on Volume knob is classic Bright Cap, which engages a capacitor across the potentiometer. This is already modeled in the AxeFx via Bright Switch. Pull Bright on Lead Master knob engages a 0.22uF cap on cathode of last triode in the OD circuit. As there’s no switch for this in the AxeFx, it requires a separate model. Cathode caps are very common in tube amps. If value is large, stage has more gain & response is relatively flat. If value is small, stage has more gain at higher freqs. Amp designers use cathode caps to shape freq response. Caps in range of 0.1 to 1.0uF are commonly used to reduce bass response. A cathode cap works by decreasing amount of negative feedback through shunting higher freqs to ground, this reduced negative feedback, increases gain (& reduces linearity). FWIW the AxeFx is the only modeler I’m aware that actually models triodes using a feedback technique. Others use static waveshapers. AxeFx triode models incorporate feedback so if there’s a virtual cathode cap, stage is less linear in addition to having more gain”
“My settings for killer high-gain tone: Input Drive 8.1, Overdrive 9-10, Bass 1, Midrange 1.8, Treble 8.9, Presence 4.5, MV 4, Bright Switch on, 80 4.8, 240 2.6, 750 -4.5, 2200 -0.2, 6600 0”

“IIC+ doesn’t have gain boost & doesn’t need one, there’s plenty of gain. Pull Deep switch engages a large cathode cap on final triode stage. With Pull Deep off there’s actually a shelving response into power amp (bass is reduced)”
“To emulate Pull Shift on Bass control: “Put Tilt EQ before Amp block with freq of 320Hz & gain of -3dB. Set Level to +3dB”

amps.name: USA MK IV LEAD/RHYTHM
body: Model: MESA/Boogie Mark IV
Lead channel, Voicing: Harmonics
Orig. Cab: Mesa 1x12 (C90, V30)
DynaCab: 4x12 Recto Straight
Controls: Gain, Treble, Bass, Mid, Presence, Master, Graphic EQ, Pentode/Triode switch, Class-A/Simul-Class switch, Voicing switch (Mid Gain/Harmonics), Variac
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)

Power tubes: 6L6 (deliver most powerful clean sounds along with richest distortion). Can also use: g1. Type 5881 (like 6L6, but just slightly less powerful, somewhat rounder, more vintage tone) g2. EL34+6L6 (for those seeking traditional British edge to their OD sounds. In Simul-Class, combo of 6L6’s & EL34’s produce louder, fatter sound, while retaining British edge. Expect clean sound to suffer a bit when EL34s are used) g3. 6V6 (for old Fender Deluxes, when Tweed/Simul-Class selected)
Notes: famous for smooth high gain tones, endorsement by Petrucci

Lead channel, with Voicing set to Harmonics, & Pull Bright off. Use the FAT switch for Pull Fat. Presence Shift is available as a switch under Presence. This is the model you may want to select when playing loud

Like all MESA amps, Mark amps feature a LOT of knobs & switches. MESA states in the manual: “Every possible useful feature is included”. This can make them difficult to dial in for those who suffer from Option Anxiety, Parameter Paralysis or Agony of Choice. Also why modeling this amp requires a # of separate models
The Lead Mode is blistering high-gain focus

“Pull” controls are: 1.	 Rhythm 1: Bright 2.	Rhythm 2: Fat (more gain) & Shift Presence (higher frequency area) 3.	Lead: Fat (more gain), Bright (adds sizzle/gain) & Shift Presence (higher freqs) 4.	Lead channel also has Voicing Switch, where Mid Gain delivers more gain (meant for low volume levels) & Harmonics sounds more balanced (meant for high volume levels) & an additional gain control (Lead Drive)
SETTING THE LEAD MODE:

LEAD GAIN: This control sets up input sensitivity for LEAD mode. More signal strength dialed in here, early in preamp, the more distortion will be available at the LEAD DRIVE control. Yet it isn’t necessary or recommended to turn LEAD GAIN fully up. Best results occur with settings in 8 range. As you approach Gain of 7, Lead sound becomes extremely focused, rich & smooth. Above 8 , total saturation occurs, reducing effectiveness of tone controls, causing increased “microphonics” & noise in preamp tubes. You’ll notice subtle shifts in distortion tonality by trying different mixes of LEAD GAIN & LEAD DRIVE. LEAD GAIN control incorporates a PULL FAT for thickening sound & saturating signal further. This pull shift lowers freq of the Treble, adding a boost in lower midrange. Try this shift for beefing up sound of weaker, stock pick-ups or simply for adding focus at virtually any setting of the GAIN control

There’s also some great singing, slightly distorted sounds that come from low settings of Gain & Drive with FAT pushed in. “Onset of Clip” & “threshold of distortion” are phrases often used to describe these sounds, which reveal the true blue beauty of the all-tube lineage

LEAD TREBLE: Control, along with RHYTHM 2’s PRESENCE should be worked with together to produce desired gain & brightness of mode. Setting TREBLE higher, as always, increases gain of mode & allows increased distortion & sustain. Lower settings allow MID & BASS controls to become more dominant adding warmth to sound.

LEAD BASS & LEAD MID: Both these controls add body & warmth without really affecting strength of signal. Merely dial in to liking but note that too much Bass combined with too high a LEAD GAIN setting will take away from tight, highly focused lead sound that this mode offers

LEAD DRIVE: Works in conjunction with LEAD GAIN to determine amount of sustain & OD. It’s not necessary or even recommended to run LEAD DRIVE way up in order to generate a sound that’s fully saturated, so try settings in 5-7 region. Higher settings tend to increase noise & squealing without really embellishing the sound or feel. Notice this control comes on very rapidly at low settings, then changes only gradually throughout rest of its rotation. Familiarize with point which represents the beginning or “threshold” of OD, it creates an expressive distortion, that’s touch-sensitive to picking dynamics, providing harmonics that stay glued to the fundamental note. You’ll find lots of great sounds in 2-4 region

LEAD BRIGHT: Importance of switch is a must to understand. At low to medium playing volumes, it adds desirable sizzle & gain in highs to the OD sound & contributes to a “big easy feeling” too. At high volumes, can cause real problems, such as excess squealing & noise. At high volumes it’s often not necessary to supplement the LEAD mode’s natural gain because the power amp will also be going into OD. Keep in mind how Boogie’s cascading gain works: there’s several amp stages in a row with “gain” controls at all crucial points in between, so you can produce enormous amounts of amp gain at low volumes. MARK IV is capable of playing very loud as well, but it isn’t feasible to play with high gain & high volume simultaneously, without causing squealing, feedback & excess noise. A Ferrari is a great car in tight corners & it’s wickedly fast on straightaway, but at some combination of speed & cornering it’ll spin-out. So will MARK IV!

So remember to balance “gain” with “volume”. In LEAD mode, a general guideline might be push in BRIGHT for loud playing & pull out BRIGHT at lower volumes. 12AX7 tubes will be much happier & so will your soundman! (Another switch similarly “dangerous” at high gain/ high volume settings is LEAD VOICING switch, described later in this manual.)

LEAD PRESENCE & LEAD MASTER: Controls work similarly to those previously discussed. Note, adding lots of PRESENCE (which is extra gain in highest freq range) to high gain & high volume settings may aggravate spin-out of squealing & excess noise. LEAD PRESENCE control has a PULL SHIFT, which operates like R2 PRESENCE PULL SHIFT, reduces amount of low freqs & shifts range of Presence effect.

You may find that pulling out PRESENCE control gives better balance playing with a band, while pushing in PRESENCE has bigger & fatter sound for mic’ed up recording. When shift is pushed in & graphic EQ is set to “Classic V” setting, unholy amounts of tight, chunking bottom end are available. Give this setting a try for metal rhythm grooves.

GRAPHIC EQ: Setting up EQ is strictly a matter of personal taste & enjoyment, prepare to have some fun dialing in fine nuances of tonal shading, or going for outer extremes of weirdness! One setting recommended as a departure point is called “classic V”. It involves boosting lowest & highest freq sliders the most, boosting 240 & 2200 sliders somewhat less & cutting 750 center band

Can be as subtle or extreme as you want with this pattern & when you’re going for extremes, you’ll notice the 750 slider is by far the most crucial. Once you’ve boosted the other 4 bands, you’ll find that by adjusting only 750, you can determine the strength of the entire EQ effect. You’ll also notice that there’s a certain point where sound goes from max thrash to thin & buzzy. Your ears quickly become adjusted (addicted?) to this hyper-tone & when you switch EQ back out of the circuit, straight tone sounds to flat. Don’t worry! It’s just “hearing hangover”. All MARK IV’s modes were developed without using the EQ so that it could be an accessory, not a necessity.
OUTPUT LEVEL: Adjusts playing loudness of all 3 modes equally. Once you’ve got sounds set up as you like, using this control allows you to work louder or softer venues
Info above is from manual, may not correspond directly to AxeFx
Models with 2 gain controls (Input Drive & Overdrive), such as USA Lead, always feel elastic & spongy, a real joy to play.

“I have a Mark IIC+, Mark IV, Mark V & Triaxis, they’re all completely different. Mesa always says things like “sounds the same as IIC+”, but circuits are different & knob tapers are completely different”
“GEQ sucks. I know some want authenticity but it’s just a bad design”
Tips: Keep bass & mids low, turn up both drives & use the 5-band GEQ (pre power amp) to fine-tune

Most amps in Mark series feature a graphic EQ (GEQ), an important part of the magic. Regular tone controls are used to dial in “feel” & GEQ is used to shape tone. On many MESA amps, incl. Mark series, you’ll want to keep Bass really low to prevent flubbiness & then boost low freqs in the GEQ to compensate. That’s why many users set GEQ in a V-shape, mostly on Lead channel. In Mark IV models, make sure location of GEQ’s set to Pre Power Amp, to correctly simulate real amp

“Mark series have lots of mids because tonestack is Pre. Can move to post, but will change feel. Pre position is what gives that singing lead tone & sustain. As with real amp, rule of thumb is set bass pretty low. Can add it back with EQ after. The kids all do the “V” EQ but I use very little. I happen to like lots of mids, it makes tone fit in mix better”

“Tone stack on Boogie is pre-distortion. Increasing gain, you want less bass into distortion stages, idea is to turn bass down. In later Boogie versions (like TriAxis) they changed taper & noon was equivalent to 1 on earlier models, as people are reticent to move controls far from noon”

Turn bright switch on. Every bit of info I’ve found says that people typically used Pull Bright on Volume knob. This is equivalent to Bright switch under Treble control on Block. I always turn it on & I’ve set it on by default for Q2.03.

“USE THE EQ. Tone stack is pre-distortion, atypical for a high- gain amp. Tone stacks almost always post-distortion. Since tone stack is pre-distortion, need to do post distortion tone shaping using EQ. Tone controls set feel & distortion texture, EQ shapes final tone. I like to do a gentle V-curve”
“With presence control at noon, it’s roughly neutral. Turn it down & you’re actually boosting bass & reducing treble”

“Having owned multiple Mark IVs, multiple Triaxis & variety of other Boogie products, I can say the Mark series in general are a bitch to dial in. Once you figure it out though they’re great amps. IMO, key to MKIV is use TMB for feel & EQ for tone”
“Key to good Boogie sound, IMO, is the Fat Switch, Pull Fat on Mark IV. Mesa knew this, had treble shift engaged by default.”

Mark IV has built-in Variac Switch (Tweed Power), like the Lone Star. Can switch between Pentode (increases power/punch, clips harder. Use this for loud, live use, like un-mic’ed blues or country gigs) & Triode (softer/smoother, makes it easier to get a rich singing sound full of sustain & harmonics, but less headroom) mode. Amp runs Class-A (2 power tubes, 30-50w) or Simul-Class (simultaneous Class A & Class A/B, 70-85w)

“There’s no way to run power tubes in triode mode, but can change Power Tube Type to 300B which is a triode. When you run a pentode (or beam tetrode) in triode mode you connect the screen to anode, which effectively defeats the screen. This lowers output impedance of plate significantly, which lowers output impedance of amp itself, making voltage output less

amps.name: USA MK IV RHYTHM 1
body: Model: Rhythm 1 channel
Controls: Gain, Treble, Bass, Mid, Presence, Master, Pull Bright, GEQ, Pentode/Triode switch, Class-A/Simul-Class switch, Voicing switch (Mid Gain/Harmonics), Variac
Notes: Rhythm 1 feels bouncy & shimmers with freshness
Rhythm 1 channel, the clean tone, stays really clean for the most part of its Gain range. Use Bright switch for Pull Bright

RHYTHM 1 GAIN CONTROL: Setting RHYTHM 1 mode adjusts input sensitivity for clean RHYTHM mode. With most guitars, mode stays clean until ~8 where it begins to OD. Use R1 MASTER to control loudness if you want slight OD at low volumes. Mode carefully designed to provide great rhythm sounds, from best of vintage, Fender-type (clean or solo) to more modern, shimmering “funk rhythm” sound. Be sure to try this mode played really loud, you’ll love it for blues/rock

PULL BRIGHT: Adds shimmer & sparkle to sound when activated, the amount of added brightness varies with R1 GAIN setting: more brights at lower settings, less at higher. For super shimmer: GAIN ~3-4 & BRIGHT pulled out. For most naturally balanced sound: Run GAIN ~6-7 with BRIGHT pulled

RHYTHM 1 TREBLE: Control dials in predominate tonal characteristic. 0-3 range: Dark, warm jazz tone. Most common settings are in 5-7 range & note how overall gain of mode increases with higher TREBLE control settings, as so much energy of musical spectrum is channeled through this control. True of all modes, has 3 separate TREBLE controls

R1 PRESENCE: Adjusts freqs higher than those found on TREBLE control. There’s many different sounds RHYTHM 1 can produce, experiment using guitar & your ears. Try settings like low on TREBLE control, high on PRESENCE control & the opposite, as an experiment, both with & without BRIGHT switch. Notice too that when playing very loud, higher PRESENCE settings help cut through the background wash of cymbals. Don’t forget to turn RHYTHM 1 up loud & blow, it’s a great OD sound too

R1 MASTER: Adjusts signal level of RHYTHM 1 mode, allows running various settings of RHYTHM 1 GAIN, while still controlling overall RHYTHM 1 volume via MASTER. Important because of striking tonal differences BRIGHT switch makes at lower & higher settings of gain & because high GAIN control settings are useful for producing “onset of clip” distortion sounds

R1+R2 BASS & R1+R2 MID: Shared tone controls. Because of internal circuitry changes, which occur when switching modes, you’ll find R1’s optimum setting works well for RHYTHM 2. Effect of these 2 tone controls is largely determined by setting of TREBLE controls. Only when TREBLE is set very low (or off) are BASS & MID controls really strong, at normal TREBLE settings, their effect is greatly diminished & serve mainly to round out & fill up overall tone. Setting BASS control too high can result in unwanted flubbiness, especially in RHYTHM 2. Recommended setting for BASS control is 0-3 & for MID control 4-7
Info above is from manual, may not correspond directly to AxeFx
See 302 for more amp info, tips & settings

amps.name: USA MK IV RHYTHM 2
body: Model: Rhythm 2 channel
Controls: Gain, Treble, Bass, Mid, Presence, Master, Pull Fat, Presence Shift, GEQ, Pentode/Triode switch, Class-A/Simul-Class switch, Voicing switch (Mid Gain/Harmonics), Variac
Notes: Rhythm 2 channel. Use FAT switch for Pull Fat. Presence Shift available as switch under Presence.
Rhythm 2 covers that elusive middle ground, can produce an alternate Clean, purring Blues or grinding Crunch”

RHYTHM 2 GAIN & FAT: RHYTHM 2 GAIN sets overall characteristic of this mode, the most versatile of 3 modes. At high settings 9-10 & FAT switch pulled, RHYTHM 2 produces max gain for crunch & can double as alternate lead sound. This mode’s gain falls off quickly as knob is reduced, providing a range of lower-gain “onset of clip” sounds & ability to produce an alternate voicing for clean sounds at its lowest settings

PULL FAT has tremendous impact on both gain & voice of RHYTHM 2. When pushed in, RHYTHM 2 loses gain in the low mids & sound reveals more of guitar’s inherent personality. For clean playing or blues styles, may prefer FAT pushed in. Work with R2 TREBLE & PRESENCE to dial in traditional blues-style solo tones from RHYTHM 2. For hard rock & metal sounds, pull out FAT & max RHYTHM 2 GAIN, using RHYTHM 2 TREBLE & PRESENCE to dial in cut & brightness

RHYTHM 2 TREBLE: This along with RHYTHM 2’s PRESENCE control should be used together to produce desired gain & brightness of mode. Setting TREBLE higher, as always, increases gain of mode & allows increased distortion & sustain. Lower settings allow MID & BASS to become more dominant, adding warmth to sound

R2 PRESENCE: Control also works on higher freqs than TREBLE, so by working with both controls, can produce any sound from fat & thick to searing & shredding. Don’t be surprised if loud group playing dynamic compression & greater harmonic spread for the ultimate single-note fluid sustain but reduces clean headroom substantially. g90w position is full Simul-Class mode with all 4 6L6s combining the 2 different bias settings to deliver max power & headroom. It’s substantially sweeter/warmer & a little more scooped vs a standard 100w amp, yet has plenty of punch & authority without harshness. g45w turns off outer pair of 6L6s, so only middle 2 are running, the ones with lowered bias so, while they’re still Class AB, their Class A region is extended. In Channel 3, they can be switched to run in Triode configuration which cuts clean headroom roughly in half.

Combining these 2 opposite styles of wiring in 1 amp gives you the best of vintage & modern amp styles. Headroom & power are there when you need it, but there is always a naturally pleasing & musically curvaceous quality to the sound that’s magic to your ears & hands. Simul feels great & is inspiring to play! g10w reconfigures the whole set of 6L6s so that 2 nearest the 5U4 run pure Class A, single-ended, no longer push-pull, the ultimate low wattage output circuit that duplicates the essence of the best really old vintage circuits. Here, the 2nd harmonic (octave above note played) is NOT canceled out (as it is in push-pull circuits) & provides a magical halo surrounding notes. Onset of clip is so gradual, it’s hard to pin down transition from clean to overdriven.

BIAS SELECT switch allows you to run EL34 power tubes in place of the stock compliment of 6L6’s. EL34-style tubes produce a brighter, skinnier sound that will clip with a whole different harmonic content that can be nice, especially for recording where you may not need the Channel switching performance of all 3 Channels & can dedicate the amp to a specific sound. 6L6’s will most likely perform better for wide range of sounds & sound fatter/ richer on most all sounds & have the most clean headroom.

GAIN & TREBLE Controls are the most powerful tone shaping controls & should be used with taste. They determine much about the attack characteristic & overall personality of the sound in all Modes. Many of the great sounds in all Channels will find these 2 Controls in their middle ranges. Avoid setting TREBLE high (above 2:00) when GAIN is set high, as it has the tendency for a slightly microphonic tube to ring or squeal.

When using high GAIN settings, try EQ for adding extra top end, as it comes later in signal chain & less stressful on the preamp tubes. Remember, MkV is a high performance amp in every sense. Just like a highly tuned car, don’t need to drive it wide open to have fun. You can get amazing performance in the middle ranges of all controls.

PRESENCE is very powerful at shaping voicing of high gain sounds in Channels 3 (& even TWEED in Channel 1). Set low, it fattens & compresses the sound, for a more voice-like focused note. Higher settings “open up” the sound & allow the full spectrum of harmonics to flow through. Use to fine tune your lead sounds after you’ve dialed the rest of the preamp controls to your liking

Beware of setting BASS too high in Channels 3, especially when GAIN is set high. Too much BASS produces flubby, indistinct attack. In Mk1 Mode of Channel 2 it’s perfectly respectable & even recommended to run BASS control extremely low (8:30) or even off when GAIN is set high (above 1:30). Modes in Channel 3 can stand some more BASS, but watch it there as well. A basic rule might be this, as GAIN goes up, BASS should come down.

NORMAL switch mode sounds more round, rich & warm & is a pure solo voice. BRIGHT lifts the veil, allowing a whole spectrum of higher harmonics to soar through unimpeded, for that classic Boogie stacked harmonic spread.

TRIODE/PENTODE switch affects Channel 3 wiring of power tubes & sound. TRIODE softens attack & makes strings feel more liquid. PENTODE tightens attack, adds midrange punch & headroom requires higher PRESENCE settings to cut through, why we’ve provided 3 modes with separate Presence controls

RHYTHM 2 (& LEAD) PRESENCE control also has a PULL SHIFT, it normalizes the amount of high freqs produced in the power section. With PULL SHIFTS engaged, Presence controls is more effective & will act on a higher freq range. When SHIFTS are pushed in, a punchier, more aggressive tone is produced & PRESENCE control’s effectiveness is reduced.
R2 MASTER: Control is used to balance playing loudness
Info above from manual, may not correspond directly to AxeFx
See 302-306 for more amp info, tips & settings

amps.name: USA MK V GREEN
body: Model: MESA/Boogie Mark V, Channel 1, Clean
Orig. Cab: Mesa 1x12 (C90, V30)
DynaCab: 4x12 Recto Straight
Controls: Gain, Bass, Mid, Treble, Presence, Master, Normal/ Bold switch & EQ switch
Preamp tubes: 12AX7
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “MK V is a greatest hits of Mark IV & IIC+”

CHANNEL 1 has the lowest gain sounds of 3 Channels & can be thought of as Rhythm or Clean channel. Other 2 channels contain modes that work better for soft clip, medium crunch & high gain rhythm work. There’s a tight, skinny CLEAN, a warm, rich FAT clean sound with added lower fundamental & a higher gain pushed version of this architecture found in TWEED. BOLD voicing switch adds punch & cut starting in high mids to help stand out in a dense mix & more headroom

POWER: MULTI-WATT: Determines amount (wattage) & type (configuration) of power each preamp Channel will be coupled with to create a classic sound. Any amp’s sound is 50% preamp & 50% power, so this switch is responsible for a large part of a Channel’s personality & feel. Choices repeat for all 3 Channels & they’re 90W, 45, & 10W. Just select power wanted for each Channel, keep in mind the greatest headroom, focus & authority will always be found in 90w position

Since ‘81, Simul-Class Power has been the heart & soul of the MARK Series amps, imparting its sweet, musical character onto any sound. Cleans that shimmer with transparent layers of sweet delicate highs, bottom end breathes a warm fundamental under a bed of rich low mid ambience. Never harsh, always fat, Simul lead tone is quintessential single-note solo sound best described with a human trait vocal.
Patented way of wiring an output section combines 2 power tube pairs with different bias settings & operating parameters, arriving at a blend showing the best attributes of both.

In simplified terms, Simul-Class output comprises 2 dissimilar pairs of power tubes operating simultaneously. 1 pair operates more toward Class A for tone, other pair runs traditional Class AB to provide extra horsepower needed for clean headroom. Since 1980, Simul-Class has evolved & improved, providing more power, while still eliminating any trace of harshness.

In the MkII-C+, Class A pair was permanently wired in Triode configuration & in MkV, it can still be achieved (Channel 3 only). By reducing the tubes’ power sensitivity, Triode wiring pushes the pair even further into Class A. This results in more

amps.name: USA MK V RED IIC+
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Channel 3, IIC+ mode, Bright off
Controls: Gain, Bass, Mid, Treble, Presence, Master, Normal/ Bright, EQ switch

Notes: CHANNEL 3 encompasses the best of the MARK Series OD sounds. Beginning with MKIIC+, the sound is pure, blistering focus & the classic Boogie voice of the 80’s, that became the foundation for players like John Petrucci, James Hetfield, Kirk Hammett & a staple for L.A.’s first call session players of the day Steve Lukather, Mike Landau, Dean Parks & many more. Classic “V” curve on Graphic EQ became an integral part of this sound & went on to be the sound of choice for some of the hardest hitting Punk & Hardcore bands of the 90’s/2000’s & still currently used by many top acts. Channel 3 is a faithful recreation of that burning sound of the IIC. MkIV is represented in FAT, where a dose of thick midrange gain is added, combined with a rounding out of upper harmonic peak. Finally EXTREME unleashes all aggression & attack of circuit for a full gain assault that cuts through any mix. (Beware of volume jumps when EXTREME is selected.) NORMAL/ BRIGHT switch affects all Modes in CHANNEL 3

While MkV Tone Controls are accurate & effective over a wide range, they come earlier in the signal chain & can only achieve so much shaping before producing possible imbalances in certain Modes. (Ex: Bass Control in Channels 3, where in MkIIC Modes, too much bass dialed early in preamp has a tubby & unfocused sound

The Graphic EQ is at the end of the signal chain, so you can shape the final sound with the only consideration being how much of a certain freq speakers, cabs & your ears can handle. (Extreme settings of 2 lowest Bands, 80Hz & 240Hz use up power quickly & overall headroom is affected)

Channel 3: Overall highest gain in amp & dedicated to creating the best high gain sounds from the fabled MkIIC+ & MkIV. Though the entire channel is aimed at high gain sounds, many expressive/dynamic medium to low-gain sounds can be found here with the GAIN control set lower (below 12:30). Again, this channel defies classification to style & is extremely versatile for both low/high gain, chording & single note sounds.

MkIIC+ is exactly that. This mode is faithful, down to the last detail, recreation of the LEAD Mode of this legendary circuit. Blistering focused gain & explosive attack gives way to morphing harmonics, are it’s soulful signature & after 20 years, these sounds remain at the forefront of Rock. Tight mid-punch focus of this sound provides an open canvas for coloring with the EQ & classic “V” setting is a staple ingredient of the II-C high gain sound for heavy music.

MkIV is a faithful re-creation of the MkIV’s Lead Channel. Because of the vast options in configuring this channel on the MkIV, combined with the fact that the MkIIC+ Mode covers a wide range of brighter sounds, we’ve configured this Mode for a slightly warmer sound. MkIV mode has a big increase in midrange gain, creates the impression of a roll-off in the upper harmonics, resulting in a huge, wide sound, the midrange is voiced higher & stays tighter. Because of this, the EQ does the job of shaping this sound into whatever you want. The EQ doesn’t have to compete with the increased low end early in the preamp of the MkI Mode & can dial in some incredibly big sounds that won’t get flubby.

EXTREME is exactly what it sounds like, extreme & takes it’s architecture from the MkIV, but voiced almost opposite the MkIV mode in this Channel. Forward, aggressive, tight & fast are it’s signature & it is the highest gain/loudest of the MkV’s 9 Modes. Use this mode for crushing impact & stop-on-a-dime dynamics. EXTREME is most suited for heavy styles where max gain & lightning fast attack are the order of the day. Increased sensitivity of the power section in this Mode causes more background noise, as the power amp & driver are “listening harder”. This is normal.

CHANNEL 3 BRIGHT/NORMAL: 2-position switch determines amount of upper harmonics in the sound of all modes of the channel. Some sounds bring forward the action of this switch more than others, depends on amount of top end of the sound to begin with, but it adds or reduces brightness at its location in the signal path in all Modes of this Channel. Ex: you’ll hear its action more in MkIIC+ Mode vs MkIV (FAT) Mode, as the high freqs are more rolled off & recessive in MkIV Mode & therefore there’s less of these freqs present for the BRIGHT switch to bring forward.

In BRIGHT position, sound is infused with upper harmonic halo around note(s) & added top end is more apparent as GAIN is increased, especially in MkIIC+ & EXTREME Modes, where BRIGHT circuit has more highs to work with. BRIGHT position is great for adding harmonic grind to high gain chording sounds in these 2 modes, lets these highs pass at a specific earlier stage in the preamp, the PRESENCE & EQ, can use to blend & shape them later.

In NORMAL position, this region of upper harmonics is rolled off, for a darker, warmer blend that works well for single note soloing. Position is great for adding a feeling of girth & fatness in low gain sounds & removes unwanted sizzle/buzz that can result from low output pickups in high gain settings

The IIC+ mode in Channel 3 uses a smaller coupling cap to deliver the tightest attack & fastest response in the time domain. You also get the bonus of having this mode work incredibly well with the EQ, as the lack of sub-low at the end of the preamp means you can add more low freqs with EQ before the sound gets flubby. So all you IIC & MkIV guys might like this characteristic even better. You can get high gain sounds with the EQ even tighter than ever before

The MkIV & EXTREME Modes utilize a bigger coupling cap, to add sub-low & slow down the attack, for a bigger/fuller slowhand feel that’s huge. Be a little more careful with 80 & 240Hz Sliders when dialing in low end with the EQ, as more low end happens earlier, but

amps.name: USA MK V RED IIC+ Bright
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Channel 3 in MK II C+ mode, Bright on
Notes: See 308 & 309 for more info, tips & settings for this amp

amps.name: USA MK V RED MKIV
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Channel 3 in MK IV mode, Bright off
Notes: See 308 & 309 for more info, tips & settings for this amp

amps.name: USA MK V RED MKIV Bright
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Channel 3 in MK IV mode, Bright on
Notes: See 308 & 309 for more info, tips & settings for this amp

amps.name: USA MK V RED XT
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Channel 3 in Extreme mode, Bright off

Notes: EXTREME Mode radically re-voices negative feedback in the power section, among its many changes & inherently has much more of this upper harmonic region vs any other Mode. This increase in top- end cut & aggression renders the PRESENCE control somewhat less active vs other Modes, as there’s so much bite already in EXTREME.
See 308 & 309 for more info, tips & settings for this amp

amps.name: USA MK V RED XT Bright
body: Model: MESA/Boogie MkV, Chnl 3, Extreme mode, Bright on
Notes: See 308 & 309 for more info, tips & settings for this amp
