amps.name: USA PRE CLEAN
body: Model: MESA TriAxis preamp, Rhythm Green
DynaCab: 2x12 Lead 80
Controls: Drive, Treble, Middle, Bass, Preamp Presence, P.A. Presence, Dynamic Voice (GEQ)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location:
Notes: “8 separate modes deliver elusive creamy gain of MkI, focus of MkIIC+, scooped Rhythm of MkIV, & modified British lead mode”
(Rhythm Green) With very high GAIN values, may need to reduce MASTER a lot. Its Dynamic response makes it the hottest, output-wise

“More vintage, old Blackface style circuit. A beyond- vintage clean sound, with bubbly, elastic highs & big breathy lows. This mode shines at many other settings too. As GAIN & MIDRANGE controls are increased, 5.5-7.5, a whole new flavor appears. This hotter region performs as a killer driving, yet still clean, rhythm sound. Bold & pushed, this dynamic range of tones works great for Blues rhythm grooves or more aggressive “alternative” clean parts or lines. This is probably the most powerful & widest of possible rhythm sounds aboard TriAxis, perfect for when it’s your turn in the spotlight. Go ahead. Crank it! Rhythm Green loves to rock.

This is the sound that helped put Boogies in the hands of widely acclaimed guitar heroes of the 70’s/early 80’s, when Blues/Fusion changed & revoiced rock with tasty medium gain sounds. This circuit was inspired by the classic 4x10 Fender Bassman & later redefined in Mark 1 Boogies. Classic, bare-bones, roots players will love how well this setting responds to pickup output. Virtuosos of the volume knob can take or leave foot switching in favor of a circuit that lets them ride gain from their guitar. Rhythm Green works extremely well for this & will surprise even the most hardcore vintage heads.

GAIN control has a Dynamic “Bright” circuit built into it. At low Gain settings, upper harmonics pass freely through this control, producing the sweetest, brightest sounds. The more GAIN is increased, the less of these upper harmonics pass through & warmer the sound becomes. GAIN all the way up virtually removes these freqs from the mix & may want to use PRESENCE to put some of them back. Use MIDRANGE with BASS to balance warmth with upper harmonics at low Gain settings & reduce PRESENCE until you achieve desired blend”
For a long time, Petrucci relied on a couple TriAxis units to get his favorite MkIIC+ tones on the road, as did Metallica
“I used a Triaxis for over a decade before designing the AxeFx. I have 2, I’d say I’m pretty familiar with the tones & to my ears (& measurement equipment), the Axe models are spot-on”
“Mesa claims it’s based on IIC+, but it’s different. Bright cap is different, mid resistor is much greater, source impedance of drive “pot” is different, etc”

“On our reference Triaxis (both of them), Presence control does nothing for the Clean & LD1 modes. Model’s Presence knob is therefore the Power Amp Presence” & “When using the LD1 modes the Presence control doesn’t do anything & is as if the control were at 10”
In Cygnus amp modeling firmware, Lead models have an authentic Preamp Presence control (not in Lead 1 model).
“Triaxis used LDRs as variable resistance elements. There were resistor strings that were used to set the LDRs but those resistors weren’t in the signal path”
“Circuit didn’t change in new ones, they just changed the taper of the bass pot, Triaxis is like this too. With control at noon it’s like the old ones with control at 9:00 or so”
“Mark series graphic EQ sucks. I know some people want authenticity but it’s simply a bad design”

Presence control: “This usually regulates either brightness or negative feedback in the power section of amp. In the TriAxis a whole new approach to this circuit was taken. Actual dynamic feedback loop, compresses highs & upper treble freqs makes control 1st of its kind.

As PRESENCE is increased, more highs are allowed to pass. The lower the signal strength at input of this, the more highs pass through it. The lower region of PRESENCE dampens these high harmonics. Greater the signal strength at circuit input becomes, the darker the sound becomes. So, if you set PRESENCE high & pick softly (sending a small signal through PRESENCE circuit) highs will be very prominent in the mix.

If you decrease PRESENCE & pick harder (increasing signal strength at PRESENCE circuit input), high harmonics will be very subtle. In other words, it helps enhance whichever direction you’re trying to go in with your sound & does so dynamically! Most players love the way tube circuits react to subtle nuances in style, This control circuit takes that idea a few steps further. When you’re “going off” on the treble strings in a solo,  you go for an expressive bend & lay hard into the note, TriAxis works with you! If you picked the note hard with PRESENCE set low, that note becomes bigger, rounder & more compressed.

Likewise, if you’re doing some low growling work on the bass strings & PRESENCE was set high, you could relax into this segment & let the control make the edge. Most everyone we know wishes their high notes could be fatter, low notes more discernible & articulate, highs where you need them, lack of highs where you don’t.  Experience the way this dynamic control can enhance your playing, especially when soloing”
“It’s just speculating but the Presence circuit in the TriAxis may have been the inspiration for Fractal to develop the Dynamic Presence & Dynamic Depth parameters” - yek

“Added the “Bright” control to the Amp block. This high treble control is a shelving filter between the preamp & power amp & may be used to darken or brighten the output of the preamp. This control also accurately replicates the “Presence” control found in the Mesa Triaxis preamp when set to negative values (Presence control in Triaxis is actually a high freq cut shelving filter)”
Tips: “Can nail sound of my Triaxis by setting Bright ~9:00-10:00”

“10 on Triaxis would be 0dB on the Bright parameter. Anything below 10 is equivalent to less than noon on the Bright. Presence control on a Triaxis is a hi-cut, it never boosts. Bright isn’t an exact match to the Triaxis Presence control though, it’s a fixed shelving filter. Triaxis’ Presence control is passive, so center freq changes with amount of cut. It also changes the load on the plate, which distorts the freq response a bit too. Presence should be set to 5.00, which is neutral”
The TriAxis was the reason for Fractal adding a Bright knob (not Bright switch) to the amp block. It represents the Presence parameter in the TriAxis, leave Presence in the amp block default.

TriAxis features Dynamic Voice, a preset EQ curve & isn’t modeled. Can use the graphic EQ in the Amp block to simulate the 5-band “pre-power amp” graphic EQ, present on Mark amps. “I gigged with a Triaxis for years. I never put Dynamic Voice above 1.0. Despite it’s name, it’s just an EQ. It’s the 5-band graphic EQ from the Mark series, with potentiometers replaced by LDRs. It’s really just a bunch of EQ presets & interpolates between them as you increase the value”

“I gigged a Triaxis for years & I’d have the knobs pretty much at noon (5.0). Triaxis is a MkIV preamp under the hood, but with very different tapers on the “pots”. I.e., with B/M/T at 5.0 on Triaxis, MKIV is something like 1.0, 6.0, 6.0. I’m just going by memory here but the point is that the bass control on the Triaxis doesn’t need turned way down like on a Mark series”
“Mark series have lots of mids because tonestack is Pre. Can move to post, but will change feel. Pre position is what gives that singing lead tone & sustain. As with real amp, rule of thumb is

amps.name: USA PRE LD1 RED
body: Model: MESA TriAxis preamp, Lead 1 Red (TX-4 board)
Based on Lead 1 Red mode with the TX-4 board
Controls: Drive, Lead Drive (Overdrive), Treble, Middle, Bass, Preamp Presence, Dynamic Voice (GEQ)

Notes: “...Pre-tone-control gain circuits. This means boosting gain by progressively slamming the 12AX7 tubes occurs before the tone controls. This method of squeezing gain through the tone controls tends to lend itself to a larger, yet slightly less focused, sound. Many rely on this looser, more spread out sound to be able to get emotional with their soloing style. This circuit also delivers large amounts of bass very well. As opposed to a “rear-end” style circuit, this circuit type lets you pump larger quantities of bass through the tone controls, without increased flub/cloudiness. Players wanting extremely high gain metal or hard rock sounds find LEAD 1 much more suited to the maxed out gain regions needed to produce over the top, believable crunch & grind.
See 315 USA Pre Clean for more amp info, tips & settings

Tips: We especially recommend Lead1 Red for this application. This aggressive mode shares much of the circuitry with both Lead 1 Green & Yellow, then adds extra focus & punch in the upper mids. This mid- forward voicing lends a more urgent, tight character to the mix, perfect for showcasing Lead 1’s heavier side. Because of this added midrange, the Red mode slices through a mix in the sonic region where rock snare lives, especially great for crunch rhythm in classic or modern gain realms. Another attribute of this EQ’ing is that higher settings of Bass remain tighter & track better at higher gain settings vs the other Lead 1 modes. This additional bump in the mid freqs also allows more Treble to be dialed in, which increases gain without sounding thin. Try setting GAIN 8.0, BASS 6.5, TREBLE 7.0 & LEAD 1 DRIVE 6.0 to audition this”

amps.name: USA PRE LD2 GREEN
body: Model: MESA TriAxis preamp, Lead 2 Green
Controls: Drive, Lead Drive (Overdrive), Treble, Middle, Bass, Preamp Presence, P.A. Presence, Presence Shift, Dynamic Voice (GEQ)

Notes: “...Post-tone control gain circuit. With heralded veterans of this design, the MkIIB, IIC+, MkIII & MkIV, the rear-end lead legacy lives on, stronger than ever! It’s in LEAD 2 we celebrate these classic lead sounds & offer up a couple variations on the theme. Again, these are the more articulate & focused lead sounds. Some players that grew up playing non-MESA amps may find these dry & stiff initially, but have patience & you’ll come to enjoy these modes. There’s simply less “slop around note” in these modes, making them a sorta magnifying glass for inaccurate playing. Though at times they’re more revealing, you’ll find they hold up better in a mix & are much more behaved on a stage full of live mics. Usually they’re also much better for any kind of rhythmic high gain playing where you need urgent attack & tight cut-off points

The Green circuit’s taken directly from a MkIV Lead channel. Green is focused gain, uses the concept of the MkIV’s Mid Gain, a feature added in ‘89, to enhance attack & lower medium midrange “meat” of the sound. Enhancing this part of the spectrum, a rich, bold, yet singing quality is produced. Thick is the best way to describe its character, while Punchy accurately describes its attack. It does saturate the note fairly completely, especially at high gain settings, but the attack envelope is so right at lower gain settings, that roots players usually love this mode. Its’ thicker midrange punch really helps melody lines played on high strings, particularly high on the neck. With higher Gain & Drive settings, high notes soar & sing, yet don’t get too soupy to be heard in a big mix. Green is especially helpful in getting rid of unwanted fret buzz or other annoying idiosyncrasies of an instrument that may be set up wrong. Tends to cover up buzz leaving just the note with greater purity. This is most apparent when a weak single coil is used for soloing, the cure for this dilemma. It adds the needed freqs & gain is the steroid habit these weaklings need to deliver a bold lead voice. Mode of choice anytime needing to deliver a statement & don’t have the luxury of several tracks. It’s single note authority at its expressive best.

Tips: As mentioned earlier in Lead 1 Red, GAIN setting in all modes is crucial to achieving the sound you’re looking for. Each mode has its’ magical optimum setting for this control, we can only give our view & a few factory settings to demonstrate our thinking on this. Ultimately, it varies for each mode, guitar, player, environment & on you to discover what fits a situation. In Lead 2. we’ve come to a simple rule of thumb to where we prefer GAIN to achieve the best blend of attack & tone quality. Try this 1st, then deviate from there in all Lead 2 modes. We like to see Gain at 7.0-7.5 (depends on instruments output) for lower gain blues sounds or very articulate medium gain solo sounds. When higher gain or straight up radical rock sound is in demand, GAIN 7.5-8.0 (again depends on pickup output) should be more than enough

With higher than recommended Gain settings, a flubby, indistinct attack occurs that the BASS control has difficulty removing. Lower than optimum Gain settings produces excessive high harmonic content, thins notes out & even add a buzzy quality to the sound”

amps.name: USA PRE LD2 RED
body: Model: MESA TriAxis preamp, Lead 2 Red

Notes: “This mode is made for shred. In fact, it may be better named LEAD 2 Shred. It’s much more aggressive in top end vs its’ yellow counterpart, boasting much enhanced upper harmonics. Though it shares almost identical basic architecture, additional parts switch in with Red that give this once balanced, well behaved mode a downright ugly attitude. Harmonics are boosted & a bit of lower treble is dipped to give this Red a sizzling edge unique to this mode only. The harmonic edge not only benefits high notes, it does wonders for the grinding “Z’s” needed for bodacious low end crunch. This enhances the growl on the low strings & adds cut/sizzle to higher strings. The freqs enhanced by this circuit are slightly higher than those found in conventional power section PRESENCE controls. They’re also higher, more rebellious & defined than those adjusted by the action of TriAxis’ PRESENCE. These highs have that out-of-control-vibe to them, that falling- apart-yet-loosely-held-together quality that’s often associated with modded early British heads, using Euro- style EL34 power tubes for their ponies. This elusive sound affects string feel & players accustomed to such sounds have difficulty feeling at home on an amp that doesn’t deliver these loose highs.”
See 315 USA Pre Clean for more amp info, tips & settings

amps.name: USA PRE LD2 YELLOW
body: Model: MESA TriAxis preamp, Lead 2 Yellow

Notes: “This is Boogie. This mode is the sound that started the craze that became what is slanged as the “California” sound or the “LA” guitar tone. Lukather, Landau, Keaggy, Lynch, Gillis, Prince & Metallica catapulted this sound into the forefront of hit making guitar sounds throughout the 80s. Metallica continues to search high & low for pristine C+ Heads to add to their amp collection, deeming them essential for recording, but you don’t have to! Yellow mode is the reincarnation of the fabled Mark IIC+ Lead mode. Its blend of bold punch & evenly stacked liquid harmonics produces a lead voice that transforms any player who spends enough time to let it, into a virtuoso of feeling, soul & statement. It growls with ferocity in lows, staying tight & urgent. A thonk & chirp is experienced traveling through the mids. Then suddenly, as if someone redialed for the highs, an explosive, yet liquid, top end comes ripping out as you squeeze every luscious note from the treble strings. Sound almost sexual? Primal? Well many a IIC+ junkie will confess, broken hearts are often cured from a couple weeks rocking with one. Seriously, the blend of this mode is so amazing most players are finding new great sounds 2 & 3 years after their 1st ear to ear grin.

While Yellow mode excels at medium to high gain settings, because of these nicely stacked harmonics, don’t underestimate it for rootsier low gain sounds. Sure, as you can make high gain sounds chirp & squeal on command, low gain sounds possess the sting essential to tortured blues soloing. Yellow doesn’t get as saturated as Green in LEAD 2, making it the choice for skinnier, more cutting & clean blues lines. PRESENCE control works extremely well in Yellow mode, dialing in & out harmonic content of a given preset. At low PRESENCE & TREBLE settings, the sound is more horn or voice like. With higher settings of Presence & possibly Treble, the character becomes much more searing & harmonic”
Tips: “LD2 Yellow is my absolute favorite. It’s the IIC+ mode. It has Pull Shift & Pull Bright enabled by default. I find this model easier to dial in than the IIC+ models & it sounds awesome.” – yek

amps.name: VIBRA-KING
body: Model: Custom Fender Vibro-King
Orig. Cab: Fender 3x10
DynaCab: 4x10 Bassguy RI
Controls: Volume, Treble, Bass, Middle, Fat switch, Tremolo effect
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: POST
Notes: “Model based on the custom version, which has higher plate voltages, which causes more OD on the power tubes”

“Fender introduced the Vibro-King in 1993 to great acclaim, marking a return to a great hand-wired amp tradition, while including a previously unavailable selection of sought-after features. In the decades since, guitarists & amp aficionados everywhere have prized the enduringly classic Vibro-King as one of the most touch-sensitive amps ever, with sparkling-clean shimmer at lower volumes & powerfully thick overdrive when cranked up” – Fender
Called the “King of Fender amps” by some, it’s a modern take on classic blackface Fender tones. Vibro-King players include Gary Clark Jr., Eric Clapton, Pete Townsend & Robert Cray.
Tips: Has 3x10” speakers. Try stock cab: 3x10 Vibrato
Amp has 2 inputs. Fractal models always based on amp’s High input. For Low input equivalent, set Input Trim to 0.5
FAT switches amp from clean into a cranked mode, for soloing. This model has FAT off & there’s a model of this amp with FAT engaged. Some players only play with FAT on.

“In ‘93, amp came with vintage style blue Eminence Alnicos, after that Jensen P10r, followed by different Celestion speakers. Changing speakers is an easy move for Fender to attract new & old customers again, by altering the tone a little bit & calling the new model “Anniversary”, etc. We find the original blue frame Eminence give more of a brownface tone, while modern & louder speakers, like Weber 10a125/f125 points you into a more scooped blackface area. We’ve found some of the best modern Jimmie Vaughan & Robert Cray tones with blue frame Eminence Alnicos (maybe also with Jensen P10r). Tone is very direct, snappy, responsive & you’ll hear all the trebly details from your fingers & strings. This tone’s not for beginners, but for those with a serious attitude for tone. If into more rock’n’roll, or fat/chunky clean sound, you should go for other speakers with more low end”
“Players seem to either love or hate the Vibro-King. If you’re not into reverb or tremolo, steer away from this amp. 56% of the controls

amps.name: VIBRA-KING FAT
body: Model: Custom Fender Vibro-King, Fat switch: on
Notes: See 320 Vibra-King for more info on this amp

amps.name: VIBRATO LUX
body: Model: Fender Vibrolux g‘62/63 Brownface 6G11 model
Orig. Cab: Fender 2x10 or 1x12
DynaCab: 1x12 Vibrato Lux
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE

Notes: Vibrolux is famous because Mark Knopfler used it to record the Sultans of Swing album. Rory Gallagher also played one & “Early Dire Straits tone is a Vibrolux. The Vibrato Lux model is based on the same model/year amp used on the eponymous album. There’s a cab in there too”  & “Our reference amp is the same model Mark Knopfler used on the Dire Straits eponymous album. Match made in heaven with a Strat” & “It’s a Vibrolux. The IR is from my personal 1962 Vibrolux, the amp used on Dire Straits eponymous album.”
“Closest to a Brownface Deluxe would be Vibrolux model. Tonestack is different though, try changing to Vintage”

“Brown” refers to period between Tweed & blackface models, ’59-63. Brown Fenders are typically cleaner vs Tweed versions, but warmer/dirtier vs blackface models. Like Tweed amps, brown models are very collectible
The Bright channel has been modeled (no Bright switch)
Fractal models of Fender amps always based on High input on amp. For Low input equivalent: set Input Trim to 0.5
Tips: “Turn down Bass some, Treble a bit & that’s it, great vintage Fender tone” - Yek
Vibrolux was available as 1x12 combo, 1x10 or 2x10” . Try stocks cabs: 1x12 Brown, 1x12 Vibrato Lux, 2x10 Vibrato Lux

amps.name: VIBRATO VERB
body: Model: Fender Vibroverb, old model
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x15
(Jensen C15N, JBL D130, Eminence)
DynaCab: 1x15 Vibratoverb
Controls: Volume, Bass, Treble, Bright switch, Tremolo effect
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: SRV’s favorite
Old model ported from the Axe-Fx Ultra
Vibroverb is one of the most sought-after vintage Fender amps, mainly due to SRV having 2 & Mark Knopfler played a ’63 brownface model

Due to low production numbers & their use by Stevie Ray Vaughan, 1964 (blackface) Vibroverbs have become highly collectible. SRV acquired 2 1964 blackface Vibroverbs in the late 70s, or early 80s, at 2 different times & locations, that somehow had sequential serial numbers. They were also modified by Cesar Diaz in many ways”

“SRV’s musical influence made the Fender Vibroverb the most sought after & hyped of all Fender amps. He played many other amps too, Super Reverb, Dumble & Marshalls. In studio, he used dozens of amps that were thrown at him. For some reason, Vibroverb has become the most famous & is now widely associated with his signature tone (though you can reproduce his tone with most Fender amps & the right speakers). Since Fender didn’t continue the Vibroverb after CBS takeover in 1965, likely because players didn’t desire 1×15” amps at that time, there’s only a few blackface Vibroverb amps out there. Given the hype & popularity, these few Vibroverbs are extremely valuable & collectible. So, what’s so special with the Vibroverb? 1st, the 15” speaker sets it apart from most guitar amps. While most guitar amps have multiple 10” & 12” speakers, Vibroverb came with 1 big Jensen C15n or JBLd130f, the last one with an aluminum dust cap & can be hard to tame. Otherwise, the blackface Vibroverb is very similar to the other black/ silverface AB763 Fender amps. In fact, there aren’t any different features or technical improvements that justifies its popularity, it’s all about emotions. But that’s what music is all about: emotions”

Vibroverb belongs to the 6L6 Fender family. It’s powerful & loud, but in a different way vs 10” & 12” amps. It didn’t sell very well in 64-65. Players probably thought the amp was suited for bass amps, we’re not sure. It did score among steel guitar players at the time. The 15” speakers are firm in bass & have lots of sparkle, but most importantly, they have an enormous spread, carrying lower mids to anyone on stage. Mid freqs is exactly what you need on stage in an electrical band. The mids blend in nicely with bass, drums, piano & horns. It won’t interfere with the bass like a 4×10” setup does. Of course, it’s a matter of taste, but a guitar tone with strong mids contributes to a rich overall melodic footprint in an electrical band”

(Blackface era) Vibroverb was given the new blackface cosmetics & somewhat different circuitry (AA763). Still rated at 40w, the new design possessed the new blackface “optical coupler” vibrato circuit (in place of tweed-style vibrato found in its predecessor) & a bright switch. Gone also were the 2 10” Oxford speakers, in their place was a 1x15”. Both JBL & Jensens used, depended what was on hand at time of production

The blackface Vibroverb has 6L6 power tubes, low & high inputs, a Normal & Bright channel. It has Volume, Bass & Treble controls & Bright switch, plus reverb & vibrato controls. The Bright switch is engaged only in the AB model.
Fractal models of Fender amps always based on High input on amp. For Low input equivalent, set Input Trim to 0.5
Tips: Blackface Vibroverbs & re-issues were paired to a 15” speaker (Jensen C15N, JBL D130 or Eminence. Try 1x15 Empire or 1x15 Vibrato Verb stock cab

amps.name: VIBRATO VERB AA
body: Model: Fender Vibroverb
Cliff’s own 1964 blackface Vibroverb with AA763 circuit
Orig. Cab: Fender 1x15 (Jensen C15N, JBL D130, Eminence)
DynaCab: 1x15 Vibratoverb
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Some different component values in AA vs AB”
See 323 Vibrato Verb for more info on this amp
Tips: “I use AA, 1x15 cab, reduce Bass, use Input Drive to set desired drive amount. If AA or CS too dark, turn Bright switch on” - yek

amps.name: VIBRATO VERB AB
body: Model: Fender Vibroverb, AB763 (based on AB763 circuit)
Preamp tubes: 7025 (12AX7A Syl)
Notes: See 323 for more info

amps.name: VIBRATO VERB SRV
body: Model: Fender Vibroverb Custom RI
DynaCab: 1x15 Vibratoverb
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: 6L6
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: Based on ’64 Vibroverb Custom Reissue, produced between 2003-2008, with Mod switch on

About reissue: Fender produced this amp, in collab with Cesar Diaz, from ‘03-08, a modded version of SRV’s blackface Vibroverb, switchable between tube (original) & solid-state (diode) rectification. Normal channel & Vibrato could be disabled for more preamp gain
“Modeled after a VibroVerb with normal channel’s preamp tube removed, roughly doubles the gain of the Vibrato channel.”

Tips: (SRV sound) “For amp model, you wanna use Vibrato Verb SRV. Stevie pulled the normal channel triode out of his amp, which increases gain & changes the bias point of the vibrato channel. With new Vibroverb DynaCab, I can get eerily close to the Couldn’t Stand the Weather album tone”

“Transformer Matching value for SRV model based on output transformer in his amp. Legend has it, used a Bassman transformer, would lead to significant overmatching. To replicate: Increase Xfrmr Match to ~1.8”
“Find CS model too dark? Engage Bright switch” - yek

amps.name: WRECKER EXPRESS
body: Model: Trainwreck Express, Bright switch in center/top position
Orig. Cab: Marshall 4x12 (G12-M25)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman GB
Controls: Volume, Bass, Mid, Treble, Presence, 3-position Bright switch (center: off, top: clarity & sparkle, bottom: jangly highs)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL34 (can use 6V6)
Tonestack location: POST

Notes: “What makes Trainwreck amps so good? To start, they’re so responsive. You can set up a Trainwreck to control the distortion amount via volume knob, but, depending on how much OD you like, can go from gorgeous clean to crunch merely altering your touch.

Tone of these amps is incredible, but if you don’t have tone in your fingers, don’t expect a ‘wreck to make you sound good. Remember, they’re totally responsive. If you have a bad sounding guitar, or you put bad sounding tubes in the amp, don’t expect it to sound great.

Unprecedented clarity of these amps makes it possible to hear tonal differences created by your guitar cable, speaker cable, removing your pickguard, etc. A Trainwreck head will allow you to distinguish the quality of every link in your signal chain. Clarity also means when the amp is distorting, can still hear all the nuances of your playing. That Ken made amps with this degree of clarity, that still sounded beautiful, even under distortion, was his unique genius. As a result of the incredibly nuanced high-end of a Trainwreck head, the guitar tends to cut through the overall mix of the band. Trainwreck amps are also built incredibly well, built to last. They very rarely need servicing.” – Trainwreck
Trainwreck Express sounds like a Marshall Plexi

“Secret to a Trainwreck is the output transformer. The impedance ratio is about 2x that of other amps. I.e. typical 50W Marshall has a primary impedance of about 3200ohms. Trainwreck is about 6500ohms. This causes the power tubes to clip much sooner”
“Reason they clean up when you back off volume is due to OT being so overmatched. It’s about 2x the optimum value”
(Reply to weird quick silence if playing quick staccato chords) “That’s blocking distortion & a “feature” of Trainwrecks”
“They’re player’s amps, meant for use with low-output pickups & a light touch. If you use a Dimarzio Dick Crusher pickup & a mason’s hammer as a plectrum you’re not going to get good results”
“The real amp is a nasty thing, very spitty as expected, given the lack of a grid stopper resistor between last triode stage & PI”

“Only difference (between 2 Express models) is the Bright Cap. An Express (mine) has a 3-position switch (Off, 100pF, 500pF). Amp block supports Off/On & I figured people wouldn’t know details, so a separate model seemed the easiest solution. You can vary the Bright Cap, but you’d have to know the real amp has 2 caps, etc”
“Got out my Trainwreck Express & compared it to model last night & it’s spot-on. Nasty, spitty, Neil Young-like blocking distortion n all”

Tips: Cliff’s Tech Note: “A real Trainwreck Express is a fizzy, spitty amp. Some people like this, same people who like fuzz pedals & that quasi-half-wave rectified sound. Personally, I like more clarity. Reason Express is like this is primarily due to the last triode stage before the phase inverter (PI). This stage is biased very cold & drives the PI very hard due to lack of any limiting resistor (grid stopper) between triode & PI. Lack of a grid stopper resistor & large coupling capacitor create significant bias excursion (blocking distortion). Appears designer attempted minimizing bias excursion by biasing the triode cold, which limits positive excursion & therefore limits PI grid conduction. Despite the cold bias point, PI grid is still heavily overdriven, resulting in massive blocking distortion. AxeFx allows you to reduce amount of block distortion via PI Bias Excursion parameter. Simply turn it down & spittiness goes away, can also rebias the triode via Preamp Bias parameter. Values closer to 0 result in a more symmetric waveform.” & “Turn PI Bias Excursion to 0. Set Preamp Bias to -0.15 or so”

amps.name: WRECKER EXPRESS BRIGHT
body: Model: Trainwreck Express
Bright switch in bottom (brightest) position
Notes: See 327 Wrecker Express for more info

amps.name: WRECKER LIVERPOOL
body: Model: Trainwreck Liverpool
Bright switch in center or top position
Orig. Cab: Marshall 4x12 (G12-M25)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman GB
Controls: Volume, Bass, Mid, Treble, Presence, three-position Bright switch (center: off, top: clarity & sparkle, bottom: jangly highs)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL84
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Just because a power amp is “Class A” doesn’t mean it can’t have negative feedback. True that majority don’t but this particular amp has quite a bit of NF”
“Uses a Vox-style phase inverter”

(Info for reducing fizzy, spitty amp also relates to Liverpool) “Applies to Liverpool because preamp is almost identical to Express. Rocket has a different preamp with the tone stack before the PI. This reduces signal level substantially & lessens the amount of blocking distortion”
Model’s a fusion between Trainwreck Express (preamp) & Rocket (power amp). In other words: Marshall meets VOX
Like all Trainwreck amps, the Liverpool is a simple amp. Because there’s no Master, Volume control sets volume level & gain too.
Tips: Maybe turn down Bass a little & you’re done
G12M (greenback) speakers are a logical choice for this amp model

amps.name: WRECKER LIVERPOOL BRIGHT
body: Model: Trainwreck Liverpool
Bright switch in bottom (brightest) position
Notes: See 329 Wrecker Liverpool for more info

amps.name: WRECKER ROCKET
body: Model: Trainwreck Rocket,
Orig. Cab: Marshall 4x12 (G12-M25)
DynaCab: 4x12 Friedman GB
Controls:  Volume (Input Drive), Bass, Treble, Cut (Hi-Cut)
Preamp tubes: 12AX7A (Syl)
Power tubes: EL84
Tonestack location: PRE
Notes: “Rocket has a different preamp (vs Express) with tone stack before PI, it reduces signal level substantially & lessens amount of blocking distortion”
A boutique amp based on a VOX AC30 Top Boost, with a tube rectifier
Tips: Model is based on Trainwreck Rocket, only a few Rockets exist. Some say that it’s the best-sounding (Trainwreck) amp ever made

“While all Trainwreck models are quite rare, Rocket is by far the rarest, only about 8 ever made. Rocket was the 3rd & last official Trainwreck model built by Ken & his increasing illness prevented him from building very many, a real shame because amp’s truly the finest, most magical sounding British-style EL84 amp ever made. Clean tones are rich & gorgeous with crystal airiness enveloping chime that no other EL84 amp complexity. Ken often referred to his personal Rocket amp as Reality Check because once you played them all, the other great amps sounded less special. “Marcy” sets the tonal benchmark & is deservedly one of the most valuable guitar amps in the world

Trainwrecks are very responsive amps, with rich harmonic complexity & beautifully balance tone. Amp’s gorgeous clean & benchmark OD tones can be controlled with the guitar’s volume control or merely altering touch. Trainwrecks set the standard for warmth, fullness, responsiveness, note articulation, touch sensitivity, string-to-string separation, fret board feel, clarity, dynamics & singing sustain. Most people feel a Trainwreck Rocket is the finest sounding, tube rectified British style EL84 amp ever built & finding one has become next to impossible” – Ultra Sound
We also have a Ruby Rocket model, also based on the Trainwreck Rocket. Models sound different though & need different tone settings
Cut is a passive tone control in output stage of amp, adjusts contour of treble freqs. Because no Master, Volume sets volume level & gain
G12M (greenbacks) are a logical choice for this amp model
“Model’s quite bass-heavy, have to turn Bass down & use Hi Cut to control highs. Otherwise, pretty much leave controls default” - yek

general.title: INTRODUCTION
general.text:
INTRODUCTION
This guide was initially made for my own use with my Axe-Fx III. I greatly enjoy 
Yek’s Amp guide, however, the information is more so in a Word format, which 
takes a little more scrolling to navigate through it, or constantly referring back to 
the table of contents to jump to the amp in question. The guide is more thorough, 
but the Wiki is more updated, so flipping back n forth really doesn’t work with my 
ADHD brain, so I wanted something that joined them & other sources. 
The way I initially conceptualized it was to create a document that had a large 
amount of FAS amp info in a more compact space, for use on my computer, using 
Axe-Edit, while I make my own music, when I am just messing around on electric, 
or when I wanna learn my workflow tools more (Axe-Fx). I managed to do it in like 
40 pages here (though the info is SMASHED together). 
I consolidated information from Yek’s Amp Guide, Fractal Wiki, FAS Forum, as well 
as information pulled from manuals, company sites, etc.
The task of collecting and then consolidating all of this into something that is 
usable is a real chore. The biggest shout out to Yek, Cliff, and anyone else who 
contributed to the Fractal information databases, as the depth of everything is 
staggering and deeply appreciated.
I’ll admit freely, this initial version is still kinda messy. It’s not exactly easy to have a 
makeshift/patchwork understanding of Adobe Indesign, Axe-Fx, amp design tech, 
etc., as well as I had no information structure, so it may bounce around to different 
topics, technical info, etc. I’ll come back to it when I’m more motivated to continue 
& refine further.
The next step I have for this guide is to link manuals, add more settings/guitarist 
use info, more tips, etc.
My plans is to also consolidate this info down to a more “essentials” guide, 
stripping all non-essentials, leaving only what is most significant for Axe-Fx use. 
This guide doesn’t take into account any Fractal Audio products, besides the Axe. I 
don’t have those units, but I think the info still applies? IDK
Please feel free to reach out if you have any questions, additional information that 
you would like to add, any comments/criticism, or if you see anything incorrect/
mis-interpreted. 
I’ve learned a lot about guitar amplifiers & electronic components in the process of 
making this guide, I hope you do too!
Clayton Welch
Amp Controls
Gain/Overdrive
	
g The Amp block has Gain and/or Overdrive & tone controls, similar to the 
equivalent real amps. The range & behavior of those controls is the same as 
on the real amp, in current firmware. If the real amp has 2 gain controls, the 
one closest to the 1/4” input is modeled as Gain in the model & the other one is 
Overdrive
Low Input, High input
	
gIf the real amp has 2 inputs, i.e. Low & High, the model is always based on 
the High input. To get the equivalent of the Low input, change Input Trim to 
0.500.
Tapers
The range of the Drive taper is 0-10. Volume knobs on Fender 
amps go from 1 to 10.
This translates to:
Fender 1 = Axe 0.00
Fender 2 = Axe 1.11
Fender 3 = Axe 2.22
Fender 4 = Axe 3.33
Fender 5 = Axe 4.44
Fender 6 = Axe 5.55
Fender 7 = Axe 6.66
Fender 8 = Axe 7.78
Fender 9 = Axe 8.89
Fender 10 = Axe 10.00

Why Your Amp Doesn’t Sound Like Our Amp 
(Fractal)
Cliff: The models in our products are based on our in-house 
reference amps. If a model doesn’t sound like your version of that 
amp it won’t sound like our reference amp either. Why?
1. Component accuracy & drift
The components used in tube amps are low-cost, consumer-
grade parts. They typically have tolerances of 10% or more. 
Over time the value of these components drifts. If your amp is 
old chances are it doesn’t sound like it did when it was new. All 
our old reference amps are given a thorough checkup prior to 
modeling with any out-of-tolerance parts replaced.​
2. Potentiometer tolerance
A typical consumer potentiometer has tolerance of up to ±20%. 
That’s huge & that’s end-to-end accuracy. On top of that the 
midpoint accuracy can be another ±20%. So if you have a 1M 
pot it could be as low as 800K. If it’s linear it’s midpoint should 
be 400K but could be as low as 360K. Now your 1M pot that 
should be 500K at halfway is only 360K. That’s an error of 28%!​
3. Potentiometer taper
A big one. Potentiometers come in a variety of tapers: linear, 
30A, 20A, 10A, etc. The taper on an audio taper pot (i.e. 30A) 
denotes the value of the pot at mid rotation. For example a 1M, 
10A pot would be 10% of its value at “noon”, or 100K.​
Manufacturers are constantly changing the taper of the pots 
in their amps. Sometimes the designer changes the taper as 
customers are reticent to turn knobs much away from noon. 
It’s a weird psychological thing. Sometimes the manufacturer 
changes the taper due to availability concerns. Sometimes 
they change the taper when moving manufacturing locations. 
Sometimes they change the taper for no apparent reason at all.​
Another factor is that almost all amps don’t use true log 
taper pots. They use “commercial log” taper which is a crude 
approximation to a log taper. This is because true log taper pots 
are expensive. Fractal Audio products use true log taper. This 
means that ‘7’ on your amp is not exactly ‘7’ on the model even 
if the pot in your amp is exactly 1M & its taper is exactly 10A. 
Why do we do it this way? Because the response is smoother 
& if true log pots were the same price as consumer log pots 
everyone would use true log taper.​
We model all amps assuming the pots are ideal. We assume 
the end-to-end resistance is exactly, say, 1M & the midpoint is, 
say, exactly 100K. We DO NOT use the values measured in our 
reference amp because no 2 amps are the same so we use the 
DESIGNERS INTENDED VALUE.​
​What all this means is ‘3’ on your amp is not necessarily the 
same as ‘3’ on the Axe-Fx.​
​Example: the Master Volume pot in a 5150 is a 1M, 15A audio 
taper pot. Theoretically it should be 150K at noon. On our 
reference amp it’s about 15% low. If the reference amp’s MV is 
set to ‘3’ we have to set the model to around ‘2.5’ to match. This 
is unsurprising due to the tolerance of the reference amp’s pot.​
4. ​Indicator accuracy
On many amps if you set a knob to noon it’s not actually 
halfway in the pot’s rotation. Why? Several reasons. Some 
amps are just weird. For example the Bogner Shiva’s minimum 
rotation is around 6:00 & the maximum is around 4:00. So noon 
is actually past midpoint. Same with Soldanos. In other cases 
the knobs aren’t oriented perfectly on the shaft. If it’s a knurled 
shaft the knob may be off one tooth. If it’s a smooth shaft then 
you’re at the mercy of the human who put the knob on the shaft 
& tightened the set screw. This is why I prefer D-shafts. Finally 
the pot itself may be rotated relative to the panel.​
​You can try this yourself. Turn the knob on your amp fully CCW. 
Note the position of the indicator. Now rotate fully CW. Note the 
marker position. If it’s an old amp it’s probably not symmetrical.​
Then there’s the whole marking thing. Fender’s are numbered 
1-10. Soldanos go to 11. We use 0-10 so be wary of amp’s #ing.​
5. Power Tube Bias.
Another big one. The transconductance (gain) of a power tube 
can vary greatly. This is why power tubes are color coded, sold 
in matched sets, etc.​
​Amps come in 2 flavors: fixed bias & cathode bias. Fixed bias 
amps apply a “fixed” voltage to the grid of the power tubes. 
Cathode bias amps use a resistor between the cathode & 
ground to self bias the tube.​
​Most, but not all, fixed bias amps allow the user to adjust the 
bias point of the amp. This allows the bias point to be set to an 
optimum value for the particular set of tubes installed (since 
the transconductance can vary greatly). Some fixed bias amps 
do not allow adjustment. Examples are Mesa/Boogies, 5150s 
& several other brands/types. The drawback of this is that the 
bias can vary greatly depending upon the gain of the tubes 
installed. Due to this the manufacturers err on the safe side & 
the bias is usually much colder than the ideal value.​
​Most cathode biased amps are not adjustable. Again you are at 
the mercy of the tube’s gain but these amps tend to be biased 
hot to begin with & have higher transformer matching which 
prevents excursion outside of the S.O.A. (safe operating area).​
​If the bias is adjustable where the manufacturer decides to 
bias their tubes is a matter of preference. Most manufacturers 
bias their tubes on the cold side to prevent premature failure & 
reduce warranty claims. Especially the larger manufacturers.​
​This leads to the question of “what is the ideal bias point?” The 
pervasive school of thought is you adjust the bias so the idle 
dissipation is 60-70% of the tube’s peak power rating. This is a 
safe approach & ensures that the tubes don’t “red plate” & live 
fairly long & prosperous lives.​
​My opinion is that the ideal bias point is NOT a function of the 
tube’s power rating. It’s the point at which the power amp’s 
transfer function is most linear. Unfortunately operating the 
tubes at that point can result in exceeding the tube’s S.O.A. So 
the optimum bias point depends on the tube’s power rating, 
the transformer primary impedance (matching) & the user’s 
tolerance to tube replacement frequency.​
For example, if we bias an EL34 based power amp at 60% 
peak dissipation it’s actually running fairly cold. If we know that 
the transformer is slightly overmatched we can bias the tubes 
hotter, 70% or even more. This will result in a warmer tone but 
the tubes will wear faster.​
​What does all this mean? Well, I bias the virtual tubes on the 
warm side. EL34s are biased at around 70% because we don’t 
have to worry about them wearing out. 6L6s are biased a little 
colder, around 60% but this is actually as “warm” as the EL34s 
because of the higher plate dissipation of a 6L6.​
​In practice this means that the models in the Axe-Fx will biased 
warmer than a new amp straight out of the box as most amps 
are biased cold (too cold IMO). After you wear the tubes out & 
bring it to a tech the tech will replace those tubes & bias them 
hotter than factory. So if you’re comparing your new, out-of-the 
box 5150 with the Axe-Fx model the amp will probably sound 
“colder”. Some people like this, many do not. If you like a colder 
sounding power amp it’s just a knob twist away.​
Axe-Fx™ is a trademark of Fractal Audio Systems and used with permission.  This guide is not an official Fractal Audio Systems publication and is not affiliated with or endorsed by Fractal Audio Systems.

general.title: Page 3
general.text:
Abbreviations
I shorted a lot of the info, including using some abbreviations, to 
make it fit in a smaller space. Here’s some abbreviations that may 
be present (some I think should be pretty obvious, but may not be, 
so I added this):
	
gAdd’l = additional
	
gB/M/T = bass, mid, treble
	
gCath = cathode
	
gCh/Chnl or chnls = channel or channels
	
gConfig or configs = configuration or configurations
	
gCW or CCW = clockwise or counter-clockwise
	
gDlx = deluxe
	
gFdbk = feedback
	
gIn = input
	
gLvl or Lvls = level or levels
	
gMV = master volume
	
gOD/ODs/ODing = overdrive/overdrives/overdriving
	
gOut = output (sometimes)
	
gPres = presence
	
gRvrb = reverb
	
gSpkr or Spkrs = speaker or speakers
	
gSw = switch
	
gTreb = treble
	
gVol = volume
	
gVs = versus
	
gW (as in ex: 30w) = watt
	
gXfrmr or Xformer = transformer
	
g~ = Approximately or about
Probably missed some :)
More to come...

general.title: Page 36
general.text:
gSpeaker Impedance has a large sonic effect on the sounds 
as the power section is switched between its 3 different 
Modes. We prefer matching 8ohm speaker load connected 
to 8ohm SPEAKER OUTPUT for the most balanced sound 
& overall performance in all 3 Channels. However, Simul-
Class Output Section is a mysterious animal & produces 
some interesting & musical results when different loads are 
applied. One favorite is connecting the 8ohm speaker load 
to the 4ohm SPEAKER OUTPUT when using the 45w (Half 
Power) setting. Feel free to experiment with different load 
combos. You may discover an impedance scenario that, 
though is a technical mismatch, produces a unique response 
that fits your needs. 
	
gChannel 1: Lowest gain of 3 Channels. 2 Modes CLEAN & 
FAT are structured for clean sounds. These 2 modes are well 
described by their names, CLEAN is sweet, sparkling low 
gain beauty & FAT adds big low end & breathiness to sound. 
These 2 have the greatest headroom & most dynamic range 
& sensitivity. They’re the least compressed & retain the 
fastest attack characteristic.
	
gLowest gain of 3 Channels & will cover all your clean rhythm 
& solo styles & slightly driven chording sound needs. As 
with Modes in each Channel, we start with slimmed down 
(skinnier), tighter voice of CLEAN & move up the girth scale 
to FAT & finally to slightly higher mid-gain of TWEED.
	
gCLEAN is derived from a blend of Mk series clean sounds 
taken from MkIV & perfect for rhythm comping in ensemble 
environments, where guitar has to fit in a defined spot of a 
more complex mix. It’s also extremely accurate in the time 
domain & has a balanced blend of tight low end to sparkling, 
immediacy of the top end. Mode also clips great in 45 & 10w 
power modes with its’ well balanced, more stripped nature.
	
gFAT stands in sharp contrast to this, with big breathy bottom 
that extends down into the sub-low region & adds air to the 
entire spectrum as the top end gets more bubbly & spacial. 
This sound pays tribute to the best blackface inspired 
sounds & circuit relies heavily on architecture from Input 2 
of the original MkI & MkV CLEAN Mode. FAT excels at big, 
featured chording parts & single note clean soloing & a 
great choice for clipped sounds (with BASS control set low) 
in 45 or 10w power mode.
	
gTWEED adds a boost in the mids & rolls back some top 
end, to create a sound that punches through a mix with 
mid authority & clips with a more rounded vocal quality. It 
works great for lower gain crunch rhythm parts & can add a 
healthy dose of top end cut with PRESENCE, as there’s less 
upper harmonics to get hashy/buzzy
	
gCHANNEL 1 VOICE SWITCH (NORMAL/BOLD): Offers 2 
choices in voicing mids & upper mids/lower treble region. 
NORMAL has a balanced smooth response that shines for 
both shimmering sweet clean sounds & creamy soft-clip 
tones as modes are driven harder with the GAIN control. 
No particular freqs stick out in this position, which creates 
a perfect base for rhythm work & makes NORMAL respond 
very well to further embellishment with the EQ
	
gBOLD punches through with a pronounced bump in these 
mid & upper mid/low treble freqs to cut through a dense 
mix & be heard. It gives the impression of increased power 
& headroom, as cleans sounds fly out the speakers with 
immediacy & authority. Likewise, OD sounds, especially 
in TWEED, take the added attack & turn it into stinging 
aggression. Be sure to work with TREBLE, MID & PRESENCE in BOLD 
to fine tune these regions & optimize both the sound & feel.
	
gCLEAN is derived from classic Mk circuits & puts emphasis on tight, 
focused clean rhythm & articulate single note playing. This mode has 
the greatest clean headroom of any mode in the amp & though it can 
be pushed to clip at extreme settings (4:00-5:30), it’ll remain tighter 
& somewhat stiffer than FAT when used for this. The good side of this 
stiffness is the BASS control can occasionally be set higher vs going for 
clipped sounds in FAT, as the overall personality of CLEAN is stripped 
of sub-low freqs that can be challenging when looking for aggressive 
threshold rhythm sounds.
	
gCLEAN works very well for aggro & brash clipped chording sounds, 
especially when Power Select is set to 45w & PRESENCE is a bit high 
(1:00-2:30). Try this vibe for Punk or super fast Rock rhythm styles & 
need aggressive almost-clean sounds that start & stop instantaneously.
	
gCLEAN shines for chording & arpeggiated work with its crystalline top 
end sparkle & high harmonic openness. Low end is tucked up higher 
so it adds breath & warmth, but doesn’t go too low, so won’t get too 
big/sluggish & get in the way if a groove calls for precise rhythmic note 
durations. Great for skanky Funk rhythm/burning Country pickin.
	
gMidrange freqs are a bit higher in freq vs FAT mode, enabling a 
percussive attack that stays tight & has max impact in a range that cuts 
& protects its territory in a complex mix. Playing with these higher mids 
can be a powerful way to shape the CLEAN mode, as the role they play 
either brings sound forward, even in the time domain, making it seem 
faster, or lends a recessive, more relaxed quality to the sound, making it 
seem slower. Experiment with BASS & MID & learn to tune the sound’s 
attack, width & girth in CLEAN to match style & tempos you play most.
	
gSome Modes work better than others with the EQ & respond better to 
radical shaping power with a more harmonious demeanor. Generally 
speaking, Modes with less low end tend to work better & are usually 
the ones that have created classic sounds with EQ in place. CLEAN in 
Channel 1 & all Modes in Channel 3 (with MkIIC+ being most friendly) 
tend to be best for positive EQ reinforcement.
	
gGAIN: By far the most powerful control in the MkV & settings determine 
the style & personality of all 3 Modes in each channel. It meters the 
gain in different tube stages depending on Channel & Mode & sets 
Input Stage headroom, which determines whether sound is clean or 
overdriven. It also acts as subtle Tone control as tube stages’ gain is 
increased/decreased & imparts its own “color” on the sound.
	
gIn all Channels, there’s 3 regions of GAIN, a low gain zone from 9:00-
11:30, a warmer, more saturated zone from 12:00-2:00 & a higher 
gain zone from 2:30-5:30. Each zone can be used for many different 
applications & all can be used for both chording & single note solo 
work. As GAIN is swept throughout its range, it imparts different 
textures & tonal character.
	
gGenerally speaking, lower end (9:30-11:30) in all Channels/Modes 
produces a brighter, more open character & more dynamic content 
available. This region’s great for clean, sparkling chording in Channel 
1, where max headroom is available, top end harmonics are bubbly 
& attack is lightning fast. Channel 3 also responds well in this lower 
region & similar chording/solo sounds await you here. The same 
Modes can sound so vintage-approved & agile when set low, can be 
ferocious high gain Modes when things get pushed.
	
gMiddle region of GAIN (12:00-2:00) is where the most balanced sounds 
live & delivers warm, full sound, detailed attack & good dynamics & 
Tone controls still have a powerful effect on signal. Channel 1 CLEAN & 
FAT Modes deliver great chording response, sound richer & have more 
body here. Depending on pickup style & strength, watch for clipping 
when nearing the crossover point, gain-wise. Channel 3 Modes offer 
their greatest flexibility, focus & detail in this middle zone. As these 
sounds are all higher gain vs their Channel 1 & 2 counterparts, the 
best dynamic response & attack character is in this window. 
Approaching 2:00, there will be plenty of saturation to keep 
chords grinding & single notes hanging, but not too much to 
start compressing the life out of the sound. If you not getting 
great gain sound results in this region for Channel 2 or 3, try 
pickups with a bit hotter output.
	
gThe highest region of GAIN (2:30-5:30) is all about saturation. 
Up here the signal gets much fatter in the low end & top end 
begins to recede for a round, compressed sound. Dynamics 
become slower with lower peaks & produces a more legato, 
creamier feel. In Channel 1, high end of GAIN produces some 
great “clipped clean” sounds as the Input stage gives up & 
starts to saturate. These sounds are further enhanced by the 
45 & 10w Power Modes & VARIAC POWER setting. Channel 
3 is absolutely over the top when GAIN is ran this high. The 
more midrange-prominent character of these Modes allows 
you to still have good articulation here, even with amounts of 
saturation that’d turn most circuits to useless mush. The focus, 
articulation & tightly-glued gain that sticks to notes & tracks 
flawlessly is a sound that’s pure Boogie. Up here, notice the 
Tone controls have a diminished effect on the sound, as notes 
are so saturated & their character has been pre-determined by 
the way each Mode is voiced & how it reacts to this gain level.
	
gMASTER: Determines overall output level, located at the very 
end of the preamp. Using it in combination with GAIN, any 
amount of preamp signal strength, gain can be achieved at 
any playing volume. 1st get sound desired, then balance the 
volume levels with this control
	
gFor general applications & to get best performance out of all 
any mode, we recommend settings in 9:00-12:00 range with 
most settling in around 10:30 for average playing volumes. 
	
gSome like to run MASTER cranked & raise GAIN until they 
reach a desired sound, thinking this achieves the purest result. 
In theory, they believe this resembles removing the control 
altogether from the signal path & in a way it does. However 
most “vintage non-master” amps they’re seeking to emulate 
have discrete resistors in that place of the circuit, to adjust 
or “tune” the preamp output to the power section sensitivity. 
MASTER is nothing more than a variable resistor that offers 
an infinite range of settings possibilities & makes amp more 
versatile with no sonic penalty. If you prescribe to this old 
school approach, then by all means, use it this way, it won’t 
hurt. However you’ll be severely limiting potential sounds 
you can achieve by removing the limitless great sounding 
combinations of GAIN & MASTER settings.
	
gPRESENCE: Adjusts high freqs above those of TREBLE, 
located in the power section, farther downstream in the signal 
path & not in the preamp. PRESENCE adjusts a specific zone 
of freqs in the negative feedback circuit of the power section 
for each individual Mode. The MkV incorporates substantial 
circuitry to achieve complex switching of parts needed to 
voice each Mode correctly & ensure an adjustable range on 
PRESENCE that’s sound-style appropriate & musically usable.
	
gCan think of PRESENCE as a control that allows either 
clamping power amp down, compressing it & darkening 
things, or open it up & let the full spectrum of upper harmonics 
come blazing through. It also has a great deal to do with how 
dynamic the signal is & how a sound will cut through the mix 
in an ensemble environment.
	
gAt low settings (7:30-10:30) the sound will be warm & round 
with a more compressed feel & dynamic fluctuation will be 
limited. As it’s increased (11:00-2:30), top end starts to become 
more dominant & compression gives way to “cut” & dynamic peaks 
jump out with startling speed & accuracy. At the top end of control 
(2:30-5:30), a super aggressive blend of upper harmonics dominate 
the sound & this region can be somewhat dangerous if not applied 
in small measures. Higher notes will slice & dice even the bravest 
set of ears & we suggest using this region mostly in the studio for 
recording heavy crunch rhythm parts & even then, mostly on parts 
that feature lower strings. This region, especially when coupled 
with the inherent curve of many microphones typically used in P.A. 
(Sound reinforcement) applications can be truly punishing.
	
gWe suggest using lower to middle range of PRESENCE (9:00-12:30) 
for the best/most balanced sound in all Modes & venturing outside 
this only for specific uses, like needing more aggressive top-end 
point or darker, more compressed & wider sound.
	
gClean sounds in Channel 1 can generally benefit from a bit higher 
settings (10:30-12:30) than sounds in this, or any Channel, that has 
OD involved. Once saturation begins, the freqs carried in the control 
can make things edgy or brittle, even buzzy, real fast if not careful. 
Overdriven chording can tolerate higher settings (10:30-12:30) 
better than single note sounds, which usually want to roam the zone
below 11:00 to stay round, focused & vocal.
	
gNOTE: High PRESENCE settings (2:30-5:30) can put extra stress 
on even borderline microphonic preamp tubes (ones susceptible 
to high pitched ringing & noise), causing them to begin ringing or 
show other signs of instability. Avoid these settings (especially in 
Combo amps, where added sympathetic & vibrational forces put 
even more stress on tubes) to ensure trouble free performance.
	
gTREBLE: If VOLUME is the most powerful control in MkV, this 
comes in a close 2nd. It’s responsible for shaping the character of 
the amp. It can overpower the other Tone controls easily & therefore 
its setting is crucial to a rich & balanced sound in all Modes. In fact, 
TREBLE feeds the signal to MID & BASS controls & acts like a valve 
for their signal strength.
	
gTREBLE set in its higher regions (2:00-5:30) it sends a smaller 
signal to those controls & they’re less active & the dominant 
character will be very bright, TREBLE-heavy freqs. Conversely, very 
low settings will produce sounds that may be a little BASS heavy 
& overly dark, as a very large amount of signal is shoveled toward 
MID & BASS. Setting with care & taste is critical for Tone control 
string to work in harmony.
	
gIn all Channels/Modes, the middle region of TREBLE has the best 
balance & creates sounds that are bright, but still rich & warm. We 
suggest you start with TREBLE at 12:00 & adjust up/down slightly 
until desired blend is achieved. Remember, you can use PRESENCE 
to add additional (& slightly higher) top end, with no penalty, in 
regards to effectiveness of other Tone controls, as it works in a part 
of the circuit that’s much farther down the line in the signal path.
	
gBe aware of TREBLE when looking for clipped Blues sounds in 
Channel 1, with GAIN cranked. The additional gain added by 
running the TREBLE high (1:30-2:30) can help saturate enough to 
get some great pushed sounds. You’ll have to run BASS rather low 
(8:30-9:30) to keep things tight.
	
gMID: Acts much more like a standard tone control & doesn’t have 
quite the massive global shaping power of VOLUME & TREBLE, 
yet its setting does impart strong character on the sound. It brings 
in & out a broad band of midrange freqs & a fair amount of higher 
“low treble” range freqs. These highs are lower than TREBLE or 
PRESENCE, but important for punch & cut of the amp in a mix.
	
gFor rhythm playing in CLEAN & FAT Modes of Channel 1, lower MID 
setting (7:30-10:00) scoops some of this midrange attack & makes 
bottom end breathe more. This range also makes things more 
resilient & create an easier to play, more elastic feel on the strings.

general.title: Page 38
general.text:
the sound is definitely bigger.
	
gIf you want it tight, urgent feeling with fastest attack & max 
focus, use MkIIC+ mode. For the fattest, warmest, biggest 
sound & don’t need super fast response, use MkIV & 
EXTREME Modes
	
gChannel 3 is a tribute to the MARK series Channel Switching 
amps & 3 circuits from the pinnacle of that architecture. 2 
most sought after, MkIIC+ & MkIV, responsible for some 
of the most iconic high gain guitar sounds ever recorded. 
Channel 3 contains the IIC+ Lead Channel & 2 Modes from 
MkIV, LEAD & LEAD EXTREME. Has highest gain of the 
MkV channels, thought of by many as the Lead channel, 
but there’s many ways to use it. Mid-focus to gain lends 
itself equally to chording rhythm & single note solo sounds, 
as it’s articulate, tight-tracking response & lush harmonic 
layering. These circuits provide amazing resolution across 
the gain scale with great threshold-of-clip, pushed clean 
sounds found at the low end of the GAIN control & searing, 
explosive lead sounds at the high end, with a whole range of 
urgent, tight crunch rhythm sounds between.
	
gCHANNEL 3 TRIODE/PENTODE: Allows you to choose 
wiring configuration of the middle pair of power tubes in 
Channel 3. Feature was popular on the MkIV & changes the 
personality of the sound’s attack & liquidity in the higher 
gain condition.
	
gThis switch rewires the configuration of the Class A output 
tube pair (inside 2 6L6’s) to produce a radical sound & feel 
difference of the modes. Many players like it only one way, 
saying it makes or breaks the vibe for them or that they can’t 
play as well when it’s not set to their favorite way. We feel it’s 
a powerful stylistic parameter & should be explored fully for 
its usefulness to your sound.
	
gTRIODE softens midrange attack & accentuates the upper 
harmonic region, creating a silky, more compressed, almost 
liquid feel & lowers overall power output. This tends to 
raise the harmonic haze, sweetens top end & it shines for 
expressive high gain solo work or high gain chording that’s 
more textural than rhythmic. It offers more complexity & 
nuance, but trade-off is it may not cut through a mix as well. 
TRIODE produces less overall power output so it’s probably 
not the choice for very low gain chording, unless you’re 
looking for more of a “vintage sag” feel. Original MkIIC+ 
amps were hard-wired to TRIODE. MkIV offered a choice 
between the 2 configurations.
	
gPENTODE accentuates midrange punch & lowers the upper 
harmonic region for a bolder, tighter attack, especially in the 
low end & increases output power & headroom. It has more 
immediate dynamics, with greater peaks & feels “faster” 
to play, almost anticipating your next input. This urgent 
attack means PENTODE excels at any style, where rhythmic 
nuance & tight-tracking accuracy are essential. May be 
the best choice for solo sounds that pop through a mix & 
defined/articulate high gain crunch chording, with huge 
tight low-end.
	
gBIAS SELECT: Allows for proper biasing when swapping 
to EL34 type power tubes, for a brighter, harmonic-laden 
response. It selects a different set of bias resistors & insures 
these extremely different power tubes will sound better & 
work more reliably.
	
gMkIIC+: Recreation of the sought after sound of these 
2-channel MARK series amps, produced in the mid 80s that 
became the voice of choice for many recording artists of 
the day. The tone is identical, except the Sylvania 415 power 
tubes, which are no longer available. During R&D, we even found 
ways to mimic the important mid-punch character inherent in those 
tubes & far better, you don’t have to compromise your clean or lead 
sound, because they share controls like in the original layout! The IIC+ 
trademark sound is urgent & stripped, but also has incredible focus 
despite it’s precious multi-dimensional layers of upper harmonics. It 
works equally well for aggressive high gain chording & blistering single 
note soloing in low or high regions of the GAIN control. When dialed 
in (GAIN 1:30-2:30, TREBLE 12:00-1:00, BASS 10:00-12:00, PRESENCE 
9:00-11:30), it has a lead voice that’s unrivaled in articulation, nuance 
& time domain accuracy in high gain genres. These qualities are 
especially favored by those players whose technical ability demands 
instantaneous response & tight, detailed tracking of pick attack. After 
the attack, a beautiful morphing of harmonics occurs & notes segue 
through a whole range of dynamic & freq changes that’s a joy to 
experience. You’ll find yourself playing like never before & reaching 
newfound depths of expression.
	
gFor high gain rhythm work, IIC+ unleashes the other sound in Heavy 
Rock Crunch. More complex & harmonically layered than its British 
fueled counterpart, the C+ comes on like a grinding wall of gain, 
that is truly menacing. The shameless scooping of mids & rebellious 
boosting of both lows & highs simultaneously led to the iconic “V” 
curve becoming so popular in the 80s recordings, that it became 
synonymous with the IIC+ sound. This sound reached further notoriety 
& became referred to as Boogie Crunch & even the “California Sound”.
	
gThrough the 90s, our Dual Rectifier’s sub-low enhanced grind & 
punishing blend of attack & harmonics became the pop-metal sound. 
However, there remained a strong cult following for the MARK Series/
Graphic EQ crunch sound ( re-introduced in the MkIV) & this tighter, 
more articulate sound is now a classic & here to stay. In the current 
decade, it’s become the crunch of choice for high tempo aggressive 
Punk & Indie Rock, chosen once again for its’ ability to stay glued to 
pick attack & track even at burning tempos.
	
gThere are no real danger zones or pitfalls with the C+ mode, other 
than the obvious, turn the BASS control down slightly in proportion to 
increase of GAIN. Don’t try to run the GAIN maxed out with TREBLE 
&/or PRESENCE way up, as preamp tubes will most likely not handle 
this abuse & begin to show their individual degree of microphonicity
Hints for the C+ Mode might include these:
	
gUse the Graphic EQ for extreme low end with high GAIN settings, 
it comes later in the circuit & will have less tendency to get loose & 
flubby vs BASS control.
	
gWork with GAIN, TREBLE & PRESENCE in finite increments when 
searching for your ideal lead voice, as these interact greatly & have a 
big effect on pick attack sensitivity & focus. PRESENCE compresses 
things in its lower regions creating a more voice-like character & 
opens up the upper harmonic region in higher settings, for a more 
cutting, blade-like attack.
	
gFor lead sounds, Roll in BASS wanted after fine-tuning the attack 
signature with GAIN, TREBLE & PRESENCE.
	
gFill things in just enough, but don’t create flub. The BASS control 
brings in beautiful rich low end, but can overtake the balance set by 
the other controls quickly & degrade attack.
	
gDon’t overlook this Mode for lower gain sounds! Because of the 
harmonic content & instant attack properties, it excels at low gain 
Blues sounds, both rhythm/single note & can produce some threshold 
of clip, almost clean sounds. It’s open, dynamic & responds great to 
plucking techniques too. Traditionalists have even commented on how 
well the old “guitar volume knob” or “oldschool channel switching” 
approach works with this sound. Set low enough, C+ works great just 
backing off for clean & cranking up for lead on the guitars’ Volume pot. 
When GAIN is this low, you can dial in more BASS to add warmth & 
3-dimensionality.
	
gThis is an addicting mode to play & one of, if not the most 
expressive, of the 9 Modes. Spend some quality time exploring 
how the controls interact, once you dial it in for your individual 
style, magic will happen.
	
gMARK IV: In comparison to the MkIIC+ mode, the MkIV mode 
is infused with more low mid & has an attack character that’s 
wider & less pointed in the upper mids, bottom end goes 
down lower & has more gain too, giving the sound thickness & 
girth, that produces huge, ominous crunch chords & smooth 
vocal single note sounds. Circuit differences create a different 
feel, it feels creamier & gives impression of less resistance on 
the strings, making it easier to play for many guitarists. Slightly 
less-instantaneous attack created by the addition of low end 
makes the IV Mode feel a little slower & less like your picking 
technique is ”under the microscope” & can be either freedom 
or lack of definition, depending on your POV & stylistic needs.
	
gFor lower gain sounds, MkIV mode has more air & 
3-dimensionality vs C+. Some nice purring threshold-of-clip 
sounds can be found with GAIN set very low (9:00-10:00) & 
TREBLE around 12:30. With GAIN this low, you can dial in 
quite a bit of BASS (11:00-1:30) before things get too tubby, 
letting you really move some air. Here’s also a great place 
to experiment with both TRIODE/PENTODE &/or POWER 
MODES. PENTODE will produce a bold, punchy sound that 
will let you jab with authority.
	
gHigh gain sounds in the IV Mode are fat! The lower bass freqs 
combined with added low mid gain makes for the biggest 
crunch chording sounds in the amp. Watch setting BASS more 
carefully, as lower freqs bottom end can get bloated much 
faster. If looking for huge bottom end with high gain settings, 
it’s wise to turn to the EQ to achieve this. BASS dumps too 
much sub-low into the signal path, at a point earlier in the 
preamp. It gets amplified over & over, until it turns to flub, 
whereas the EQ comes at the end of the signal chain & adds 
low end to the “finished preamp sound”.
	
gNORMAL/BRIGHT switch is very effective at bringing forward 
upper harmonics, especially for high gain chording & even 
single note sounds. When adding this upper-stratosphere 
layer of tone by engaging BRIGHT, it may be necessary to 
reduce PRESENCE (which contains a narrower, adjustable 
band of these highs) in order to arrive at the sound that’s 
right for you. Some people are very sensitive to these upper 
highs, heard as edgy, buzzy & unpleasant. For those players, 
NORMAL position provided to notch them out almost 
completely. We feel they’re an important part of the spectrum, 
as they give the sound spacial width, openness & a beautiful 
layer of stacked harmonics. However, they must be blended in 
as an integral part of the sound. 
	
gCan be achieved in a number of ways once you’ve decided 
to include this region of the spectrum with BRIGHT, such as 
the Tone Controls, EQ, TRIODE/PENTODE, the speaker cab 
& finally, a big component often overlooked, their guitar & its 
pickups. Even their own individual picking/fingering technique 
& the gauge of strings they use, all of these factors affect the 
type & amount of high harmonics that can be introduced & 
still sound balanced, an integrated tone, rather than shrill or 
annoying.
	
gPickups are a huge factor in the overall tonal character. Many 
players stick with 1 instrument & if they have trouble dialing 
in a sound on their amp, they overlook the fact that the 
sound begins & is largely defined by, what’s sent to the amp 
from their instrument. High gain sounds are especially sensitive 
to output/voicing of pickups & even more so, to the amount of 
high end that’s emphasized. Weaker, more vintage style pickups, 
accentuate upper harmonics & are wonderful for shimmering, bell-
like harmonics & clarity in low to medium gain applications. When 
you’re looking for higher gain performance, these types of pickups 
present some challenges, as those upper harmonics can get buzzy 
& thin sounding as gain increases. You will likely find it necessary 
to roll TREBLE & PRESENCE back substantially when searching 
for a high gain sound that’s focused & pure with these pickups.
	
gIf high gain is your bag, you’ve probably already come to the 
conclusion that you need a fairly high output pickup, at least in 
the bridge position, to achieve the tight focus needed to produce 
cohesive high gain sounds. If not, may we suggest you try several 
different higher output pickups, which tend to put emphasis on 
the mids & higher midrange, as opposed to top end. This voicing 
difference will produce tighter low end for crunch chords & a more 
voice-like single note sound.
	
gSome higher output pickups will have taps, or can be tapped, at 
a halfway point in the winding, giving the best of both worlds, 
a weaker output/brighter tap & the full winding, high output/
punchier mid setting. This is usually controlled via a toggle or 
pull pot, but can also be hard-wired to a specific position on a 
standard 5-way or toggle. This is a great way to have the most 
versatility possible & keep high harmonic content & openness for 
your low to mid gain applications & then be able to switch your 
guitar, along with the Channels of the MkV, to a high output/high 
gain machine, capable of tight, ferocious heavy sounds.
	
gEXTREME: The last Channel 3 mode is named appropriately & 
is all about more! More gain, more attack, more punishing mids, 
more tight, huge lows & more output volume. EXTREME traces its 
lineage back to a feature on the MkIV that resided on the Channel 
3 (LEAD) PRESENCE control of that amp. A pull-pot fitted there 
allowed removal of negative feedback from the power section & 
unleashed the pent-up fury of the Simul-Class output.
	
gThis feature allows the power section Presence circuit to respond 
to all freqs more equally, instead of a chosen adjustable set of high 
freqs & removes an element of “control” & inherent compression 
in the power amp. This wider EQ curve in the rear end creates a 
rebellious, unleashed & extremely open personality that can’t be 
duplicated with front end preamp circuitry. When this radically 
aggressive power personality is combined with the high gain wall 
of layered harmonics in the MkIV mode, a sound of truly stunning 
proportions is created.
	
gTop end has been warmed, low end has been tightened & feel 
improved, making EXTREME in the V easier to play & even more 
expressive vs its’ predecessor. The attack, body, girth & versatility 
of the V’s EXTREME Mode will allow you to wield this power in 
more places for more styles, even venturing into some lower gain 
uses the IV dare not go.
	
gMany Hard Core devotees love the way the EXTREME Mode can 
handle very high settings of the GAIN control & still retain attack 
& tightness of high gain chording. It’s not even necessary to run 
TREBLE high to achieve this tightness & settings in 12:00-1:00 
range are common. Remember that for very high gain chording, 
it’s better to set BASS lower (10:00-11:00) & look for additional low 
end via the EQ, as bottom end introduced late in the signal chain 
stays tighter longer that its’ early preamp counterpart.
	
gAs mentioned, some urgent, packing-an-attitude lower gain 
sounds are possible when EXTREME’s used with lower GAIN 
settings. For these types of sounds you can increase BASS (11:30-
1:30) to add 3D warmth, since there’s less gain dialed & less 
chance of causing unwanted flub
